change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.acoustic_privacy.definition: Acoustic privacy: the appraised likelihood of not being overheard in a depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + soft-material/absorbent-surface segmentation + partition/enclosure detection + estimated distance-to-nearest-other-occupant + room-volume estimate

key_refs: Sundstrom, E., Burt, R. E., & Kamp, D. (1980). Privacy at work: Architectural correlates of job satisfaction and job performance. Academy of Management Journal, 23(1), 101-117. https://doi.org/10.2307/255498

Mehta, R., Zhu, R. J., & Cheema, A. (2010). Is noise always bad? Exploring the effects of ambient noise on creative cognition. Journal of Consumer Research, 39(4), 784-799. https://doi.org/10.1086/665048
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.

notes.bn_notes: Inverse-of relationship with L15 Social Exposure is observable (visual-exposure) versus auditory-exposure; the two latents share some upstream observables but the acoustic-vs-visual axis must remain doubly-dissociated in the BN parent set.
notes.extraction_notes: Inherits V2.6 Tier 2 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 4). Acoustic privacy is partially inferable from 2D imagery via material and enclosure proxies; 3D / VR substrates with audio rendering provide direct acoustic evidence (Sundstrom et al., 1980) and are correspondingly more reliable.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised degree to which a depicted space affords speech and conversational privacy - that is, the likelihood that ordinary in-room speech will *not* be overheard by uninvited others (Sundstrom, Burt, & Kamp, 1980). Operationalised through cues that predict low speech-transmission to neighbouring occupants: soft, sound-absorbing surfaces (carpet, drapes, upholstered furniture); explicit acoustic treatment (panels, baffles, ceiling tiles); spatial enclosure (full or partial walls, high partitions, closed doors); and absolute distance from nearby occupants. The construct is image-based and therefore an *affordance* of the depicted space rather than a measured speech-transmission-class value; ground-truth acoustic measurement requires audio rendering and is achievable only in the 3D / VR substrate (Mehta, Zhu, & Cheema, 2010, on the moderating role of ambient sound on focused cognition).
semantics.definition_short: Not being overheard.
examples_negative: A bench in an open-plan office with no partitions, hard floor, and adjacent workstations within 1.5 m - none of the soft-material or enclosure cues are present; conversation would be audible to several neighbours.
A hard-surfaced atrium with marble floor, glazed walls, and a central seating cluster - high reverberation and no enclosure; speech privacy is near-zero regardless of subjective coziness.
A coffee-shop high-top counter facing the street, hard surfaces throughout and other patrons within 1 m on either side - the space is intimate but acoustically transparent.

A boardroom with full enclosure but a glass curtain-wall separating it from a hallway and no visible soft furnishings; visual privacy is high but acoustic privacy is moderate at best because hard glass surfaces increase intra-room reverberation and inter-room speech transfer through the door.

A library reading room with high ceilings, hard-tile floor, and groups at adjacent tables - the visual coding reads as a quiet space, but the absence of absorbent surfaces means the *acoustic* privacy is much lower than the *behavioural* privacy norm.
examples_positive: A small private office with closed door, carpeted floor, drapes on the window, and an upholstered chair - all visible soft-absorbent surfaces; door closure and full-height walls suggest speech will not carry.
A library study room enclosed on three sides by floor-to-ceiling acoustic panels, the fourth side a glazed door; the panels and the visual depth of the enclosure cue strong speech containment.
A residential study with bookshelves wall-to-ceiling on two walls, a thick rug, and a closed door; bookcases function as broadband absorbers and the room reads as private for conversation.

A two-person conference nook in a hotel lobby separated from circulation by a high upholstered banquette and a tall planted screen; the visible barriers cue acoustic separation despite no full enclosure.
A telephone booth-style work pod with felted interior walls, a glazed but gasketed door, and a single occupant chair; image cues converge on conversational confidentiality.
A therapist's office with carpet, heavy curtains, upholstered furniture, and a closed door visible at the edge of the frame; ensemble of soft surfaces plus closure dominates the read.
A residential bedroom retrofitted as a remote-work space, the door closed and acoustic foam visible behind the desk; foam plus closure cues high non-overhearability.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor consumes upstream observables: (a) `materials.soft_absorbent_surface_present` and the segmented soft-area fraction (carpet, drapes, upholstery), (b) `materials.acoustic_panel_present` and panel coverage, (c) `geometry.enclosure_degree` derived from visible wall/partition area and `geometry.partition_height`, (d) `geometry.distance_to_nearest_workstation` estimated by monocular depth, and (e) `scene.room_volume_estimate` from monocular geometry. The Likert is composed by a weighted sum of these with weights calibrated to the Sundstrom et al. (1980) speech-privacy literature. Per the V2.6 cue list 'Soft materials; acoustic panels; distance; enclosures', each cue family contributes independently; an absent cue family does not zero the score (a marble-walled but enclosed booth still scores moderate). Confidence is reduced when soft-material visibility is occluded or when room volume cannot be estimated from the image.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate with audio rendering (per the V2.6 'VR + audio rendering' enrichment column) the extractor can compute a metric speech-privacy score directly from impulse-response simulation: place a speech-source at a designated occupant location and a receiver at a designated overhearer location, render the impulse response, and compute Speech Transmission Index (STI) or Articulation Index. The Likert 0-4 then derives from binning the STI (e.g., STI<0.2 -> 4, 0.2-0.35 -> 3, etc.). When audio rendering is unavailable, the 3D fallback uses metric absorptive-area fraction, metric enclosure ratio, and metric inter-occupant distance, all of which are direct measurements rather than monocular estimates. Confidence is materially higher in 3D than 2D for this construct.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-PRIV-1 (Acoustic Privacy): rating items 'how likely overheard (reverse)', 'comfortable having a private conversation', 'distracting other conversations (reverse)'; behavioural proxy '2AFC choice for focused work' and 'NASA-TLX after standardised task'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *privacy/attention* superfactor alongside L12 (Interruption Likelihood) and L13 (Territorial Support).
scope_excludes: Outdoor spaces and rooms whose acoustic privacy is dominated by ambient masking noise rather than by structural cues - the construct here is the *structural* affordance, not the measured privacy index.
Spaces whose privacy is purely visual (e.g., glazed pods with no acoustic seal) - those should be coded for L15 (Social Exposure inverse) and not for L11.

Single-person spaces with no other occupants in the depicted scene - L11 is meaningfully defined only when at least one potential overhearer is plausible, and a totally empty private room should be coded as a ceiling-effect maximum.
scope_includes: Spaces where soft-absorbent surfaces (carpet, drapes, upholstery, acoustic panels, books, vegetation) are visibly dominant and predict low reverberation and low speech transmission.
Spaces with full or partial enclosure (walls, partitions, doors, banquettes, screens) that visibly block direct sound paths to other occupants.
Spaces where absolute distance to the nearest other occupant exceeds the rough threshold (~3 m for normal speech, ~1.5 m for hushed speech) such that overhearing is unlikely from geometry alone.

cognitive.arousal_potential.definition: Activation level the scene is likely to elicit — energy, alertness, stimulation.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for high-contrast luminance, saturated colour, edge density, motion-implication, and busyness, read by a VLM conditioned on the SAM-arousal item and Berlyne collative variables

key_refs: Berlyne, D. E. (1971). Aesthetics and psychobiology. Appleton-Century-Crofts.
Russell, J. A., & Mehrabian, A. (1977). Evidence for a three-factor theory of emotions. Journal of Research in Personality, 11(3), 273-294. https://doi.org/10.1016/0092-6566(77)90037-X

Bradley, M. M., & Lang, P. J. (1994). Measuring emotion: The Self-Assessment Manikin and the Semantic Differential. Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry, 25(1), 49-59. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7916(94)90063-9

notes.extraction_notes: Source/response distinction: novelty (L38) and complexity (separate latent) are *sources* of arousal per Berlyne (1971); L23 is the *response* prediction. Sprint-3 BN integration must avoid double-counting by routing source variables as parents of L23 rather than as independent arousal predictors.

semantics.definition_long: Arousal Potential indexes the degree of physiological/psychological activation a scene is likely to elicit in a typical observer — the 'arousal' dimension of Russell and Mehrabian's (1977) PAD model, distinct from valence (L24) and dominance. Berlyne (1971) frames the upstream determinants as collative variables: complexity, novelty, intensity, conflict, and incongruity together drive the arousal response. Arousal in this construct is *predicted from* the scene's stimulus properties — high luminance contrast, saturated colour, high edge density, dense object population, implied motion — rather than measured from the observer's body. Because arousal is orthogonal to valence in the circumplex (Russell, 1980), high arousal can be experienced as exciting or as overstimulating depending on context; the panel therefore assigns `valence_polarity: mixed` and warns that downstream BN integration should not collapse arousal into preference. The construct sits in the mixed-interaction register: high arousal can support focused vigilance, broad-band scanning, or social excitement.
semantics.definition_short: Activation level the scene is likely to elicit — energy, alertness, stimulation.
examples_negative: A pre-dawn Quaker meeting room with bare walls, low-contrast grey light, evenly spaced wooden benches, and no implied motion.
A library reading room at midday: well-lit but with restrained palette, low edge density, and no implied motion.
A monochrome modernist gallery with one painting on a long wall — low arousal even though the painting itself may be intense.
A foggy mountain pass photographed in soft diffused light with low colour saturation and no visible figures.
examples_positive: A neon-lit Tokyo arcade at night with saturated reds, blues, and yellows, dense signage, animated screens, and a high density of moving figures.
A children's birthday party photographed mid-celebration: streamers, balloons, primary-colour cake, motion blur on running children, high edge density.
A football-stadium concourse at half-time: dense crowds, sharp shadows, vivid team-colour merchandise, and the visible suggestion of motion in the crowd.
A neon-and-mirror nightclub interior with high-contrast lighting and rapidly varying patterns.
A chaotic open-air spice market with saturated reds, yellows, and oranges piled in conical heaps, and an implied bustle of buyers and sellers.
A rock-concert audience photographed from the stage, with stage-light flares, raised arms, and high dynamic range.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is the canonical substrate. The extractor reads upstream observables of luminance contrast (variance and high-percentile of luminance distribution), colour saturation (mean and high-percentile saturation in HSV), edge density (per-pixel edge response from a Sobel/Canny-like filter), busyness (count of distinct objects per unit area and inhomogeneity of texture), and implied-motion cues (motion-blur signatures, postures suggesting movement, vehicles in transit). A VLM conditioned on the SAM-arousal probe (Bradley & Lang, 1994) and the Berlyne collative-variable framing returns a 5-point Likert. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: arousal-source mappings (which colours read as 'energetic', what crowd densities read as 'stimulating') vary moderately across cultures but the underlying construct is well-supported as a near-universal affective dimension.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scene supplies richer arousal-relevant cues than a still image: motion is no longer merely 'implied' but can be observed across frames; lighting variation across vantages registers in luminance contrast; spatial proximity to high-contrast or saturated elements can be computed. The extractor MUST report a per-vantage arousal estimate as well as a scan-aggregate, since arousal is highly position-dependent (a corner of a nightclub away from the dance floor can be calm). The construct itself is snapshot-temporal; for sustained-exposure arousal predictions the BN should chain L23 to a downstream temporal-arousal node not in this layer.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L23 indicator block — three rating items (energising; overstimulating; expected alertness) anchored on SAM-arousal (Bradley & Lang, 1994) and two behavioural proxies (post-exposure PVT RT; choice under fatigue).
scope_excludes: Valence (L24): valence is the pleasant/unpleasant dimension and is orthogonal to arousal in the Russell (1980) circumplex.
Novelty (L38): novelty is one *source* of arousal (per Berlyne, 1971); L23 is the *response* to that and other collative sources combined. Authors must keep the source/response distinction visible.

Personal arousal as measured from the body (heart rate, GSR, pupillometry): the construct is the predicted activation, not the measured one. Observed physiology can be a downstream child variable in the BN.
scope_includes: The image-derivable prediction of activation a scene is likely to evoke in a typical observer.
Cases where arousal is high-positive (exciting) and high-negative (overstimulating) — the construct is amplitude-only and does not encode sign.
Both interior and exterior scenes; both populated and unpopulated scenes provided the scene's stimulus properties are sufficient to ground a prediction.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.awe.definition: Vastness and need for cognitive accommodation — the architectural cathedral effect.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + ceiling-height proxy + vertical-composition analysis + light-direction estimation + proportional-canon scoring

key_refs: Vartanian, O., Navarrete, G., Chatterjee, A., Fich, L. B., Leder, H., Modrono, C., Nadal, M., Rostrup, N., & Skov, M. (2015). Architectural design and the brain: Effects of ceiling height and perceived enclosure on beauty judgments and approach-avoidance decisions. *Journal of Environmental Psychology*, *41*, 10-18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvp.2014.11.006
Keltner, D., & Haidt, J. (2003). Approaching awe, a moral, spiritual, and aesthetic emotion. *Cognition and Emotion*, *17*(2), 297-314. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699930302297

Bermudez, J., Krizaj, D., Lipschitz, D. L., Bueler, C. E., Rogowska, J., Yurgelun-Todd, D., & Nakamura, Y. (2017). Externally-induced meditative states: An exploratory fMRI study of architects' responses to contemplative architecture. *Frontiers of Architectural Research*, *6*(2), 123-136. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foar.2017.02.002
Joye, Y. (2007). Architectural lessons from environmental psychology: The case of biophilic architecture. *Review of General Psychology*, *11*(4), 305-328. https://doi.org/10.1037/1089-2680.11.4.305

notes.bn_notes: Sprint-3 candidate for ranking-theoretic recoding (Spohn 2012, Sec.5.3) given the binary-leaning structure of awe response — see Keltner & Haidt 2003. The construct is a regime-switch: awe is engaged or not, and intermediate Likert positions (1, 2, 3) are less interpersonally stable than the endpoints. Sprint-3 should evaluate whether L36 should be migrated from ordinal Likert to a ranking-theoretic encoding alongside the Sprint 1 binary-candidate latents (L46, L52, L53). The parent set is dominated by vertical-composition observables (`geometry.ceiling_height_proxy`, `geometry.vertical_aspect_ratio`, `geometry.proportional_canon_score`) which are largely disjoint from the prestige (L32) parent set; the Spohn 50%-overlap audit should pass. | Sprint 3 contest #4 disposition: cross_cultural_variance demoted from 'high' to 'medium'. Keltner & Haidt (2003) two-core-appraisal pattern (vastness + need for accommodation) replicates across cultures; Yaden et al. (2019) 23-country evidence supports universality of awe perception. Cultural variance is in *meaning* and *downstream behavior*, not the image-extractable appraisal. L25 and L40 retain 'high' (per contest #4 per-latent vote).
notes.extraction_notes: Awe is the Sprint 2 cognitive-affect latent for which the panel flagged the strongest binary-leaning structure (a perceiver typically reports either an awe response or none, with intermediate gradations less stable). The Likert encoding is preserved for Sprint 2 to maintain comparability with the rest of the cognitive-affect set, but a Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic recoding should be considered (see bn_notes).

semantics.definition_long: Awe/Grandeur is the perceiver's awe response to architectural vastness — the dual condition of perceived vastness and a corresponding need for cognitive accommodation that, in Keltner and Haidt's (2003) prototypical specification, defines awe as an emotion. The architectural correlate has been documented experimentally by Vartanian and colleagues (2015), whose work on the cathedral effect shows that high ceilings and dramatic verticality reliably elicit measurable awe responses, and by Bermudez and colleagues (2017) on contemplative architecture, which extends the result to spaces designed for sustained meditative engagement. Joye (2007) provides the biophilic extension, arguing that natural-form awe (vast canyons, tree canopies, stone formations) shares perceptual primitives with architectural awe (high vaults, dramatic verticality, vistas). Awe is positive in valence in the sense that the experience is sought and reported as meaningful, but it is *self-diminishing*: the perceiver feels small in a way they welcome rather than a way they avoid. This distinguishes awe sharply from prestige (L32): prestige is status-asserting (the space marks its occupants as high-status), whereas awe is self-diminishing (the space humbles its occupants regardless of their status). Cross-cultural variance is high (Keltner & Haidt 2003 cross-cultural review): the perceptual primitives of vastness and verticality recur across cultures, but the cultural codings of what counts as awe-worthy and what affective register awe occupies (sacred, sublime, contemplative) vary substantially. Awe-spaces frequently exhibit strong shared-attention anchors (a vault keystone, an altar, a vista terminus), and L36 therefore co-occurs frequently with the Sprint 1 social latent `social.shared_attention_anchor` (L53).
semantics.definition_short: Vastness and need for cognitive accommodation — the architectural cathedral effect.
examples_negative: A high-end hotel lobby with prestige markers (marble, brass, fresh flowers) but standard ceiling height and spatial proportions — high on L32 (prestige), low on L36 (awe).

A vast warehouse interior with high ceilings but no proportional canon, no compositional verticality, and no terminus to the gaze — vastness is present but the cognitive-accommodation response does not engage.
A large open-plan office with high but unarticulated ceilings — the vertical dimension is not perceptually salient.
A small but intricate chapel with rich detailing but a low ceiling — high on care or placeness, low on awe.
examples_positive: A Gothic cathedral nave with high vaulted ceilings, light entering through clerestory windows above eye level, and the human-scale altar reading as small in the vertical composition.
A grand library reading room with a domed or coffered ceiling well above eye level and with sustained vertical sightlines unbroken by intervening floors.
A natural-history museum great hall with a suspended whale skeleton and a six-storey atrium ceiling.

A modernist concrete chapel (e.g., a Tadao Ando design) where the vastness is felt through the proportional canon of the volume rather than through ornament — the cathedral effect achieved with sparse means.
A large train station hall with a barrel-vault roof and tall arched windows, where the human-scale concourse reads as small relative to the volume above.
A canyon-overlook viewing room or observation deck whose vista frames a scale that overwhelms human reference.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image supports awe extraction when the vertical composition is in frame. The extractor should consume upstream observables `geometry.ceiling_height_proxy` (a monocular ceiling-height estimate from vertical-vanishing-point analysis), `geometry.vertical_aspect_ratio` (the ratio of perceived vertical extent to horizontal in the principal compositional axis), `geometry.proportional_canon_score` (a measure of whether the volume exhibits classical proportional relationships consistent with awe-architecture), `light.above_eye_level_illumination` (a measure of whether the principal light source enters above eye level — clerestory cue), and `compositional.vista_terminus_present`. The Likert mapping is monotone in the joint presence of vastness, articulation, and overhead-light cues. Region support is false: awe is an integrated room-level score. Monocular-3D approximation is full because the perceptual primitives of awe (verticality, proportion, light direction) are robust to depth noise.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans, awe extraction benefits from accurate ceiling-height measurement, from full proportional analysis of the volume, and from light-direction estimation from photon paths or normal-map analysis. The 3D extractor should compute (i) volumetric vertical aspect ratio, (ii) proportional-canon scoring against a library of awe-architecture exemplars, (iii) sectional articulation measures, (iv) overhead-light entry from light-source geometry, and (v) vista-terminus distance and scale. VR enriches awe substantially (per V2.6) because the embodied experience of looking up engages the cognitive-accommodation system more strongly than a flat photograph.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Awe/Grandeur': rating items for (a) awe-inspiring quality, (b) felt vastness, (c) self-diminishing positive response. Behavioural proxies (attention bias upward; dwell-time on vertical features) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Prestige (L32) — prestige is status-asserting; awe is self-diminishing. The two co-occur in some monumental spaces (a grand cathedral may be both) but the constructs are distinct: a marble lobby with fresh flowers and ordinary proportions is L32-high and L36-low. (Per the panel: this exclusion is constitutive — prestige is status; awe is self-diminishment.)

Vastness without articulation — a warehouse, a gymnasium, an unfinished volume can be physically vast but elicit no awe response because the proportional canon, sectional composition, or anchoring features required to engage the cognitive-accommodation system are absent.
Novelty (L38) — a novel design can be striking without being awesome; a familiar cathedral remains awesome. Novelty and awe are independent.
scope_includes: Architectural vastness with proportional canon — high ceilings, dramatic verticality, articulated sectional composition.
Vista-terminating views where the gaze is led toward a scale beyond human reference.
Contemplative-architecture markers — sustained vertical sightlines, light entering above eye level, attentional anchors at a self-diminishing scale.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.being_away.definition: Being-Away: the appraised psychological distance the depicted space affords from everyday demands per Kaplan & Kaplan's ART.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + VLM prompt encoding being-away ART component + threshold/separation detection + natural-view detection + demand-artefact absence check

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, S. (1995). The restorative benefits of nature: Toward an integrative framework. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 15(3), 169-182. https://doi.org/10.1016/0272-4944(95)90001-2

Hartig, T., Korpela, K., Evans, G. W., & Garling, T. (1997). A measure of restorative quality in environments. Scandinavian Housing and Planning Research, 14(4), 175-194. https://doi.org/10.1080/02815739708730435

notes.bn_notes: Component of L17 (cognitive.restorativeness). Per Sprint 3 contest #1 structured-factor reorganisation; the parent-child relationship is a BN-structural commitment, not an extraction-time merger — independent extractor stubs retained.
notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 1 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 5). L18 is the *being-away* sub-component of Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) ART, distinct from L19 (Soft Fascination) and L20 (Compatibility). L18 specifically tracks the affordance for psychological retreat from demands - through visible thresholds, separation from workspace, natural views, and the absence of demand-cueing artefacts (work tools, deadlines, screens).

semantics.definition_long: The appraised psychological distance the depicted space affords from everyday demands - the 'I can be elsewhere here' affordance that is one of the four sub-components of Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) Attention Restoration Theory. Operationalised through cues that predict demand-decoupling: visible thresholds (doorways, gates, transitions) that mark the depicted space as distinct from a demand-laden setting; separation in space and view from workplace, household, or screen contexts; natural views (windows onto greenery, water, sky) that signal a different domain than the workplace; and the absence of demand-cueing artefacts (computers, paperwork, deadlines, unread mail). The construct is grounded in the Kaplan and Kaplan (1989) ART framework and is the canonical 'being away' subscale of the Hartig et al. (1997) PRS. L18 is *one of four* ART components and is to be distinguished from the integrated L17 (Restorativeness) and from the sibling components L19 (Soft Fascination) and L20 (Compatibility).
semantics.definition_short: Psychological distance from demands.
examples_negative: An office desk with a computer monitor, paperwork, and a phone - demand artefacts dominate the scene; being-away affordance is at floor.
A kitchen table mid-meal-prep with cooking implements and a half-finished list - functionally a demand setting; no being-away cue.
A living-room sofa in a room with a TV showing the news, a laptop on a side table, and visible household-task cues; demand artefacts dominate even in a putatively-restful setting.
A coffee shop seat directly facing an open laptop and a stack of papers - the depicted scene's primary affordance is work, not retreat.
examples_positive: A garden bench reached through a stone gateway from a hedge-bordered house yard - the gateway is a literal threshold and the garden-side of it is visually decoupled from the household's demand context.
A residential window seat with a wide view onto a forest, no work artefacts visible inside the seat enclosure - both natural-view and demand-absence cues are strong.
A hotel reading lounge separated by a corridor and a glazed door from the elevator lobby and the conference area; the threshold and demand-absence cues are explicit.
A park bench at the end of a walking path, no buildings or vehicles visible - the depicted enclosure is fully decoupled from any demand setting.
A residential bedroom converted to a meditation space with a closed door, a low cushion, and natural light from a small window; the door-threshold and demand-artefact-absence cues converge.
A treetop deck reached by a staircase from the main house - the stair is a visible threshold and the deck is visually distinct from the household context.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor uses a VLM prompted with the being-away ART component, plus structured upstream observables: (a) `scene.threshold_present` (door, gate, archway, stair detection), (b) `geometry.separation_from_workspace` (heuristic estimate of distance from a typical demand setting, e.g., presence/absence of office furniture in frame), (c) `scene.natural_view_present` (window onto greenery or sky detection), and (d) `scene.demand_artefact_absent` (boolean: no computer, paperwork, phone visible). The Likert is composed by the VLM against the Hartig et al. (1997) PRS being-away subscale. Per the Spohn 50%-overlap audit mandate, L18 is intentionally distinct from L19 (Soft Fascination) which uses *attentional-capture* features; L18 uses *demand-decoupling* features. The V2.6 cue list 'Thresholds; separation; natural views' maps onto these observables. Confidence is high (Image_Judgability = 5).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR transitions and distance cues' enrichment) the extractor can model the threshold transition explicitly - rendering the avatar's passage from a demand-laden space through a doorway into the depicted space - and verify that the depicted space supports being-away under that transition. Distance from workplace and natural-view orientation are direct metric measurements. Threshold detection is materially more reliable in 3D because viewpoint can disambiguate occluded transitions. As with L17, 3D substrate provides materially higher confidence than 2D.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-REST-2 (Being-Away): rating items 'separate from demands', 'mentally switch off', 'psychologically distant from work'; behavioural proxies 'rumination change pre/post' and 'task-switch cost change'.
Loads as a sub-factor of the broader V2.6 *restoration* superfactor and is one of four ART components per Kaplan and Kaplan (1989).
scope_excludes: Soft fascination (attentional capture by gentle stimuli) - that is L19 (Soft Fascination), a different ART component; L18 is specifically the demand-decoupling axis.
Compatibility (fit between space and activity) - that is L20 (Compatibility); L18 may be high while L20 is low (e.g., a beautiful but inappropriate space for the intended activity).
Integrated restorativeness - that is L17 (Restorativeness); L18 is one of four sub-components and is intentionally narrower.
scope_includes: Spaces with visible thresholds (doors, gates, transitions) marking the depicted location as distinct from a demand-laden setting.
Spaces with views onto natural domains (greenery, water, sky) that signal a domain change from the workplace or household.
Spaces showing the *absence* of demand-cueing artefacts - no screens, no paperwork, no unread mail visible in frame.

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.care_signal.definition: Binary inference that the place is actively maintained and someone is in charge of it.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + disorder-cue detection + fixture intactness + cleaning state estimation

key_refs: Wilson, J. Q., & Kelling, G. L. (1982, March). Broken windows: The police and neighborhood safety. *The Atlantic Monthly*, 29-38.

Sampson, R. J., & Raudenbush, S. W. (1999). Systematic social observation of public spaces: A new look at disorder in urban neighborhoods. *American Journal of Sociology*, *105*(3), 603-651. https://doi.org/10.1086/210356

Sampson, R. J., Raudenbush, S. W., & Earls, F. (1997). Neighborhoods and violent crime: A multilevel study of collective efficacy. *Science*, *277*(5328), 918-924. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.277.5328.918

notes.bn_notes: Care (L33) shares some surface-quality parents with prestige (L32). The Spohn 50%-overlap audit must verify disjointness: L33's parent set is dominated by disorder cues (litter, graffiti, broken-fixture, dust) which L32 does not consume; L32's parent set is dominated by luxury-material cues which L33 does not consume. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: The binary (latent_score) value type follows the Sprint 2 panel decision that care/maintenance is a regime-switch construct rather than a smooth ordinal. Borderline cases default to negative because the construct's downstream consumers (trust, safety, approach behaviour) penalise false positives more strongly than false negatives. Method family is `vlm` because the integration of many small disorder/order cues is what a perceiver does and what a VLM trained on indoor-scene maintenance can replicate.

semantics.definition_long: Care/Maintenance Signal is the perceiver's binary inference that the space is actively stewarded — that cleaning, repair, and ordering happen on a regular cadence and that some human or institutional agent treats the space as their responsibility. The construct is the inverse of the broken-windows construct of Wilson and Kelling (1982), and its operationalisation follows Sampson and Raudenbush's (1999) systematic-social-observation work on physical disorder in Chicago neighbourhoods, which established that perceivers reliably read maintenance state from a small set of cues (litter, graffiti, broken fixtures, weed growth, dilapidation) and that these readings strongly predict trust, perceived safety, and downstream civic behaviour. The construct is encoded as binary (latent_score) per the Sprint 2 panel binary-candidate decision: a space either signals 'someone is in charge' or it does not, and intermediate gradations are unstable across perceivers. The positive case requires the absence of disorder cues and the presence of order cues (visible cleaning state, intact fixtures, ordered storage, recent surface maintenance). Care signal is asymmetric in valence: well-maintained is positive (predicting trust, approach, prosocial behaviour); poorly-maintained is sharply negative.
semantics.definition_short: Binary inference that the place is actively maintained and someone is in charge of it.
examples_negative: A public restroom with broken stall doors, graffiti on every surface, exposed wiring from a missing fixture, and litter on the floor — care signal is clearly negative.
A storefront with a cracked plate-glass window taped over, a faded handwritten sign, and accumulated dust in the entrance — disorder cues dominate.
A corridor with a missing ceiling tile exposing wiring, a flickering fluorescent fixture, and scuff marks on the walls without recent cleaning.
A park with overflowing bins, broken bench slats, and overgrown weeds in the planted beds — disorder cues converge.
examples_positive: A public library reading room with no visible litter, no graffiti, intact light fixtures, books shelved in order, and freshly-vacuumed carpet.
A small neighbourhood park with mown grass, emptied bins, painted benches, and no graffiti on the play equipment.
A modest diner whose floor, counter, and tables are visibly clean, whose menus are intact, and whose lights are all working — care is unambiguous even though luxury markers are absent.
A stairwell in an apartment building with no graffiti, working overhead lighting, intact handrails, and recently-cleaned floors.
A school corridor with student work mounted neatly on the walls, intact lockers, and a clean floor.
A workshop or maker-space with tools visibly stowed in their places, swept floor, and no abandoned debris.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is the canonical substrate for care extraction because the disorder/order cue inventory (litter, graffiti, broken fixtures, dust, weed growth, dilapidation, cleaning state) is directly perceptually visible. The extractor should consume upstream observables `disorder.litter_pixels` (proportion of floor and surface area covered by litter), `disorder.graffiti_presence` (binary), `disorder.broken_fixture_count` (number of detectably broken or missing fixtures — light bulbs out, missing ceiling tiles, broken handrails), `disorder.dust_or_grime_score` (a surface-cleanliness estimate from low-frequency luminance variation on floors and counters), `order.stored_items_alignment_score` (a measure of whether shelved/stored items are aligned versus chaotic), and `material.fixture_intactness_score`. The binary judgement is positive when the disorder set is empty and at least one positive order cue is detected; the threshold is asymmetric and conservative — borderline cases default to negative because the construct's downstream consumers (trust, safety, approach) penalise false positives more than false negatives.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans, care extraction benefits from canonical-distance inspection of fixtures, from view-independent litter detection, and from the ability to verify fixture intactness from multiple angles (a cracked or bent fixture invisible from one angle is recoverable from another). The 3D extractor should compute (i) per-surface cleanliness via local micro-geometry and reflectance, (ii) per-fixture intactness from full-mesh inspection, (iii) per-region litter detection from object segmentation, and (iv) order scores from stored-item alignment. Region support is false: care is an integrated room-level binary. Monocular-3D approximation is partial because some disorder cues (dust grime under glancing illumination) require accurate depth-and-light estimation.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Care/Maintenance Signal': rating items for (a) extent of perceived maintenance, (b) extent to which the space signals someone is in charge, (c) trust attributable to upkeep. Behavioural proxies (trust choice for leaving belongings, avoidance for eating/close contact) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Aesthetic value alone — a beautifully-designed but uncared-for space (e.g., a once-grand lobby now neglected) is L33-negative regardless of its design quality.
Luxury (L32) — care and prestige are independent; a clean fast-food outlet is L33-positive and L32-low.
Welcome (L34) — a meticulously maintained but exclusionary space is L33-positive and L34-low.
scope_includes: Cleaning state — visible recent cleaning of floors, surfaces, and fixtures.
Repair state — intact fixtures, undamaged furniture, no exposed structural elements that should be concealed.
Order state — items stored in their canonical places, signage and notices not torn or peeling, plant material maintained.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.choice_richness.definition: Availability of distinct zones / behavioural-setting options in the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + zone segmentation + seating-type clustering + lighting-zone analysis + niche / activity-setting enumeration

key_refs: Barker, R. G. (1968). Ecological psychology: Concepts and methods for studying the environment of human behavior. Stanford University Press.
Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (1985). Intrinsic motivation and self-determination in human behavior. Plenum.
Iyengar, S. S., & Lepper, M. R. (2000). When choice is demotivating: Can one desire too much of a good thing? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(6), 995–1006.

notes.bn_notes: The Iyengar and Lepper (2000) choice-overload finding implies that the relationship between L07 and downstream satisfaction is non-monotonic: too few zones constrains, too many overwhelms. This is logged as a Sprint-3 panel item for whether choice richness should be encoded as a non-monotonic latent or whether the inverted-U is captured downstream.
notes.extraction_notes: Composite extractor combines zone-count enumeration with a VLM scoring the V2.7 generic rating items.

semantics.definition_long: The availability of distinct zones, niches, or behavioural-setting options within the depicted space, conceived within Barker's (1968) ecological-psychology framework of behavioural settings and within Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) concept of *compatibility* (the match between an environment and a person's intended activities). Choice richness is high when the space affords multiple distinguishable activity niches — a quiet reading area, a collaborative table, a window seat, a standing-desk station — each visually distinguishable from the others. The construct is the competence-supporting complement of L06's autonomy (Deci & Ryan, 1985): autonomy concerns control over conditions; choice richness concerns the breadth of the option set. Iyengar and Lepper's (2000) work on choice overload qualifies the construct: beyond a certain richness, additional options reduce rather than increase satisfaction, suggesting an inverted-U relationship to downstream affect.
semantics.definition_short: Choice Richness — availability of distinct zones/options.
examples_negative: A large open space with uniform seating throughout — spatially generous but no zone differentiation; choice richness is low despite the room being large.
A space with two zones that are visually distinct but functionally identical — e.g., two identical conference tables on either side of a partition.
A space crowded with twenty different seating types in a single room — nominal richness but the choice may overwhelm rather than support (Iyengar-Lepper choice overload).
A long corridor with multiple alcoves all sized for the same activity — multiple slots but a single behavioural setting.
examples_positive: To what extent does this space feel like: Choice Richness?
How strongly would this appraisal influence your choice to be here?
How confident are you in this judgment from the image alone?
Choice frequency in repeated 2AFC trials against matched alternatives.
Response-time consistency across repetitions (reliability).
A modern library reading floor with a quiet study area, a group-work zone with whiteboards, a standing-table area, lounge sofas, and individual carrels all visible from a single viewpoint.
A contemporary cafe with a long counter for solo work, booth seating for pairs, communal long tables, a sofa cluster, and outdoor patio seating.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `affordance.zone_count` (count of perceptibly distinct activity zones), `furniture.seating_type_variety` (variety of seating typologies present), `lighting.zone_count` (count of perceptibly distinct lighting conditions), `scene.niche_diversity` (count of architectural niches affording different activities), and `scene.activity_setting_count` (Barker-style behavioural-setting enumeration). The V2.7 cue list "multiple seating/lighting zones; variety of niches" maps directly onto these. The 2D extractor returns `partial` because not all zones may be visible from a single viewpoint; a composite extractor combines the count cues with a VLM scoring the V2.7 rating items, with the composite typically being more reliable than either alone.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can enumerate zones explicitly via per-zone segmentation of the floor plane, with each zone typed by its furniture and lighting cluster. Per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR navigation to zones" — the behavioural proxy of how many zones a user actually visits in unconstrained navigation provides ground-truth calibration for the perceptual appraisal latent. The 3D substrate also allows the extractor to apply Barker's (1968) full behavioural-setting taxonomy (programme, occupants, time, place) to each candidate zone and report a richness count weighted by setting distinctness rather than mere geometric separation.
scope_excludes: Agency over conditions — that's L06 (cognitive.perceived_control); choice richness concerns options *available*, not control over them.
Visual complexity / clutter — a perceptual construct distinct from option-richness.
Sheer spatial size — a large undifferentiated space has size but not choice richness.
scope_includes: Count and diversity of distinct activity zones perceptible in the depicted scene.
Variety of seating, lighting, and niche typologies.
Affordance for differentiated behavioural settings in Barker's sense.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.clutter_load.definition: Cognitive load imposed by visual clutter — object density, disorder, occlusion.

extraction.compute_from: composite of object density count, edge-density distribution, occlusion overlap, feature-congestion measure (Rosenholtz et al.), and a VLM-read NASA-TLX-style probe

key_refs: Vogt, S., & Magnussen, S. (2007). Long-term memory for 400 pictures on a common theme. Experimental Psychology, 54(4), 298-303. https://doi.org/10.1027/1618-3169.54.4.298
McMains, S., & Kastner, S. (2011). Interactions of top-down and bottom-up mechanisms in human visual cortex. Journal of Neuroscience, 31(2), 587-597. https://doi.org/10.1523/JNEUROSCI.3766-10.2011
Rosenholtz, R., Li, Y., & Nakano, L. (2007). Measuring visual clutter. Journal of Vision, 7(2):17, 1-22. https://doi.org/10.1167/7.2.17

Hart, S. G., & Staveland, L. E. (1988). Development of NASA-TLX (Task Load Index): Results of empirical and theoretical research. Advances in Psychology, 52, 139-183. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0166-4115(08)62386-9

notes.extraction_notes: Inverse-of L21 is true *in expectation* but not deterministic: the off-diagonal cases (uncluttered-but-incoherent; cluttered-but-coherent) are real and important for BN dissociation. Sprint-3 panel should consider whether to model the L21/L28 relationship as a non-deterministic inverse with a residual term.

semantics.definition_long: Clutter Load names the demand a scene places on visual attention and working memory through high object density, mutual occlusion, disorder, and feature heterogeneity. Vogt and Magnussen (2007) and McMains and Kastner (2011) establish the neuroscience of clutter at the level of visual cortex: clutter slows search, inflates false alarms, and produces measurable interference between target and distractor representations in V4 and beyond. Clutter is conceptually the inverse of coherence (L21) — `inverse_of: cognitive.coherence_order` — but the inversion is not perfect, because a scene can be uncluttered yet incoherent (a sparse but stylistically inconsistent room) and cluttered yet coherent (a dense but rigorously organised library). The panel assigns `valence_polarity: negative` because high clutter load is, by definition, the costly end of the construct: the literature consistently links high clutter to elevated cognitive load, slower task performance, and elevated stress.
semantics.definition_short: Cognitive load imposed by visual clutter — object density, disorder, occlusion.
examples_negative: A monastic refectory with two long tables, identical chairs, and bare walls — low object density, low edge variance, low clutter.
An empty modernist gallery with a single sculpture — minimal content, no clutter.
A well-organised library: high object count (books) but low clutter because the organisation reduces feature congestion via repetition and alignment.
A meticulously arranged display window with three objects on a uniform backdrop.
examples_positive: An overstuffed thrift store interior with merchandise on every horizontal and vertical surface, mismatched colours and forms, and substantial inter-object occlusion — the canonical high-clutter case.
A cluttered desk with paper stacks, coffee cups, cables, and small objects piled without organisation.
A children's playroom mid-play with toys scattered across the floor and sofa, blankets in disarray, and visible disorder.
A workshop with tools hung on multiple pegboards, parts in mixed bins, and projects in various states of completion strewn across benches.
A storage unit during a move-out: stacked boxes of mismatched sizes, loose items poking out, no consistent organisation.
A warehouse aisle with mixed merchandise palletised at varying heights and orientations.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is canonical for clutter. The composite extractor combines (i) object density (count of distinct objects per unit image area, segmentation-based), (ii) edge density (per-pixel edge response from Sobel/Canny), (iii) occlusion overlap (count of partial-occlusion events between detected objects), (iv) feature-congestion measure following Rosenholtz et al. (2007), and (v) a VLM read of the NASA-TLX-style probe (Hart & Staveland, 1988) phrased against the V2.6 indicator language. A 5-point Likert is returned with confidence. Note that the composite is needed because no single observable captures clutter: edge density alone scores a forest as cluttered, object count alone misses heterogeneity-driven clutter. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: the perceptual-cognitive load mechanism is plausibly universal, but cultural tolerance for and aesthetic appreciation of clutter varies (a *wabi-sabi* aesthetic and a Scandinavian-minimalist aesthetic differ on whether high clutter is also high-disvalue).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan supports more accurate clutter estimation than 2D because object segmentation is grounded in 3D geometry rather than image-only inference, and occlusion-overlap counts can be computed directly from the depth field. Per-vantage clutter values are informative because clutter is locally concentrated — a cluttered corner in an otherwise clean room. The construct itself is snapshot-temporal; cumulative clutter effects over sustained exposure (clutter-driven fatigue) are out of scope and would route to a downstream temporal child node.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L28 indicator block — three rating items (felt clutter; difficulty tracking what is where; mental tiredness, NASA-TLX anchored) and two behavioural proxies (visual-search RT + false alarms; concurrent n-back errors).
scope_excludes: Complexity per se: a complex but well-organised scene (a Gothic cathedral interior) is high-complexity but not high-clutter. The Sprint-2 schedule reserves complexity for a separate latent.
Disorder as moral judgment: clutter is the perceptual-cognitive load measure, independent of normative judgments about tidiness.
Information density in well-designed visualisations: a dense data graphic can be high-information and low-clutter if features are organised.
scope_includes: Cases of high object density without organisation (the canonical clutter case).
Cases of moderate density with high feature heterogeneity (mismatched colours, materials, scales).
Both interior and exterior scenes (a cluttered alley, a cluttered shop window, a cluttered desk).

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Coherence in the scene or data. Use when observer reports or validated instruments indicate this affective/cognitive response. Avoid projecting the response from style cues alone.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Coherence in the scene or data. Use when observer reports or validated instruments indicate this affective/cognitive response. Avoid projecting the response from style cues alone.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Coherence in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Coherence.
Coherence is not visibly present.
User reports do not describe the space as Coherence.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Coherence.
Coherence is a dominant visible attribute.
User reports describe the space as Coherence.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Coherence; avoid inference. Use only when explicitly reported or supported by validated data.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Coherence; avoid speculation. Prefer user feedback or validated behavioral data.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Coherence.
Inferred Coherence without visual evidence.
No user-reported evidence of Coherence.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Coherence.
Dominant visible instance of Coherence.
User reports describe the space as Coherence.

cognitive.coherence_order.definition: Perceived consistency and visual organisation of a single-view scene.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for alignment, repetition, palette consistency, and edge-density regularity, read by a vision-language model conditioned on the V2.6 indicator items

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, S. (1987). Aesthetics, affect, and cognition: Environmental preference from an evolutionary perspective. Environment and Behavior, 19(1), 3-32. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013916587191001

notes.extraction_notes: L21 is the single-view, image-extractable axis of organisation. The Sprint-2 panel flagged L21 vs L29 (legibility) as a high-overlap pair: both name a kind of 'comprehensibility'. The disambiguation is that L21's parent_tags emphasise *visual-organisation* observables (alignment, repetition, palette, material consistency), while L29's emphasise *spatial-topology* observables (axial integration, sightline depth, decision-point clarity). Authors and validators should hold this distinction firmly.

semantics.definition_long: Coherence/Order names the perceived consistency, alignment, and organisation of a scene as it is read from a single vantage. It is the second axis of Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) Preference Matrix — paired with complexity at the immediate-understanding level — and indexes the degree to which the elements of a view appear to belong together, share a palette and material vocabulary, and are arranged in a grammar the eye can parse without effort. Following the Kaplans, coherence is distinct from legibility (their fourth cell): coherence is the *organisational* read of a single image, whereas legibility is the *configurational* read of a whole spatial system. The Sprint-2 panel preserves this distinction by routing coherence to L21 (single-view, image-extractable) and wayfinding-legibility to L29 (configurational, floor-plan-augmented). The construct sits in the focused-interaction register: it affects task performance by lowering the cost of visual parsing and by freeing attentional resources for non-visual concerns.
semantics.definition_short: Perceived consistency and visual organisation of a single-view scene.
examples_negative: A storage closet whose shelves are crowded with objects of unrelated shapes, sizes, colours, and materials, with no axis of alignment and no repeating element — the room is *visually noisy* but the noise is the absence of coherence (this is the L21–L28 dissociation: low coherence, high clutter).

A vintage shop deliberately styled as a curated jumble: the merchandise is interesting and the lighting is good, but mismatched eras and patterns produce low coherence even though the space is not strictly cluttered.

A lobby in which two competing renovations have left half the wall in dark wood panelling and half in white drywall, with mismatched flooring at the seam — the failure of *consistency* across an otherwise tidy room is the negative case.
A street market visible through a single photograph: lively, full of activity, but inherently ungoverned by a single grammar — high arousal (L23), low coherence.
examples_positive: A bookshop where shelves run parallel, books are sorted by colour and height, and the lighting is uniform — every element rhymes with every other and the eye reads the room at a glance.
A modernist living room in which the sofa, rug, and artwork all share a restricted palette of three earth tones, and the furniture is aligned to a clear axis perpendicular to the window wall.
A pharmacy aisle in which signage is consistent in font and colour, products are faced and gridded on the shelves, and the floor pattern repeats in regular bands.
A monastic refectory with two long tables flanking a central aisle, identical chairs along each table, and identical light fixtures overhead — the repetition is the order.
A hospital corridor in which floor, wall, and ceiling planes are each finished in a single material, doors recur at regular intervals, and signage uses one typeface and one colour.
A Japanese tea-room with mat modules, sliding screens, and a single tokonoma alcove — every dimension is a multiple of the tatami and the eye registers the grid as an organising principle.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is the canonical substrate for L21. The extractor reads upstream observables of alignment regularity (how closely furniture, fenestration, and architectural elements track a small number of dominant axes), repetition score (rhythmic recurrence of motif, module, or interval), colour-palette consistency (number of distinct hues and the variance of saturation/lightness within hues), material consistency (the count of distinct materials and the regularity of their distribution), and edge-density uniformity (whether complexity is evenly distributed across the field of view or concentrated in pockets). A VLM prompt conditioned on the Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) Preference-Matrix coherence cell and the V2.6 indicator items yields a 5-point Likert. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: the underlying perceptual-organisation principle is plausibly universal, but the *prototypical* coherent scene varies by tradition (a coherent Scandinavian interior and a coherent Mughal courtyard look very different).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D scan or VR scene supplies the same upstream observables as the 2D path with an additional benefit: the alignment regularity score can be computed against true 3D axes rather than image-projected ones, which removes the foreshortening artefacts that bias 2D estimates of order. Multi-view evidence also stabilises the colour-palette and material-consistency estimates by averaging across vantages. The construct itself does not require floor-plan augmentation — coherence is a property of the *appearance* of the scene from typical vantages, not of its global topology. If a scene is coherent from one vantage and incoherent from another, the extractor MUST report the per-vantage values rather than averaging, and downstream consumers can decide whether to summarise.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L21 indicator block — three rating items (felt coherence; ease of understanding what is where at a glance; visually organised vs chaotic) and two behavioural proxies (visual-search RT/accuracy; dual-task interference under high vs low coherence).
scope_excludes: Legibility/wayfinding (L29): legibility is configurational — a property of how the whole spatial system is organised for movement and orientation — and is image-degraded; L21 is the perceptual-organisation read of a single view.

Perceptual fluency (L27): fluency is the *processing-speed* readout (how quickly and easily the visual system parses the scene); L21 is the *organisational-structure* readout (whether the scene has a grammar to be parsed). They are causally linked but conceptually distinct — high coherence usually causes high fluency, but a degraded image of a coherent scene can be coherent yet not fluent.

Aesthetic preference per se (L24): coherence contributes to preference in the Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) model but is not preference itself; a coherent scene can still be unpleasant (cold institutional uniformity).
Beauty or stylistic merit: the construct is descriptive, not evaluative.
scope_includes: The single-view, image-extractable read of organisation: alignment, repetition, palette and material consistency, and the perceived presence of a compositional grammar.
Both interior and exterior scenes; the construct is agnostic to setting type provided a single dominant vantage is available.
Coherence-as-affordance: the construct names the *opportunity for fluent visual parsing* the scene provides, independent of whether a particular viewer takes that opportunity.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.compatibility.definition: Compatibility: the appraised fit between the depicted space's affordances and the occupant's intended activity per Kaplan & Kaplan's ART.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + VLM prompt encoding compatibility ART component + task-equipment detection + ergonomic-fit estimate + layout-task alignment inference

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, S. (1995). The restorative benefits of nature: Toward an integrative framework. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 15(3), 169-182. https://doi.org/10.1016/0272-4944(95)90001-2

Hartig, T., Korpela, K., Evans, G. W., & Garling, T. (1997). A measure of restorative quality in environments. Scandinavian Housing and Planning Research, 14(4), 175-194. https://doi.org/10.1080/02815739708730435
Gibson, J. J. (1979). The ecological approach to visual perception. Houghton Mifflin.

notes.bn_notes: Component of L17 (cognitive.restorativeness). Per Sprint 3 contest #1 structured-factor reorganisation; the parent-child relationship is a BN-structural commitment, not an extraction-time merger — independent extractor stubs retained.
notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 2 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 4). L20 is the *compatibility* sub-component of Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) ART, distinct from L17 (integrated Restorativeness), L18 (Being-Away), and L19 (Soft Fascination). L20 specifically tracks the affordance-activity fit dimension per Gibson's (1979) ecological-affordance framework: the depicted space matches the occupant's intended activity if task-relevant equipment is present, posture support is appropriate, layout supports the activity flow, and no task-obstructing features are visible. Temporal window is *sustained* because compatibility is a property of the activity-aligned-with-space relationship over the duration of the activity, not a snapshot.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised fit between the depicted space's affordances and the occupant's intended activity - the 'this place supports what I want to do here' dimension that is one of the four sub-components of Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) Attention Restoration Theory. Operationalised through cues that predict affordance-activity match: presence of task-relevant equipment (a desk and chair for work, a mat and quiet floor for yoga, a workbench and tools for craft), ergonomic posture support appropriate to the inferred activity, layout that supports the activity's spatial requirements, and absence of obstructions that would impede the activity. The construct sits at the intersection of Kaplan and Kaplan's ART framework and Gibson's (1979) ecological-affordance theory: it asks whether the depicted environment's invariant affordance structure matches the activity for which restoration is being sought. Compatibility is the canonical *compatibility* subscale of the Hartig et al. (1997) Perceived Restorativeness Scale.
semantics.definition_short: Fit between space affordances and intended activity.
examples_negative: A large glossy hotel lobby with marble surfaces and decorative art - visually pleasant but the affordances do not match any specific restorative activity (no comfortable seat positioning for reading, no quiet for meditation, no equipment for any task).
A cluttered workspace where work paperwork covers the surface intended for a meal or rest - the depicted activity-equipment is mismatched.
A hard-bench seating arrangement in a transit station - no posture support for any sustained activity beyond brief waiting.
An ornate but ergonomically poor antique chair in a museum-style display setting - task-relevant in appearance only, not in function.
examples_positive: A reading nook with a comfortable upholstered chair, a side-table at appropriate height, a reading lamp, and a stack of books within reach - all task-relevant equipment for sustained reading is present and ergonomically appropriate.
A meditation space with a cushion at an appropriate height, a clear floor area, soft natural light, and no task-obstructing artefacts - the layout exactly matches the intended sitting practice.
A workshop bench with the right tools deployed, the right surface height for the inferred craft, and adequate task lighting - explicit affordance-activity match for the craft work.
A garden bench placed facing a view, with footrest and side-surface for a drink, supporting sustained outdoor relaxation without obstructions.
A yoga studio with mats laid out in the appropriate spacing, mirrors, and hardwood floor - the space's affordances align with the expected practice.
A studio painting setup with easel, palette, natural daylight from a north-facing window, and a stool at the right height - canonical compatibility for the inferred painting activity.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor uses a VLM prompted with the compatibility ART component, plus structured upstream observables: (a) `furniture.task_relevant_equipment_present` (detection of equipment matched to the inferred activity - reading equipment for reading, mat for yoga, etc.), (b) `ergonomics.posture_support_index` (whether seat heights, back support, surface heights match standard ergonomic ranges), (c) `geometry.layout_task_alignment` (whether the spatial arrangement supports the inferred activity flow), (d) `scene.intended_activity_inferred` (the VLM's classification of the most plausible activity for the depicted space), and (e) `scene.task_obstruction_absent` (no visible obstructions to the inferred activity). The Likert is composed by the VLM against the Hartig et al. (1997) PRS compatibility subscale, conditional on the inferred activity. The V2.6 cue list 'Task-relevant equipment; ergonomic fit; layout for task' maps directly onto these observables. Confidence is moderate (Image_Judgability = 4) because activity inference from a static image can be ambiguous when the space affords multiple plausible activities.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR enactment of task' enrichment) the extractor can have an avatar attempt to enact the inferred activity and observe whether the space supports it - whether reach distances are appropriate, whether posture is sustainable, whether the activity flow is supported. This is materially more informative than 2D static-image inference because the activity is *enacted* rather than inferred. Ergonomic measurements (seat heights, surface heights, reach distances) are direct metric quantities rather than monocular estimates. As with the other restoration latents, 3D substrate provides materially higher confidence than 2D, particularly for distinguishing visually-similar spaces whose affordances differ only in metric ergonomic detail.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-REST-4 (Compatibility): rating items 'feels like compatibility', 'influence on choice', 'confidence from image'; behavioural proxies '2AFC choice frequency' and 'response-time consistency'.
Loads as a sub-factor of the broader V2.6 *restoration* superfactor; one of four ART components per Kaplan and Kaplan (1989) and Hartig et al. (1997).
scope_excludes: Pure being-away (decoupling from demands without activity-fit) - that is L18 (Being-Away); L20 specifically requires the activity-fit axis.
Pure soft fascination (effortless attention capture without activity context) - that is L19 (Soft Fascination); L20 requires an inferred activity to which the space is fit.
Integrated restorativeness - that is L17 (Restorativeness); L20 is one of four sub-components and is intentionally narrower.
scope_includes: Spaces whose visible equipment, posture support, and layout match an inferable activity - reading, meditation, craft, conversation, rest.
Spaces whose ergonomic affordances (seat dimensions, surface heights, lighting placement) align with the demands of the inferred activity per Gibson (1979).
Spaces showing the absence of task-obstructing artefacts - the inferred activity can proceed without removing or rearranging visible obstructions.

semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Complexity in the scene or data. Use when observer reports or validated instruments indicate this affective/cognitive response. Avoid projecting the response from style cues alone.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Complexity in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Complexity.
Complexity is not visibly present.
User reports do not describe the space as Complexity.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Complexity.
Complexity is a dominant visible attribute.
User reports describe the space as Complexity.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Complexity; avoid inference. Use only when explicitly reported or supported by validated data.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Complexity; avoid speculation. Prefer user feedback or validated behavioral data.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Complexity.
Inferred Complexity without visual evidence.
No user-reported evidence of Complexity.

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.
