cognitive.contamination_risk.definition: Hygiene threat and disgust appraisal of the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + surface-condition segmentation + trash / mould detection + touchpoint-condition assessment

key_refs: Rozin, P., Haidt, J., & McCauley, C. R. (2008). Disgust. In M. Lewis, J. M. Haviland-Jones, & L. F. Barrett (Eds.), Handbook of emotions (3rd ed., pp. 757–776). Guilford Press.
Schaller, M., & Park, J. H. (2011). The behavioral immune system (and why it matters). Current Directions in Psychological Science, 20(2), 99–103. https://doi.org/10.1177/0963721411402596

Curtis, V., de Barra, M., & Aunğer, R. (2011). Disgust as an adaptive system for disease avoidance behaviour. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 366(1563), 389–401. https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.2010.0117
Ulrich, R. S. (1984). View through a window may influence recovery from surgery. Science, 224(4647), 420–421. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.6143402

notes.bn_notes: Encoded as latent_score (binary) per panel: contamination risk is most usefully represented as a probability (0=clean, 1=contaminated) rather than a Likert because behavioural-immune responses are threshold-like (Schaller & Park, 2011) — below a critical level, contamination cues are ignored; above it, they trigger categorical avoidance. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: VLM prompted with the V2.7 indicator items ("How clean and hygienic\u2026", "How much does this space trigger disgust\u2026", "How willing would you be to touch common surfaces\u2026") returns a 0–1 contamination probability with confidence interval.

semantics.definition_long: The viewer's appraisal of hygiene threat and the disgust response to depicted contamination cues. The construct draws on Rozin, Haidt, and McCauley's (2008) cognitive analysis of disgust as a category-specific aversion to potential pathogen sources, and on Schaller and Park's (2011) behavioural-immune-system framework which interprets visually-triggered disgust as a low-cost, error-tolerant proxy for pathogen detection. Curtis, de Barra, and Aungar (2011) document the cross-cultural reliability of disgust elicitors associated with bodily fluids, decay, and infestation. Ulrich's (1984) classic study showed that even brief visual exposure to cues of biological contamination delays surgical recovery, anchoring contamination risk as a visually-extractable construct of clinical significance.
semantics.definition_short: Contamination/Disgust Risk — hygiene threat and disgust appraisal.
examples_negative: A messy desk piled with papers — visual disorder but no biological contamination cues; this is clutter (separate construct), not contamination.
A dim and isolated space — may evoke threat (L01) but is not necessarily dirty.

An industrial workshop with grease and oil stains on a concrete floor — visually unclean but the cues are non-biological; classification depends on whether contamination is interpreted as pathogen risk specifically.
A space with intentional patina or weathered surfaces (rustic restaurant, vintage cafe) — design cues that simulate age without signalling contamination.
examples_positive: How clean and hygienic does this space seem?
How much does this space trigger disgust?
How willing would you be to touch common surfaces here? (reverse-coded)
Avoidance: choose this vs alternative for eating (2AFC).
Go/no-go: 'enter' decision latency and rate.
A public restroom with visible stains on the toilet rim, paper trash overflowing the bin, and damp patches on the floor.
A cafeteria table with leftover food crusts, a dirty napkin, and a fly visible on the surface.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `surface.stain_density` (visible stains per unit visible surface area), `object.trash_presence` (count and prominence of refuse items), `surface.mold_cues` (damp / mould discolouration on walls and ceilings), `surface.touchpoint_condition` (state of door handles, faucets, switches that the viewer would imagine touching), and `scene.clutter_index` as a contributing but non-sufficient cue. The V2.7 cue list "stains; clutter; trash; damp/mold cues; touchpoints" maps directly onto stain_density, trash_presence, mold_cues, and touchpoint_condition. A VLM is prompted with the three V2.7 rating items on a 0–1 scale and the responses are aggregated into a contamination probability. Per panel mandate the value type is `latent_score` (binary 0–1) rather than ordinal Likert because behavioural-immune-system responses are threshold-like rather than gradiated (Schaller & Park, 2011).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can directly measure surface coverage of contamination indicators (stains, mould patches) as fractions of total visible-surface area, and can employ specialised sensors per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR usually not needed; sensors optional" — e.g., simulated olfactory cues or surface-condition tags attached to scene objects. The 3D substrate allows the extractor to identify high-touchpoint surfaces (handles, switches, faucets) by their geometric affordance and to weight contamination evidence preferentially toward those surfaces, reflecting the empirical finding that touchpoint cleanliness drives contamination appraisals more than peripheral-surface cleanliness.
scope_excludes: Personal-attack threat — that's L01 (cognitive.perceived_threat), a different threat category.
Visual disorder or clutter without biological-contamination cues — a separate aesthetic construct.
Surveillance pressure — a social-evaluative construct unrelated to hygiene.
scope_includes: Visible cues to biological contamination (stains, mould, trash, bodily-fluid traces).
Disgust-driven willingness/unwillingness to touch surfaces or occupy the space.
Pathogen-avoidance appraisals about food preparation, eating, or sleeping in the space.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.coziness.definition: Inviting softness, warmth, and enclosure that supports lingering.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for textile presence, warm-CCT lighting, soft seating, partial enclosure, low-luminance ambient with localised pools of warm light, read by a VLM

key_refs: Park, C. W., & MacInnis, D. J. (2006). What's in and what's out: Questions on the boundaries of the attitude construct. Journal of Consumer Research, 33(1), 16-18. https://doi.org/10.1086/504122
Linnet, J. T. (2011). Money can't buy me hygge: Danish middle-class consumption, egalitarianism, and the sanctity of inner space. Social Analysis, 55(2), 21-44. https://doi.org/10.3167/sa.2011.550202
Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.

notes.extraction_notes: High cross-cultural variance flagged: prototypes differ across hygge/Gemütlichkeit/iyashi/convivencia traditions. Sprint-3 panel may consider per-culture anchor sets and a culture-prior parent in the BN.

semantics.definition_long: Coziness names the cluster of perceptible properties that mark a space as inviting prolonged, low-arousal, positively-valenced occupancy: textiles and soft surfaces, warm-colour-temperature lighting, partial enclosure (a space within a space), localised pools of warm light against a darker ambient, and soft seating arrangements. The construct draws on Park & MacInnis's (2006) account of feelings as integral to consumer evaluation, and on the small but substantial cross-cultural literature on culturally-specific coziness concepts (Danish *hygge*, German *Gemütlichkeit*, Japanese *iyashi*, Mediterranean *convivencia*). The panel therefore flags L25 as `cross_cultural_variance: high`: although the underlying perceptual cluster is recognisable across cultures, the *prototype* of a cozy scene varies substantially, and self-report measures must be administered with culture-aware anchors. Coziness is positively-valenced by definition (`valence_polarity: positive`); a 'cold' or 'sterile' anchor at the low end of the scale is the negative pole, but the construct is not bi-polar in the L24 sense.
semantics.definition_short: Inviting softness, warmth, and enclosure that supports lingering.
examples_negative: A modernist gallery hall with white walls, polished concrete floor, cool-CCT downlights, and bench seating in stainless steel — high coherence (L21), low coziness.
A hotel-lobby waiting area with hard contemporary chairs, large unscreened windows, and uniformly bright cool-white lighting.
A clinical examination room: clean and orderly but with hard surfaces, cool-CCT lighting, and no textile presence.
A bus terminal departure hall: enclosed and well-lit but in cool tones with hard plastic seating and no localised warm light.
examples_positive: A small living-room corner with a wing-back armchair, a soft wool throw, a side table with a lit candle, and a single warm-CCT pendant lamp overhead — the rest of the room in shadow.
A traditional pub snug with leather banquettes, low warm sconces, dark-stained wood, and a small wood-burning stove.
A book-lined alcove off a larger reading room, with a deep upholstered chair and a brass desk lamp casting a warm pool of light.
A Japanese kotatsu setup in a small tatami room: low table with a heated underside, a quilted skirt, floor cushions, and a single low warm-CCT lamp.
A children's reading corner in a library with bean-bag seating, a textured rug, fairy lights, and partial enclosure by a low bookcase.
A bed-and-breakfast guest room with a quilted bedspread, lace curtains diffusing late-afternoon sun, and a small armchair by a side table with a warm lamp.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is the canonical substrate. The extractor reads upstream observables of textile presence (visible fabric area: cushions, throws, rugs, curtains), warm-CCT lighting (low-CCT signature in luminaires and reflected highlights), soft seating (upholstered furniture vs hard plastic/wood), partial enclosure (visible boundaries that screen the space-within-the-space without fully closing it off), and localised warm-light pools (luminance-and-colour gradient suggesting a small bright-warm region in a darker ambient). A VLM conditioned on Park & MacInnis (2006) and the cross-cultural coziness literature returns a 5-point Likert. Cross-cultural variance is HIGH: the prototypical cozy scene differs by tradition (Scandinavian hygge emphasises candlelight and minimalism; Mediterranean convivencia emphasises intimate gathering and food; Japanese iyashi emphasises tactile naturalness and hush). Sprint-3 panel may want to consider per-culture anchor sets.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan supports vantage-aware coziness estimation: a coziness 'pocket' (the corner with the lamp and the chair) may be high while the room as a whole is moderate. The extractor MUST report per-vantage values when scan coverage permits, since coziness is locally concentrated by definition. Multi-vantage data also helps disambiguate apparent coziness from photographic styling — a hero shot can manufacture coziness signatures that disappear from other angles.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L25 indicator block — three rating items (cozy felt-quality; lingering desire; physical comfort look) and two behavioural proxies (dwell-time allocation in multi-image browsing; soft-vs-hard seat-choice 2AFC).
scope_excludes: Restorativeness (typically a separate latent — the Kaplan attention-restoration construct): coziness is short-term lingering invitation, not the deep cognitive-restoration affordance of soft fascination, being-away, etc.
General positive valence (L24): coziness is a specific cluster within positive-valence space, characterised by low-arousal warmth; a sunlit garden is positive-valence but not particularly cozy.
Comfort-of-the-body considered apart from felt warmth: ergonomic comfort is not coziness; coziness is the specifically affective-perceptual reading of warmth and enclosure.
scope_includes: Interior scenes whose perceptible cluster of textile, warm light, partial enclosure, and soft seating conjoin to support lingering.
Exterior scenes are in scope when they offer analogous cluster signatures (a sheltered porch with cushioned seating and warm lantern light).
Both small- and large-scale spaces, provided localised cozy zones are perceptible (a large hall with a coziness-zone fireplace nook can score moderately high).

cognitive.crowding_pressure.definition: Crowding Pressure: the appraised proxemic compression imposed on an occupant by density and layout in the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + person/occupant detection + seating/area density estimate + aisle-width and queue detection

key_refs: Stokols, D. (1972). On the distinction between density and crowding: Some implications for future research. Psychological Review, 79(3), 275-277. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0032706
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.
Evans, G. W. (1979). Behavioral and physiological consequences of crowding in humans. Journal of Applied Social Psychology, 9(1), 27-46. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1559-1816.1979.tb00793.x

notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 1 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 5). Crowding Pressure is the *appraisal* of density rather than density itself, in the Stokols (1972) sense; the Likert reflects the perceived stress of the depicted density, which is sensitive to aisle width, queue presence, and proxemic distance to neighbours, not just bodies-per-area.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised proxemic compression imposed on an occupant by the density and layout of the depicted space - the perceived 'too-many-bodies-too-close' pressure that Stokols (1972) distinguished from raw physical density. Operationalised through cues that predict the subjective experience of crowding: the ratio of seating or floor-area to occupant count, the narrowness of aisles and circulation paths, the visible presence of queues, and the absolute interpersonal distances depicted (against Hall's 1966 proxemic zones). High crowding pressure is associated with downstream stress, reduced satisfaction, and lower performance (Evans, 1979). The construct is image-based and so reflects the *appraised affordance* of the depicted scene; ground-truth measurement requires occupancy time-series and is achievable in dynamic VR settings (V2.6 'VR dynamic occupancy' enrichment).
semantics.definition_short: Density/proxemic compression appraisal.
examples_negative: An empty atrium in mid-afternoon with one or two distant occupants and ample floor area - density is near zero and crowding pressure is at floor.
A spacious open-plan office with workstations spaced 4-5 m apart and few occupants visible; the depicted layout precludes proxemic compression.
A wide shopping mall corridor with occasional shoppers spread over a generous width; even with several people visible, the per-area density is low.
A theatre auditorium during intermission, a few patrons in the aisles but ample seat-row space; the proxemic distances exceed Hall's social zone.
examples_positive: A crowded subway car at rush hour with passengers shoulder-to-shoulder, no seats free, and standing-room density exceeding ~4 persons/square metre - Hall's intimate-zone is breached for most occupants.
A narrow cafe aisle (~50 cm wide) with patrons queueing along its length, brushing past seated diners; both the aisle compression and the queue presence cue high crowding pressure.
A small lift with eight occupants and a posted maximum of six; visible body-to-body contact and the inability to maintain Hall's personal distance cue maximum crowding.
A standing-only mosh at a music venue, packed bodies with no inter-person space; the depiction is at ceiling-effect density.
A hospital waiting room with every chair occupied and additional patients standing along the walls; seat-to-occupant ratio is below 1 and proxemic compression is clearly visible.
A school cafeteria at peak lunch with all tables full and a queue snaking through the seating area; both density and queue cues converge on a high crowding score.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor consumes upstream observables: (a) `scene.occupant_count` (person-detection count within the depicted scene), (b) `scene.seat_to_floor_ratio` and `scene.persons_per_unit_area` (estimated by combining person-detection with monocular floor-area estimation), (c) `geometry.aisle_width` from circulation-path detection, and (d) `geometry.queue_indicator` (linear arrangement of standing persons). The Likert composes these via thresholds calibrated to the Stokols (1972) and Evans (1979) literature: persons/square-metre is the primary driver, with aisle compression and queue presence as additive multipliers. The V2.6 cue list 'Seats/area; narrow aisles; queues' maps directly onto these three cue families. **Cross-cultural-variance** (Hall, 1966) is medium: cultural norms for acceptable density vary (e.g., Tokyo subway versus North American transit), and the extractor's stress-threshold calibration is Western-derived; downstream model trainers should treat the Likert as conditionally interpretable on cultural prior.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR dynamic occupancy' enrichment) the extractor can compute density directly from agent positions on the floor plane and can simulate dynamic occupancy time-series to derive *expected* crowding under varying activity loads. Aisle widths and queue formations are direct geometric measurements rather than monocular estimates. Per-pair proxemic distances against Hall's (1966) zones are computable per occupant pair, so the Likert can be derived as the worst-quartile proxemic violation rather than from a single global density value. Confidence is materially higher than 2D, particularly for distinguishing visually-similar high-density and low-density scenes whose appearance differs only in metric scale.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CROWD-1 (Crowding Pressure): rating items 'how crowded', 'stressful because of nearby people', 'personal space (reverse)'; behavioural proxies 'interpersonal-distance choice' and 'low-vs-high-occupancy 2AFC'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *crowding/density* superfactor alongside L15 (Social Exposure) and L16 (Resource Scarcity).
scope_excludes: Sparse high-occupancy spaces where the area is ample - high *count* with low *density* is not crowding (per Stokols' 1972 density-crowding distinction).
Single-occupant or empty scenes - crowding requires at least several persons in proximate relation; an empty room scores 0.
Social-exposure feelings due to being central in a sparse space - that is L15 (Social Exposure), not L14.
scope_includes: Spaces where the depicted ratio of bodies to floor or seat area exceeds threshold values associated with reported crowding stress (Stokols, 1972; Evans, 1979).
Spaces where physical proxemic distance between occupants is below Hall's (1966) personal-zone threshold (~1.2 m) for the predominant pair or cluster.
Spaces showing queue indicators (line of waiting persons), narrow aisles being negotiated by multiple persons simultaneously, or visible body-to-body contact.

cognitive.decision_point_load.definition: Branching/choice complexity at a navigational decision point.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + corridor-branch geometry + signage detection + luminance/material differentiation

key_refs: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning. *Cognitive Science*, *12*(2), 257-285. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15516709cog1202_4
Passini, R. (1984). *Wayfinding in architecture*. Van Nostrand Reinhold.
Weisman, J. (1981). Evaluating architectural legibility: Way-finding in the built environment. *Environment and Behavior*, *13*(2), 189-204. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013916581132004

notes.bn_notes: L31 is the only wayfinding-subdomain latent in Sprint 2; its parent set is structurally distinct from the social-signal and familiarity-novelty parents and the Spohn 50%-overlap audit should be straightforward.
notes.extraction_notes: Method family is `composite` (not `vlm`) because the Likert score is a function of perceptually anchored upstream observables (branch count, symmetry, signage adequacy) that are best computed by a geometry-and-segmentation pipeline. The composite extractor will combine these observables into a monotone Likert mapping documented in the extractor's docstring. The construct is instrumental: load is neither inherently good nor bad — high load is bad for naive navigators but is irrelevant in spaces where wayfinding is not the primary task.

semantics.definition_long: Decision-Point Load is the cognitive cost imposed on a navigator confronted with a junction whose branching topology, signage ambiguity, and perceptual symmetry require working-memory effort to resolve. The construct sits at the intersection of cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988), where intrinsic load scales with the number of interacting elements that must be held in mind, and Passini's (1984) wayfinding decision-point analysis, which operationalises wayfinding as a sequence of decisions taken at perceptually distinguishable junctions. A high-load decision point presents many alternative directions of travel, weak perceptual differentiation between alternatives, and either absent or contradictory signage; a low-load decision point presents a single dominant continuation, an unambiguous turn, or rich differentiating cues (light, view, signage, material change) that reduce the choice problem to a near-automatic one. The construct is image-judgable in the sense that the perceptual ingredients of branching, symmetry, and signage adequacy are visible, even when whole-route configurational depth is not. L31 is the only wayfinding latent in the Sprint 2 set and is method-family `composite` because the Likert score is a function of upstream observables — corridor count, dead-end visibility, signage presence, asymmetry indices — combined geometrically rather than read directly by a VLM.
semantics.definition_short: Branching/choice complexity at a navigational decision point.
examples_negative: A long single corridor with one obvious door at the end and a clear sign — load is low because there is no choice to make.
A T-junction with strong differentiation: one branch leads visibly toward a daylit atrium, the other toward a darker service zone — the choice is perceptually pre-resolved.
A lobby with one main entrance, one elevator bank visible from the entrance, and one clearly signed staircase — branching is present but each option is unambiguous.
A hotel corridor where room-number ranges are large, well-lit, and consistent on each end of the corridor — orientation is solved before approach.
examples_positive: A four-way corridor junction in a hospital where each branch is visually identical (same width, same flooring, same lighting) and the only signage is a single overhead sign with eight destinations crammed into two columns.
An airport intersection where five gates fan out from a central node and the floor-mounted gate-direction strips are partly occluded by a queue.
A subway mezzanine where two staircases, one escalator, and a corridor depart from a single landing, with no clear primary continuation and signage hung at conflicting heights.

An office building lobby with three unmarked corridors leading away from the elevator bank, each corridor visually similar in finish, with the only orientation cue being a small directory plate at adult shoulder height.
A university science building junction where wings A, B, and C diverge symmetrically and the only differentiator is room-number ranges on small wall plaques.
A shopping mall ring corridor at a four-way meeting with a central planter, where shop fronts on every side present similar luminance and sign density.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is sufficient when the junction itself is in frame. The extractor should consume upstream observables `geometry.corridor_branch_count` (the number of distinct continuations radiating from the principal vanishing region), `geometry.branch_symmetry_index` (a measure of how perceptually differentiable the branches are by width/luminance/material), `signage.presence_at_junction` (binary on whether wayfinding signage is visible at the decision node), `signage.legibility_score` (a composite of size, contrast, and occlusion of any visible signs), and `geometry.dead_end_visibility` (whether any branch terminates visibly within the image, providing a free elimination cue). The Likert mapping is monotone: load 0 = single continuation or one branch with strong asymmetric cue; load 4 = >= 4 branches, high symmetry, signage absent or illegible. The composite score is robust to camera position only when the camera is approximately at the navigator's standpoint at the junction; oblique or ceiling-mounted views distort the branching count and should be flagged.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans the branch count is recoverable from the room graph rather than vanishing-line analysis, and signage legibility can be evaluated from canonical viewing positions rather than the photographer's viewpoint. The 3D extractor should compute (i) the number of convex-space exits from the junction node, (ii) the angular distribution of those exits (more uniform => higher symmetry => higher load), (iii) the integrated luminance and material variance along the first metres of each branch (higher variance => more differentiation => lower load), and (iv) the canonical-viewing-distance legibility of every detectable sign. Region support is false: L31 is an integrated score over the junction region rather than a per-pixel attribute. Monocular-3D approximation is partial: branch count and angular distribution are recoverable, but luminance variance along branches degrades with depth-estimation noise.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Decision-Point Load': rating items for (a) extent to which the space feels like a high-load decision point, (b) extent to which the appraisal would influence choice to be there, (c) confidence in the image-only judgement. Behavioural proxies (2AFC choice frequency, RT consistency) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Whole-route configurational complexity (overall depth from entrance to destination) — that is a floor-plan property and is out of scope for image-only Sprint 2.
Subjective navigational anxiety as a downstream affect — L31 is the perceptual/structural load, not the felt anxiety it may produce.
Crowd-induced confusion (people blocking signs) as a transient occlusion artefact — L31 codes the spatial-and-signage substrate, not the dynamic occlusion.
scope_includes: Local junction load measurable from a single image: branch count, branch symmetry, signage presence, signage legibility.
Perceptual symmetry between alternatives that defeats heuristic choice (identical finishes, identical widths, identical lighting).
Signage adequacy at the junction — present, legible, unambiguous versus absent, occluded, contradictory.

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Error in the scene or data. Use when observer reports or validated instruments indicate this affective/cognitive response. Avoid projecting the response from style cues alone.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Error in the scene or data. Use when observer reports or validated instruments indicate this affective/cognitive response. Avoid projecting the response from style cues alone.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Error in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Error.
Error is not visibly present.
User reports do not describe the space as Error.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Error.
Error is a dominant visible attribute.
User reports describe the space as Error.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Error; avoid inference. Use only when explicitly reported or supported by validated data.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Error; avoid speculation. Prefer user feedback or validated behavioral data.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Error.
Inferred Error without visual evidence.
No user-reported evidence of Error.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Error.
Dominant visible instance of Error.
User reports describe the space as Error.

cognitive.escape_efficacy.definition: Belief that escape from the depicted space is easy and fast.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + exit-instance segmentation + path-clarity geometry + signage detection

key_refs: Bandura, A. (1977). Self-efficacy: Toward a unifying theory of behavioral change. Psychological Review, 84(2), 191–215. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295X.84.2.191
Appleton, J. (1975). The experience of landscape. Wiley.
Lazarus, R. S., & Folkman, S. (1984). Stress, appraisal, and coping. Springer.
Stamps, A. E. (2005). Visual permeability, locomotive permeability, safety, and enclosure. Environment and Behavior, 37(5), 587–619. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013916505276741

notes.bn_notes: Conceptual inverse of cognitive.perceived_threat (L01) along the secondary-appraisal axis (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984): threat = primary appraisal of harm; escape efficacy = secondary appraisal of coping resources. The two are separable in scenes that combine high concealment with abundant exits, or vice versa.
notes.extraction_notes: VLM prompted with the V2.7 indicator items ("How easy would it be to leave quickly\u2026", "How clear are the routes\u2026", "How confident are you that you could find your way out\u2026") returns a Likert 0–4.

semantics.definition_long: The viewer's belief that, were it necessary, escape from the depicted space would be easy and fast. The construct sits within Bandura's (1977) self-efficacy framework as a domain-specific efficacy belief about egress, and within Lazarus and Folkman's (1984) appraisal model as the secondary-appraisal complement to primary threat appraisal. Stamps (2005) operationalised the related construct of *locomotive permeability* and showed that perceived safety in built environments is jointly determined by visual prospect, refuge availability, and the perceived ease of locomotive egress. Escape efficacy is therefore not the inverse of threat: a viewer can simultaneously perceive a scene as threatening (e.g., a long alley) and as easy to escape (e.g., the alley terminates in an open plaza visible at the far end).
semantics.definition_short: Escape Efficacy — belief that escape is easy and fast.
examples_negative: A windowless basement room with no visible exit — low efficacy, but the appraisal could equally be classified as a refuge cue (L‐refuge in V2.7) rather than escape efficacy proper.
A space with abundant exits but a confusing maze-like layout — multiple exits but route clarity is poor; high exit count alone does not yield high efficacy.
A cosy enclosed library reading nook — the absence of visible exits is intentional and welcome; classifying this as low efficacy would conflate efficacy with desirability.
An open plaza with no concealment — efficacy is high but the question is moot because no escape is contemplated.
examples_positive: How easy would it be to leave quickly if needed?
How clear are the routes to an exit?
How confident are you that you could find your way out without help?
VR navigation: time-to-exit, path inefficiency, decision-point errors.
Image task: locate exit; RT + accuracy.
A wide open lobby with multiple visible exit doors and clear sightlines from the implied viewpoint to each one.
A short hallway with a clearly-marked exit sign at the end and no decision points along the route.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `affordance.exit_visibility` (whether at least one exit is visible from the implied viewpoint), `affordance.exit_count` (number of distinct exits visible), `scene.path_length_to_exit` (estimated distance along the depicted route), `scene.route_clarity` (number of decision-points and presence of obstacles along the route), and `wayfinding.signage_density` (presence and legibility of exit signage). The V2.7 cue list "visible exits; short paths; clear routes" maps directly onto exit_visibility, path_length_to_exit, and route_clarity respectively. A VLM is prompted with the three V2.7 rating items on a 0–4 Likert and the responses are aggregated into the latent score with a confidence interval. The VLM approach is preferred because escape efficacy is an integrative appraisal that synthesises exit availability, route clarity, and self-efficacy beliefs, none of which decomposes cleanly into a single hand-crafted geometric measure.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can directly measure path-to-exit distance and the count of decision-points along the shortest path. Per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR path-to-exit time; wayfinding errors" — the behavioural proxies of actual time-to-exit and wayfinding error rate provide ground-truth calibration for the appraisal latent, though the registry-level value remains the appraisal score and not the behavioural measurement. Sightline isovist analysis from the implied viewpoint enables explicit calculation of the angular subtense of visible exit doors, which is a strong predictor of efficacy ratings. The 3D substrate also exposes the floor plane geometry needed to verify that the apparent route is in fact traversable rather than blocked by furniture or steps.
scope_excludes: General navigability or wayfinding for getting *to* destinations within the space — efficacy is specifically about egress.
Aesthetic preference for open spaces — not the same as efficacy.
Crowd-egress capacity (fire-code load factor) — a separate engineering construct.
scope_includes: Belief that one could leave the depicted space rapidly if one wished to.
Perceived clarity and short length of the route to an exit.
Confidence that one could find an exit unaided.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 3 contest #1 disposition: fourth ART component split out of L17 per Spohn structured-factor decision)

cognitive.extent.definition: Extent — sense that the environment is a coherent rich whole.

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.

Hartig, T., Korpela, K., Evans, G. W., & Gärling, T. (1997). A measure of restorative quality in environments. Scandinavian Housing and Planning Research, 14(4), 175–194. https://doi.org/10.1080/02815739708730435
Korpela, K., Hartig, T., Kaiser, F. G., & Fuhrer, U. (2001). Restorative experience and self-regulation in favorite places. Environment and Behavior, 33(4), 572–589.

notes.bn_notes: Component of L17 (cognitive.restorativeness) per Sprint 3 contest #1 structured-factor disposition; the fourth ART component, separate from L18 (being-away), L19 (soft fascination), L20 (compatibility).
notes.extraction_notes: Sprint 3 introduced as a new latent. Image judgability is marginal (Sprint 2 panel rule judg=3 → from_2d 'partial', but the panel deliberately set from_2d='no' here because the construct requires whole-world inference that monocular 2D consistently fails on).

semantics.definition_long: Extent is the fourth Attention Restoration Theory (ART) component (Kaplan & Kaplan, 1989, pp. 184–187): the sense that the environment is rich and coherent enough to engage attention as a whole world rather than as a collection of disjointed elements. A small garden can have extent if it is internally coherent and inviting of mental exploration; a vast but disorganised parking lot lacks extent. Sprint 3 added this latent as a separate construct rather than absorbing it into restorativeness, per the contest #1 disposition (Spohn structured-factor decision, 2-1 over Russell's full split). See Hartig, Korpela, Evans, & Gärling (1997) for the validated PRS measurement of extent and Korpela, Hartig, Kaiser, & Fuhrer (2001) on extent's discriminant validity from coherence.
semantics.definition_short: Extent — the sense that the environment is a coherent rich whole.
examples_negative: A blank corridor that suggests no further world.
A cluttered storage room whose contents do not form a coherent set.
A parking lot whose vastness lacks internal organisation.
A room composed of mismatched furniture suggesting no shared purpose.
examples_positive: A small Japanese garden whose paths invite continued mental exploration.
A reading room with bookshelves that suggest a coherent intellectual world.
A coastline view whose features suggest a continuous landscape system.
A curated museum gallery whose objects relate as parts of a larger story.
A craftsman's workshop whose tools form a coherent practice ecology.
A wooded trail whose turns promise more of a coherent forest beyond view.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Image-only extraction is degraded for extent because the construct requires judging whether the visible scene implies a coherent further world. Use a VLM prompted with the Hartig PRS-extent items (e.g., 'There is much to explore and discover here'); supplement with a scene-graph completeness score from upstream observables scene.global_coherence_index and geometry.scene_graph_completeness. Field-of-view matters: ≥ 90° horizontal improves judgability noticeably.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: VR rendering improves extent judgement substantially because the user can look around and infer the wider world. Pair the VLM prompt with a VR-trial where the user rates extent after a 30-second free-look exploration. The comparison metric is intra-rater consistency between 2D and VR judgement.
scope_excludes: Soft fascination (L19) — the *capture* of attention, distinct from the *world* the attention enters.
Coherence/Order (L21) — local visual organisation, not the world-extent judgement.
Compatibility (L20) — fit between user goals and environment, not the world's intrinsic richness.
scope_includes: Whole-scene mental-world coherence judgements.
Sense of an interpretable environment beyond what is currently visible.
Coherent extension of the present view into an implied larger setting.

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.familiarity.definition: Binary inference that the space is prototypical and category-typical for its evident function.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + scene-style classification + canonical-fixture detection + layout-graph schema matching

key_refs: Zajonc, R. B. (1968). Attitudinal effects of mere exposure. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology Monograph Supplement*, *9*(2, Pt.2), 1-27. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0025848
Whittlesea, B. W. A. (1993). Illusions of familiarity. *Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition*, *19*(6), 1235-1253. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.19.6.1235

Reber, R., Schwarz, N., & Winkielman, P. (2004). Processing fluency and aesthetic pleasure: Is beauty in the perceiver's processing experience? *Personality and Social Psychology Review*, *8*(4), 364-382. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327957pspr0804_3

notes.bn_notes: L37 and L38 are explicit duals (`inverse_of: cognitive.novelty`). Per the special-case rule, the two parent sets are kept nearly disjoint: L37 emphasises corpus-*similarity* proxies — `style.consistency_score`, `objects.common_fixture_presence`, `style.corpus_similarity_score`, `layout.canonical_schema_match` — none of which appear in L38's parent set. L38's parent set instead emphasises corpus-*distance* proxies (unusual fixture-class presence, novel material combinations, layout-graph distance from category centroid). This architecture honours Spohn's 50%-overlap rule for explicitly dual constructs and ensures that the BN can represent the inverse relation cleanly. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: The binary (latent_score) value type follows the panel decision that prototypicality is more stable across perceivers as a binary judgement than as a fine-grained gradient. The familiarity-novelty pair (L37/L38) is structured per the special-case rule with disjoint parent sets — L37 emphasises corpus-similarity proxies (style consistency, common-fixture presence, layout schema match); L38 emphasises corpus-distance proxies. This structuring honours the Spohn 50%-overlap rule for explicitly dual constructs.

semantics.definition_long: Familiarity is the perceiver's binary inference that the space is prototypical for its evident category — a setting whose layout, fixtures, and stylistic conventions match the schematic expectations a perceiver brings from prior exposure to spaces of this type. The construct rests on two literatures: Zajonc's (1968) mere-exposure work, which established that repeated exposure to a stimulus increases its perceptual fluency and felt familiarity, and Whittlesea's (1993) revealing-fluency studies, which demonstrated that subjective familiarity is a downstream attribution of perceptual fluency rather than a direct memory access. Familiarity in the architectural setting is therefore the inference 'I have seen spaces like this before, and this one matches the schema'. The construct is encoded as binary (latent_score) per the Sprint 2 panel binary-candidate decision: a setting either matches the perceiver's schema for its category (cafe, library, bedroom, hotel lobby) or it does not, and the binary judgement is more stable across perceivers than a fine-grained gradient. Familiarity is the explicit dual of L38 (Novelty); the two constructs are inverse on the corpus-similarity axis, with L37 emphasising similarity to the corpus and L38 emphasising distance from it. Per the special-case rule, L37's parent set emphasises corpus-similarity proxies (style consistency, common-fixture presence) and L38's parent set emphasises corpus-distance proxies, keeping the two parent lists nearly disjoint per Spohn. Familiarity is neutral in valence and instrumental: it produces comfort for some perceivers and boredom for others depending on individual differences and the demands of the situation.
semantics.definition_short: Binary inference that the space is prototypical and category-typical for its evident function.
examples_negative: A converted industrial loft repurposed as a cafe with no counter visible from entry, exposed mechanical systems, and bespoke furniture — high on novelty (L38), low on familiarity.
A hotel room with a centred soaking tub, no bed visible from the entry, and an unconventional layout — schema-violating.
A library that looks like a cafe (movable furniture, a coffee bar, no perimeter stacks) — schema-mixed and likely L37-negative for both schemas.
A maker-space or co-working area whose layout follows none of the conventional office or library schemas.
examples_positive: A high-street chain coffee shop with a counter at the back, dark-wood seating, pendant lighting, a chalkboard menu, and standard merchandising — every element matches the global cafe schema.

A hotel room with a queen bed centred on the long wall, a bedside table on each side, a desk facing the window, a flat-screen TV opposite the bed, and standard hospitality artwork — every element matches the global hotel-room schema.
A suburban living room with a couch facing a TV, a coffee table between, and a side chair at right angles — the canonical American suburban living-room schema.
A classroom with rows of desks facing a teacher's desk and a whiteboard, with bulletin boards on the side walls — every element matches the schoolroom schema.
A standard suburban kitchen with stove, sink, and refrigerator in an L-arrangement, an island in the centre, and standard upper cabinets — every element matches the kitchen schema.
A traditional library reading room with long tables, table lamps with green shades, perimeter stacks, and a quiet-zone signage convention — matches the library-reading-room schema.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is well-suited to familiarity extraction because schema-match operates on perceptually salient features. The extractor should consume upstream observables `style.consistency_score` (a measure of stylistic homogeneity within the visible scene), `objects.common_fixture_presence` (a binary indicator vector over the canonical fixtures of the inferred category — counter, bed, conference table, etc.), `style.corpus_similarity_score` (a measure of how close the scene is to the centroid of its inferred category in a learned scene-style space), and `layout.canonical_schema_match` (a binary indicator that the layout matches the canonical configuration for the category). The binary positive judgement requires a high corpus-similarity score, presence of canonical fixtures, and canonical-layout match. Per the special-case rule, the parent set is dominated by corpus-*similarity* proxies that L38 (Novelty) does not consume.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans, familiarity extraction benefits from full-mesh fixture recognition, from canonical-distance style classification, and from layout matching against schematic templates. The 3D extractor should compute (i) per-fixture canonical-class probabilities, (ii) layout-graph match against schematic category templates, (iii) full-scene style-consistency scoring, and (iv) corpus-similarity scoring against a learned scene-style space populated with category exemplars. Region support is false. Monocular-3D approximation is partial because layout-graph matching depends on accurate depth estimation.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Familiarity': rating items for (a) felt familiarity, (b) extent to which the appraisal would influence choice to be there, (c) confidence in the image-only judgement. Behavioural proxies (2AFC choice frequency, RT consistency) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Coziness (L25 in the Sprint 2 set is the affective coziness construct; L37 is the recognition construct). Per the panel: 'coziness is affect; familiarity is recognition.' A novel design can be cozy; a familiar one can be cold.

Personal autobiographical familiarity — L37 is *prototypical* familiarity (this space matches the schema) not *autobiographical* familiarity (I have been here before). Autobiographical familiarity belongs in placeness/attachment territory, not L37.
Care/maintenance (L33) — a poorly-maintained space can still match its category schema; a meticulously-maintained space can be schema-violating.
scope_includes: Schema-match for the evident category — fixtures, layout, and stylistic conventions consistent with the perceiver's prior exposure to spaces of this type.
Common-fixture presence — the canonical fixtures of the category (counter in cafe, bed in bedroom, conference table in boardroom).
Style consistency with corpus — finishes, materials, and detailing within the typical range for this category.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.formality.definition: Seriousness, status intensity, and norm strength legible from material austerity, symmetry, and signage.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + axial-symmetry analysis + material-austerity scoring + signage-script detection + furniture-arrangement formality scoring

key_refs: Goffman, E. (1959). *The presentation of self in everyday life*. Anchor Books.
Mehrabian, A. (1981). *Silent messages: Implicit communication of emotions and attitudes* (2nd ed.). Wadsworth.
Goffman, E. (1967). *Interaction ritual: Essays on face-to-face behavior*. Anchor Books.

notes.bn_notes: Formality shares some material parents with prestige (L32) and care (L33). The Spohn 50%-overlap audit must verify disjointness via formality-specific parents — `composition.axial_symmetry_score`, `signage.behavioural_script_presence`, `furniture.formal_arrangement_score`, `composition.ceremonial_threshold_present` — none of which appear in L32 or L33 parent sets.
notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 records L39 as Tier 3 (Image_Judgability_1to5: 3); the registry preserves this with `from_2d: "partial"`. Cross-cultural recalibration is essential because the cues that mark formality differ substantially across traditions.

semantics.definition_long: Formality is the perceiver's inference that the space prescribes a strong behavioural script — that the norms governing comportment, dress, speech volume, and posture are salient and demanding. Following Goffman's (1959) *Presentation of Self in Everyday Life*, formality is the front-stage register in which performance is governed by explicit conventions; the architectural correlate consists of the cues that make those conventions legible: material austerity (rather than informal warmth), symmetry (rather than informal asymmetry), explicit signage prescribing or proscribing behaviour, formalised seating arrangements (rather than informal clusters), and ritual markers (raised platforms, axial alignments, ceremonial thresholds). Mehrabian's work on formality cues established that perceivers reliably read these cues from the built environment and adjust their comportment accordingly. Formality is mixed in valence: the same space (a courtroom, a state legislature, a temple) signals respect-worthy seriousness to some perceivers and exclusionary anxiety to others, and downstream affective response depends on the perceiver's relation to the institution. Cross-cultural variance is high: the cues that mark formality differ substantially across cultures (Japanese formality through restraint and material precision; Western formality through symmetry and material weight; Islamic formality through geometric repetition and threshold articulation), and extractors trained on one tradition will mis-classify within another.
semantics.definition_short: Seriousness, status intensity, and norm strength legible from material austerity, symmetry, and signage.
examples_negative: A neighbourhood cafe with mismatched furniture, casual signage, and no prescribed behavioural script — informal in every register.
A loft-converted gallery with informal seating, exposed structure, and minimal signage.
A community recreation centre with mixed-use spaces and no axial-symmetric arrangement.
A backyard barbecue setting with picnic furniture and no behavioural-script signage.
examples_positive: A courtroom with a raised judicial bench, axial symmetry between bench and well, formal jury and counsel arrangements, polished hardwood throughout, and explicit posted signage on courtroom decorum.
A cathedral nave with axial symmetry, ceremonial threshold articulation, material weight (stone, hardwood, brass), and posted signage prescribing comportment.
A state legislature chamber with axial-symmetric tiered seating, raised speaker's platform, formal portraits on the walls, and material austerity.
A traditional Japanese tea room with prescribed seating, restrained material palette, ritual threshold change (shoe removal), and an explicit behavioural script encoded in the architecture itself.
An academic lecture hall with a raised dais, axial symmetry, formal signage (lecture title, lecturer name, university emblem), and tiered fixed seating.
A hotel ballroom set for a black-tie event with axial symmetry, material weight (chandeliers, polished floor, draped tables), and formalised seating cards on a plan.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image supports formality extraction when the compositional structure and signage are in frame. The extractor should consume upstream observables `composition.axial_symmetry_score`, `material.austerity_score` (a measure of palette restraint and material weight), `signage.behavioural_script_presence` (binary indicator on whether posted signage prescribes or proscribes behaviour), `furniture.formal_arrangement_score` (a measure of formalised versus informal seating arrangements), and `composition.ceremonial_threshold_present`. The Likert mapping is monotone in the joint presence of these cues. Cross-cultural recalibration is required because formality cues differ across traditions.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans, formality extraction benefits from full-volume axial-symmetry analysis, from canonical-distance signage legibility, and from full inspection of material weight and precision. The 3D extractor should compute (i) volumetric axial-symmetry scores from full-mesh analysis, (ii) per-surface material weight and precision scores, (iii) per-fixture arrangement formality scores, and (iv) ceremonial-threshold detection from sectional analysis. Region support is false. Monocular-3D approximation is partial because compositional symmetry is robust to depth noise but ceremonial-threshold detection benefits from full geometry.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Formality': rating items for (a) felt formality, (b) extent to which the appraisal would influence choice to be there, (c) confidence in the image-only judgement. Behavioural proxies (2AFC choice frequency, RT consistency) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Prestige (L32) — prestige can be informal (a private members' lounge with luxurious but informal furnishings is L32-high, L39-low); formality can be inexpensive (a courtroom in a modest county building is L39-high, L32-low).
Awe (L36) — awe is self-diminishing; formality is norm-intensifying. They co-occur in some monumental spaces but are distinct constructs.
Care/maintenance (L33) — informal spaces can be well-maintained, formal spaces can be poorly-maintained.
scope_includes: Material austerity — restrained, weighty, or precise material palettes that signal seriousness.
Symmetry and axial alignment — formal compositional structure that signals ritual or institutional weight.
Behavioural-script signage — posted rules, prescribed seating, ceremonial thresholds, dress-code notices.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.interruption_likelihood.definition: Interruption Likelihood: the appraised rate at which an occupant of the depicted space would be interrupted.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + circulation-path detection + workstation/seat instance segmentation + boundary/partition detection + doorway proximity estimate

key_refs: Mark, G., Gudith, D., & Klocke, U. (2008). The cost of interrupted work: More speed and stress. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 107-110). ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/1357054.1357072

Sundstrom, E., Burt, R. E., & Kamp, D. (1980). Privacy at work: Architectural correlates of job satisfaction and job performance. Academy of Management Journal, 23(1), 101-117. https://doi.org/10.2307/255498
Czerwinski, M., Horvitz, E., & Wilhite, S. (2004). A diary study of task switching and interruptions. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 175-182). ACM.

notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 1 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 5) - circulation-path and boundary cues are reliably visible from 2D imagery. Construct is rate-typed (per Mark, Gudith, & Klocke, 2008, on interruption frequency in modern work) and so is operationalised over a 'short_period' temporal window rather than an instantaneous snapshot.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised rate at which an occupant doing focused work in the depicted space would be interrupted by other people - co-workers approaching to ask questions, passers-by routing through, or proximate conversations drawing attention. Operationalised through cues that predict pass-through traffic and approachability: circulation paths that route directly past or through the workspace, absence of boundaries (low or no partitions, no doors, open frontage), workstation orientation that exposes the occupant's face to the circulation, and proximity to high-traffic destinations (entrances, kitchens, printers). The construct is grounded in the Mark, Gudith, and Klocke (2008) literature on interruption frequency and the task-resumption cost it imposes on focused work, and in Sundstrom et al. (1980) on architectural correlates of workplace privacy.
semantics.definition_short: Chance of being interrupted.
examples_negative: A private corner office with a closed door at the end of a cul-de-sac corridor - circulation does not pass the door and the occupant is structurally shielded.
A dedicated focus-room within a co-working space, accessible by a separate door and not on any pass-through route.
A residential study at the end of a hallway with no continuing circulation beyond it; the hallway is a dead end so no through-traffic is possible.

A workstation at the inside corner of an L-shaped partitioned cubicle, oriented away from the cubicle opening and the corridor beyond - the worker's task surface and visual field are decoupled from the circulation path.
examples_positive: An open-plan workstation directly adjacent to the main circulation corridor of an office, no partitions and chair-back facing the corridor; visible pass-through traffic cues high interruption rate.
A reception-desk worktop facing the entrance lobby with no shielding partition; the occupant is structurally exposed to every arrival and is therefore highly interruptible.
A kitchen-island desk arrangement in a co-working space where the desk sits on the route between the entrance and the coffee bar; circulation routing alone predicts frequent interruption.
A bench-style hot-desking row in an open office with no end-cap partitions and a printer at one end of the row, drawing colleagues past every workstation.
An open lounge nook flanked by two corridors meeting at a T-junction directly behind the seat; both circulation streams converge near the worker.
A small meeting table placed directly inside an entry vestibule with no door between table and circulation; entrants pass within 1 m of seated occupants.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor consumes upstream observables: (a) `geometry.circulation_path_proximity` from path-trace inference based on floor visibility and door placement, (b) `geometry.boundary_presence` and partition heights, (c) `scene.doorway_proximity` and `scene.through_traffic_indicator` (whether circulation routes through rather than terminates at the depicted workstation), and (d) `furniture.workstation_orientation_to_circulation` (does the occupant face or have back to the path). The Likert is composed by a weighted sum reflecting Mark et al. (2008) findings that frequent visual exposure to colleagues is the strongest single predictor of self-reported interruption. The V2.6 cue 'Circulation through workspace; lack of boundaries' is operationalised as path-routing-through plus boundary-absence; either alone is moderate, both together cue maximum interruption likelihood. Confidence is reduced when circulation paths are occluded or when the depicted scene shows only a fragment of the surrounding circulation network.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extractor can simulate pedestrian flow directly (per the V2.6 'VR traffic simulation' enrichment): place agents at scene entry points, route them to plausible destinations, and count the rate at which agent paths pass within an interruption-radius (~2 m) of the workstation. The metric becomes a rate (events / time) rather than an inferred-from-cues estimate. Distance-to-nearest-circulation-path is directly measurable from the floor plane and workstation position, and boundary-aperture areas (door openings, partition gaps) are direct measurements rather than monocular estimates. As with L11, 3D substrate provides materially higher confidence than 2D for this construct.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-PRIV-1 (Acoustic Privacy): rating items 'how likely interrupted', 'easy to focus without disturbance', 'layout invites pass-through (reverse)'; behavioural proxies 'sustained-attention lapses' and 'relocation frequency'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *privacy/attention* superfactor alongside L11 (Acoustic Privacy) and L13 (Territorial Support).
scope_excludes: Acoustic-only interruption (overheard conversations) - this is captured by L11 (Acoustic Privacy) and not by L12, which concerns *direct* social interruption events.
Self-imposed task-switching (notifications, phone) - L12 concerns environmentally-driven interruption from co-present others, not internally-driven distraction.
Crowding-induced disturbance (lots of people present but stationary) - that is L14 (Crowding Pressure); L12 is specifically about the *rate of interruption events*.
scope_includes: Spaces where an occupant performing focused cognitive work would be visible and approachable from circulation paths.
Spaces where physical boundaries (partitions, doors, screens) are absent or low enough that visual and conversational interruption is geometrically unimpeded.
Spaces whose layout positions a workstation in a high-traffic zone (entrance, hallway intersection, route to shared resource).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.landmark_clarity.definition: Presence and saliency of stable, distinctive anchors that support orientation.

extraction.compute_from: composite of distinctive-feature detection, signage clarity, contrast-against-context, and Sorrows-Hirtle landmark-type classification combined with a VLM probe

key_refs: Lynch, K. (1960). The image of the city. MIT Press.

Sorrows, M. E., & Hirtle, S. C. (1999). The nature of landmarks for real and electronic spaces. In C. Freksa & D. M. Mark (Eds.), Spatial information theory: Cognitive and computational foundations of geographic information science (pp. 37-50). Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-48384-5_3
Hillier, B., & Hanson, J. (1984). The social logic of space. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511597237

notes.extraction_notes: L30 is a constituent of Lynch's broader legibility (L29) but is tagged separately so the BN can decompose legibility = f(landmark clarity, axial integration, convex-map depth). Sprint-3 panel may revisit whether to model L30 as a deterministic-given-features parent of L29 or as an independent latent with shared parents.

semantics.definition_long: Landmark Clarity names the degree to which a setting offers stable, distinctive anchors that an inhabitant can use to orient and to construct a mental map. Lynch (1960) identified landmarks as one of the five elements of the urban image, and Sorrows and Hirtle (1999) refine the typology into visual landmarks (distinguished by perceptual contrast against their context), structural landmarks (distinguished by their position in the spatial structure), and cognitive landmarks (distinguished by personal/cultural significance). L30 is primarily concerned with visual and structural landmarks, both of which can be assessed from imagery: a visually distinctive feature (a coloured wall, a unique object, a contrasting form) or a structurally distinctive position (a node at a major intersection, a terminus). L30 is one of the constituent elements of L29 (Lynch's broader legibility), but the panel separates them so the BN can model legibility as a function of landmark clarity *plus* axial-integration *plus* convex-map depth, rather than as a single black-boxed construct.
semantics.definition_short: Presence and saliency of stable, distinctive anchors that support orientation.
examples_negative: A homogeneous suburban tract with identical houses on identical plots and no distinguishing features along the street.
A modernist housing estate whose buildings are visually uniform and whose courtyards repeat without variation.
A featureless underground concourse with identical white walls and identical signage at each junction.
An academic building whose corridors are visually identical floor-to-floor with no per-floor or per-wing distinguishing finish.
examples_positive: A neighbourhood with a single tall church spire visible from most streets — the canonical visual landmark.
A campus with a distinctive sculpture at the centre of its main quad, visible from the principal entry paths.
A hospital with a brightly coloured atrium visible from each ward corridor terminus.
A shopping mall with a distinctive central fountain at the convergence of the main concourses.
An office building with a unique wall-finish at each elevator lobby, so that exiting the lift gives an unambiguous orientation cue.
A historic district with named, visually distinctive buildings at the corners of each major intersection.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image supports partial extraction: distinctive features visible in the frame can be detected, but landmark *clarity* is also a function of how visible the landmark is from common vantages across the setting — a property the single image cannot fully ground. The from_2d flag is therefore set to 'partial'. The composite extractor combines (i) distinctive-feature count (objects with unique form/colour/material relative to their immediate context), (ii) signage clarity (presence, contrast, legibility of orienting signs), (iii) feature contrast against context (luminance, colour, and form contrast against the visible surround), (iv) Sorrows-Hirtle classification (visual vs structural — assessed via VLM), and (v) a VLM probe operationalised against the V2.6 indicator items. A 5-point Likert is returned with confidence. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: the underlying landmark mechanism is universal, but cultural conventions about what constitutes a navigational anchor (a religious structure, a tree, a colour-coded entry) vary modestly.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan supports much better landmark clarity extraction because landmark visibility from common vantages can be computed directly from the scan (which vantages see this distinctive feature?), and the structural-landmark dimension can be assessed via the configurational position of the candidate landmark in the local axial graph. Per-vantage data lets the extractor identify landmarks that are well-anchored from many vantages (high L30) vs landmarks visible only from one vantage (low L30 in aggregate even if locally striking). The construct itself is snapshot-temporal; learned-landmark effects (a landmark that becomes salient through experience) are out of scope.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L30 indicator block — three rating items (felt landmark-clarity; influence on choice; image-confidence) and two behavioural proxies (2AFC choice frequency; RT reliability).
scope_excludes: Cognitive landmarks of personal significance unavailable from imagery (your grandmother's old house): the construct is restricted to landmarks recoverable from the visible scene.
Legibility as a whole (L29): landmark clarity is a constituent of Lynch's legibility, not the totality.
Aesthetic distinctiveness for its own sake: a building can be aesthetically distinctive without serving as a wayfinding anchor (if it is hidden from common paths).
scope_includes: Visual landmarks (distinctive forms, colours, materials) and structural landmarks (distinctive positions in the spatial structure).
Both indoor (a unique sculpture in a lobby) and outdoor (a city's iconic spire) settings.
Cases where signage substitutes for or supplements physical landmarks — a clear, distinctive sign at a junction can serve as a wayfinding anchor.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.legibility.definition: Ease of understanding the spatial layout and routes through it.

extraction.compute_from: floor-plan-augmented isovist and axial-map analysis, with image-derived sightline-extent and decision-point clarity contributing as image-only proxies; geometry-driven composite

key_refs: Lynch, K. (1960). The image of the city. MIT Press.
Hillier, B., & Hanson, J. (1984). The social logic of space. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511597237
Hillier, B. (1996). Space is the machine: A configurational theory of architecture. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
search_terms: Lynch image of the city legibility

notes.extraction_notes: Per Hillier panel argument (Sprint 1 disposition), this construct is configurational and image-based extraction yields a degraded estimate. Augment with floor-plan when available. The construct is fundamentally about whole-system spatial structure (axial integration / convex map depth / connectivity graph). Sprint-2 panel flagged L21 vs L29 as a high-overlap pair: L21 emphasises visual-organisation observables (alignment, repetition, palette consistency); L29 emphasises spatial-topology observables (axial integration, sightline depth, decision-point clarity, connectivity). The disambiguation must hold in BN integration.

semantics.definition_long: Legibility — Lynch's (1960) fourth element of *The Image of the City* and the legibility cell of Kaplan & Kaplan's (1989) Preference Matrix — names the ease with which an inhabitant can apprehend the structure of a spatial system, anticipate routes through it, and form a stable mental map. Lynch operationalises legibility through the five elements (paths, edges, districts, nodes, landmarks); space-syntax theory (Hillier & Hanson, 1984) operationalises it through configurational measures of the axial map (integration, connectivity, depth) and the convex map. The Sprint-2 panel — following the Hillier-driven Sprint-1 disposition — treats legibility as fundamentally configurational: it is a property of the *whole spatial system*, not of any single image. A single-view image of a corridor cannot ground a defensible legibility judgment because the corridor's contribution to the building's legibility depends on its position in the global axial graph, the depth from this point to other key destinations, and the connectivity of nodes the inhabitant has not yet encountered. Image-based extraction therefore yields a degraded estimate; floor-plan augmentation is mandatory for high-confidence values.
semantics.definition_short: Ease of understanding the spatial layout and routes through it.
examples_negative: A sprawling shopping mall with multiple disconnected wings, intersecting circulation paths, no consistent landmarks, and signage that conflicts with sightlines — the negative-legibility paradigm.

A mediaeval city centre at street level with no visible spires, twisting alleys, and convex spaces that change orientation mid-block — even though such cities are often beloved, they are low-legibility in Lynch's sense.
A modernist superblock housing estate with identical building masses, repeating courtyards, and no distinguishing landmarks.
An academic building with multiple wings added across decades, each with its own numbering scheme, and corridors that change levels mid-route.
examples_positive: A small-town centre with a clear main street, a visible church spire as a landmark, a market square as a node, and short sightlines to all destinations from the central node — a Lynch-paradigm legible setting.
A modernist hospital wing with a single spine corridor, doors at regular intervals, signage at every junction, and unambiguous paths to entrance, lifts, and departments.
A grid-pattern neighbourhood with named streets, consistent block size, and visible landmarks at key intersections.
An academic building with a central atrium that serves as the primary node, from which corridors radiate to clearly-labelled departments, each corridor visible from the atrium.
A cathedral with a clear longitudinal axis, transept crossing as the dominant node, and chapels arranged symmetrically along the nave.
An airport terminal with a clear central concourse, gates radiating from numbered piers in monotonic order, and direct sightlines from the security checkpoint to all major destinations.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image alone yields a *degraded* L29 estimate — a single photograph cannot ground a defensible configurational measure. Per the Hillier panel argument (Sprint-1 disposition, applied here in Sprint-2): this construct is configurational and image-based extraction yields a degraded estimate; augment with floor-plan when available. The image-only path uses upstream observables of sightline extent (how far the eye sees down the visible paths), decision-point clarity (whether the next branching node is visible and unambiguous), and signage presence/clarity. These yield a single-view legibility proxy that the extractor MUST report with `requires_floor_plan: true` and a confidence-degraded flag. When floor-plan data is available, the extractor computes axial integration (local Rn=3), convex-map depth from the photographed vantage to canonical destinations, and the connectivity of the convex space containing the vantage; these configurational measures bin into the 5-point Likert with high confidence. The configurational_measure field is set to `connectivity` because connectivity is the most parsimonious of the three space-syntax measures for legibility purposes (integration is more apt for L41-style chance-encounter; depth is too sensitive to pruning of the axial graph). Cross-cultural variance is `medium`.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan supports much better legibility extraction than a single image because the scan supplies the local axial graph directly: doors, thresholds, and partition heights yield the convex map; sightline polygons yield isovists; the connectivity graph is computable in-scan. For scans of full configurations (a whole building or campus), the extractor can compute legibility entirely from the scan without external floor-plan data. For partial scans, the extractor MUST mark the result as bounded by scan extent and downgrade confidence. Image-derived decision-point clarity proxies remain useful as parents for the local read but should not stand alone.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L29 indicator block — three rating items (ease of finding way; ease of seeing where to go next; layout confusion reverse-coded) and two behavioural proxies (VR route-finding time/errors/path-inefficiency; junction confidence/latency).
scope_excludes: Coherence/Order (L21): coherence is the visual-organisation read of a single view; legibility is the configurational comprehensibility of the whole system.
Landmark Clarity (L30): landmark clarity is a sub-component of Lynch's legibility but is tagged separately so the BN can decompose Lynch's legibility into its constituent elements.

Single-view 'looks easy to navigate' impressions untethered from the underlying configuration — these are an unreliable proxy and should NOT be reported as L29 without floor-plan augmentation flagging.
scope_includes: Both indoor (building, complex) and outdoor (urban, campus, neighbourhood) configurations.
Cases where legibility derives from the configuration's axial integration, convex-map depth, or connectivity graph.
Cases where image-derived proxies (sightline extent, decision-point clarity) supplement floor-plan-derived configurational measures.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.mystery.definition: Promise of further information beyond the visible vantage — invitation to explore.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for partial occlusion, deflected sightlines, curving paths, gradient of light into receding regions, and indirect glimpses, read by a VLM conditioned on Kaplan-mystery indicator items

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Berlyne, D. E. (1971). Aesthetics and psychobiology. Appleton-Century-Crofts.
Kaplan, S. (1987). Aesthetics, affect, and cognition: Environmental preference from an evolutionary perspective. Environment and Behavior, 19(1), 3-32. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013916587191001

notes.extraction_notes: Berlyne's (1971) collative-variable framing is preserved: mystery can drive approach (curiosity, exploration) or withdrawal (uncertainty, hazard avoidance) depending on the rest of the affective context. Sprint-3 panel may want to consider whether mystery should be modelled with an interaction term against L33/L34 (prospect/refuge) and L24 (valence).

semantics.definition_long: Mystery — in the Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) sense — names the degree to which a scene promises information beyond what is presently visible, such that an observer would learn more by moving deeper into it. It is the inferred-environment cell of the Preference Matrix, paired with legibility as the higher-order pair, and is operationalised by partial occlusion (a wall edge that hides part of a room beyond), deflected sightlines (a path that curves out of view), gradients of light into receding regions, and the indirect glimpse (something half-seen through a doorway or screen). The construct overlaps theoretically with Berlyne's (1971) collative variables — particularly novelty and complexity — but mystery is narrower: it is specifically the structural promise of *deferred* information, not the immediate complexity of what is visible. Berlyne's framework also reminds us that mystery is a 'collative' variable in the sense that it can produce either approach (curiosity-driven exploration) or withdrawal (uncertainty-induced avoidance), depending on the observer's state and the rest of the affective context — hence the panel's `valence_polarity: mixed` assignment.
semantics.definition_short: Promise of further information beyond the visible vantage — invitation to explore.
examples_negative: A single-room studio apartment in which every part of the room is visible from the entry — no occlusion, no deflection, no promise of more.
An open-plan office whose ceiling is uniform and whose lighting makes every workstation equally visible — no gradient into depth.

A wide-open agricultural field photographed from a tractor seat: scenically rich but perfectly flat in the information-promise sense, because the eye sees as far as the horizon and there is nothing 'beyond'.
A long straight corridor with no doors and a clearly visible terminal wall — the path is unambiguous and there is nothing hidden.
examples_positive: A garden path that curves behind a hedgerow, so that the eye follows it to the bend and is then invited to follow the body further.
A library reading room whose far wall has a partially open doorway through which one glimpses the spines of more shelves — a hint of depth not yet read.
