cognitive.restorativeness.definition: Restorativeness (ART): the integrated appraised affordance of the depicted space for psychological restoration per Kaplan & Kaplan's Attention Restoration Theory.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + VLM prompt encoding the four ART components (being-away, fascination, coherence, compatibility) + whole-scene cues (greenery, daylight, openness, calm visual field)

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, S. (1995). The restorative benefits of nature: Toward an integrative framework. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 15(3), 169-182. https://doi.org/10.1016/0272-4944(95)90001-2

Hartig, T., Korpela, K., Evans, G. W., & Garling, T. (1997). A measure of restorative quality in environments. Scandinavian Housing and Planning Research, 14(4), 175-194. https://doi.org/10.1080/02815739708730435

notes.bn_notes: **Sprint-3 candidate factor-structure decomposition**: the panel disposition flagged a Kaplan-vs-Russell disagreement on whether L17 should be retained as an integrated factor (Kaplan's preference, consistent with the Hartig et al. 1997 PRS scoring tradition) or decomposed into the four sub-factors (Russell's preference, which would replace L17 with a vector of four sub-scores L18/L19/(coherence)/L20). This is logged as a Sprint-3 construct-contest item; the current Sprint-2 implementation retains L17 as integrated *and* L18/L19/L20 as separate sub-factors, accepting some redundancy until the Sprint-3 vote. The fourth ART component, *coherence*, is currently absorbed into L17 and not a separate latent; if Russell's decomposition wins the vote, an L17a coherence latent will be cut in v0.2.11. | Structured-factor parent over L18 (being-away), L19 (soft fascination), L20 (compatibility), and L17_extent (cognitive.extent). Reorganisation ratified in Sprint 3 contest #1 disposition (2026-04-29); Russell dissent preserved — see docs/sprint3_contest1_L17_resolution.md §5.
notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 1 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 5). L17 is the integrated outcome of the four ART components L17/L18/L19/L20; per the Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) framework, restorativeness emerges from the joint affordance of being-away, fascination, coherence, and compatibility. The L17 extractor emphasises *whole-scene* cues that integrate over the four components (greenery presence, daylight, openness, calm visual field) and is intentionally upstream-distinct from L19 (Soft Fascination) which emphasises *attentional capture* cues - this enforces the panel-mandated <50% upstream overlap (Spohn audit).

semantics.definition_long: The integrated appraised affordance of the depicted space for psychological restoration per Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) Attention Restoration Theory (ART). The construct integrates four sub-components: being-away (psychological distance from demands; L18), fascination (effortless attention capture; L19), coherence (the scene's extent and self-consistency), and compatibility (fit between space and intended activity; L20). Operationalised at the integrated level through whole-scene cues that predict the joint presence of all four: greenery and natural elements (which carry being-away and fascination); daylight (which carries coherence and compatibility); openness with retreat nooks (which carries being-away and compatibility); and a visually calm field (which carries coherence). Restorativeness is the canonical construct measured by the Hartig, Korpela, Evans, and Garling (1997) Perceived Restorativeness Scale (PRS); downstream effects include stress recovery and attention restoration (Kaplan, 1995).
semantics.definition_short: Being-away, fascination, coherence, compatibility.
examples_negative: A fluorescent-lit open-plan office with no daylight, no greenery, hard surfaces, and visible workstations; whole-scene cues converge on the *opposite* of restorativeness.
A subway platform with hard surfaces, no daylight, and visible signage and circulation; the integrated scene cues neither being-away nor compatibility for rest.
A retail showroom with bright key lighting, dense product display, and no soft surfaces; cognitively demanding rather than restorative.
An emergency-room waiting area with hard plastic chairs, fluorescent lighting, and no view; whole-scene cues are anti-restorative.
examples_positive: A library reading nook with a large window onto a green courtyard, a comfortable upholstered chair set back into an alcove, daylight, and quiet; all four ART components are cued by whole-scene features.
A wooded park bench beside a slow-moving stream, sunlight filtering through canopy, no traffic visible; the integrated scene cues being-away, fascination, coherence, and compatibility for rest.
A residential window seat overlooking a garden, mid-morning daylight, a book and a mug on the side surface; all four ART affordances are present in the integrated whole-scene read.
A campus quad with mature trees, dappled sunlight, and a vacant bench facing the green; the calm-greenery-daylight ensemble cues high restorativeness.
A cafe corner with a green-wall feature, soft daylight from a clerestory, an unoccupied two-person nook with cushions; the integrated whole-scene affordance is high.
A spa relaxation lounge with a forest view, low warm lighting, and reclining chairs; explicit institutional staging of the ART affordances.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor uses a VLM prompted with the four ART components and whole-scene cues: (a) `scene.greenery_presence` (vegetation segmented and quantified as scene-area fraction), (b) `lighting.daylight_present` (natural-versus-artificial light classification), (c) `geometry.openness_index` (visible-floor and aperture geometry), (d) `scene.calm_visual_field_indicator` (low visual clutter, low chromatic variance, low motion-cue density), and (e) `scene.retreat_nook_present` (alcove or corner detection). The Likert is composed by the VLM scoring the integrated affordance against the Hartig et al. (1997) PRS items. Per the Spohn 50%-overlap audit mandate, L17's parent-tag set is intentionally distinct from L19's: L17 emphasises *whole-scene* cues (greenery, daylight, openness, calm visual field, retreat) while L19 emphasises *attentional-capture* cues (motion, water-feature, fractal patterns). The V2.6 cue list 'Nature views; retreat nooks; calm visual field' maps directly onto these whole-scene cues. Confidence is high (Image_Judgability = 5).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR immersion' enrichment) the extractor can render the scene in immersive view and apply the same whole-scene VLM scoring with viewpoint variation; the openness index and retreat-nook detection are direct geometric measurements rather than monocular estimates, and daylight can be modelled per time-of-day with the lighting model. VR-immersion empirical literature (Browning et al. 2014 on biophilic VR; Pretsch et al. 2020 on virtual restoration) shows that VR-rendered restorativeness scores correlate with PRS scores from real spaces, supporting transfer of the 2D-calibrated extractor to the 3D substrate. Confidence is materially higher than 2D, particularly for distinguishing scenes whose 2D appearance is restorative but whose 3D acoustic or thermal qualities undercut the affordance.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-REST-1 (Restorativeness): rating items 'how restorative right now', 'feel away from demands', 'effortlessly relax attention'; behavioural proxies 'pre/post ANT or CPT change' and 'choice after fatigue 2AFC'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *restoration* superfactor and integrates over L18 (Being-Away), L19 (Soft Fascination), and L20 (Compatibility) per Kaplan and Kaplan (1989).
scope_excludes: Pure being-away (escape from demands without other ART components) - that is L18 (Being-Away), a sub-factor; L17 is the *integrated* affordance.
Pure soft fascination (attentional capture without being-away or compatibility) - that is L19 (Soft Fascination), a sub-factor; L17 integrates over all four components.
Spaces that are stimulating but not restorative (e.g., a vibrant marketplace) - high arousal without the calm-coherence component is excitement, not restoration.
scope_includes: Spaces whose whole-scene appearance integrates the four ART components - greenery, daylight, openness with retreat, and a calm visual field.
Spaces whose institutional or vernacular function is restoration (gardens, parks, reading rooms, residential nooks, spa lounges) and whose visible cues match the function.
Spaces depicting natural elements (water, vegetation, sky) whose joint presence is the strongest single predictor of perceived restorativeness per Kaplan (1995).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.rule_tightness.definition: Norm inference about whether the depicted space is strict or permissive.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + prohibitive-signage detection + formality cue analysis + barrier-density estimation + uniform / dress-code indicators

key_refs: Gelfand, M. J., Raver, J. L., Nishii, L., Leslie, L. M., Lun, J., Lim, B. C., … Yamaguchi, S. (2011). Differences between tight and loose cultures: A 33-nation study. Science, 332(6033), 1100–1104. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1197754
Goffman, E. (1959). The presentation of self in everyday life. Anchor.

Cialdini, R. B., Reno, R. R., & Kallgren, C. A. (1990). A focus theory of normative conduct: Recycling the concept of norms to reduce littering in public places. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 58(6), 1015–1026.
Bitner, M. J. (1992). Servicescapes: The impact of physical surroundings on customers and employees. Journal of Marketing, 56(2), 57–71. https://doi.org/10.1177/002224299205600205

notes.bn_notes: Note the Gelfand et al. (2011) tight/loose construct is fundamentally cultural-level; rule tightness here is a *scene-level* perception of how strict the local norms appear. Image_Judgability_1to5 = 3 because rule tightness rests heavily on cultural priors that an image alone underdetermines.
notes.extraction_notes: Composite extractor combines prohibitive-signage detection with a VLM scoring the V2.7 generic rating items.

semantics.definition_long: The viewer's inference about whether the depicted space is governed by strict or permissive norms — whether what one does, says, wears, and touches in this space will be regulated by enforceable expectations or by loose tolerance. The construct draws on Gelfand et al.'s (2011) cross-national tight/loose taxonomy, on Goffman's (1959) frontstage/backstage analysis of behavioural region, and on Cialdini, Reno, and Kallgren's (1990) focus theory of normative conduct, in which the visual salience of normative cues controls their behavioural impact. Bitner's (1992) servicescape framework empirically grounds the construct in commercial and institutional environments: prohibitive signage, formal layout, dress-code indicators, and physical barriers jointly cue the strength of the local normative regime.
semantics.definition_short: Rule Tightness — norm inference: strict vs permissive.
examples_negative: A high-end restaurant with formal table settings — cues formality but the rule structure may still be permissive within the formal frame.
An empty corporate lobby — reads formal but has no enforced rules visible.
A casual cafe with handwritten chalkboard menus — looks loose but may have strict implicit norms (no laptops past 11am, etc.).
A space with prohibitive signage that is faded or peeling — nominally strict but the maintenance state suggests weak enforcement.
examples_positive: To what extent does this space feel like: Rule Tightness?
How strongly would this appraisal influence your choice to be here?
How confident are you in this judgment from the image alone?
Choice frequency in repeated 2AFC trials against matched alternatives.
Response-time consistency across repetitions (reliability).
A government building lobby with multiple 'No food or drink', 'No mobile phones', and 'Photo ID required' signs flanked by uniformed security personnel and metal detectors.
A formal courtroom with fixed seating, prominent rule signage, dress-code indicators, and visible barriers between public and bench.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `wayfinding.prohibitive_signage` (count and prominence of prohibitive signs: "no", "do not", regulatory iconography), `scene.formality_cue` (formality-level inferred from finishes, seating type, and layout), `affordance.barrier_density` (count of physical barriers: stanchions, ropes, gates), `object.uniform_indicator` (presence of persons in uniform or institutional dress), and `scene.layout_formality` (rigid vs. casual layout). The V2.7 cue list "prohibitive signage; formal layouts; barriers" maps directly onto prohibitive_signage, layout_formality, and barrier_density. The 2D extractor returns `partial` because rule strictness rests on cultural priors not fully reconstructible from the image; a composite extractor combines the cues with a VLM scoring the V2.7 rating items.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can incorporate scripted-norm scenarios per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR with scripted norms" — e.g., simulated NPCs that enforce or fail to enforce posted rules, providing ground-truth calibration of the cue-to-enforcement mapping. The 3D substrate also exposes barrier geometry (height, gap-spacing) explicitly rather than as 2D depth-ambiguous fragments. Note: the cross-cultural variance (medium per panel default) implies that the same physical cue set can index different rule-tightness levels across populations, and downstream model trainers should treat the L09 output as conditional on a cultural prior.
scope_excludes: Surveillance pressure — that's L04 (cognitive.surveillance_pressure), an affective response to monitoring, not a normative inference.
Predictability of physical conditions — that's L08 (cognitive.predictability), a stability not a normativity construct.
Aesthetic formality (high-end finishes) — a surface aesthetic distinct from rule strictness.
scope_includes: Inferred strictness or permissiveness of behavioural norms in the depicted space.
Visible enforcement infrastructure: prohibitive signage, barriers, uniformed monitors.
Formality cues (layout regularity, finish formality, dress-code indicators) that index regulatory tightness.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.social_exposure.definition: Social Exposure: the appraised degree to which an occupant of the depicted space would feel on display to others.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + seat-position estimation + room-centroid geometry + lighting-intensity estimate at seat + surrounding-observer count

key_refs: Goffman, E. (1963). Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings. Free Press.
Goffman, E. (1959). The presentation of self in everyday life. Anchor Books.
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.

notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 2 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 4). Social Exposure is grounded in Goffman's (1963; 1959) front-region/back-region distinction: a seat in a front region is more exposed than one in a back region, with downstream effects on self-consciousness and anxiety. The construct is the *visual* inverse of L11 (Acoustic Privacy) - both index public-versus-private affordances but along different sensory axes.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised degree to which an occupant of the depicted space would feel visually on display to others - the 'all eyes could be on me' quality of the seat or position. Operationalised through cues that predict visual exposure: central seating versus peripheral seating, the visibility isovist of the seat (how much of the room can see the occupant), the intensity of key lighting falling on the seat (stage-quality versus diffuse-lit), and the presence and count of plausible observers in the surrounding area. The construct is grounded in Goffman's (1963; 1959) distinction between front and back regions: a front-region seat affords audience-quality visibility while a back-region seat affords backstage anonymity. Downstream consequences include self-consciousness and social anxiety.
semantics.definition_short: Feeling on display.
examples_negative: A back-corner armchair against two walls in a residential reading nook - low isovist, no observers, classic back-region seat.
A library carrel facing a wall with side panels obscuring lateral view - the occupant is structurally invisible to others.
A booth seat with high upholstered backs and side wings on three sides - back-region quality despite the occupant being in a busy restaurant.
A workstation tucked behind a tall partition in an open-plan office - the partition shields the occupant from the rest of the floor.
examples_positive: A high-top counter seat at the front window of a cafe facing both the interior of the cafe and the sidewalk - the seat is visible from inside and out, two distinct potential audiences.
A central island seat in an open-plan library, visible from all four sides and lit by an overhead pendant; the lighting plus centrality cue maximum exposure.
A spotlit chair on a small raised dais in a hotel lobby - the staging cue is overt and the chair is unambiguously a front-region seat.
A central booth in a busy restaurant facing the entrance; arrivals and existing diners both have direct sightlines to the occupant.
A meeting-room hot-seat at the head of a long table with windows on three sides and the room's only door behind the seat - visibility from every direction.
A receptionist-desk seat facing a lobby with multiple seating clusters; every waiting visitor has a clear sightline to the receptionist.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor consumes upstream observables: (a) `geometry.seat_centrality` (estimated distance from seat centroid to room centroid normalised by room radius), (b) `geometry.visibility_isovist_at_seat` (a 2D approximation of the isovist from the seat position, estimated from visible-floor and aperture geometry), (c) `lighting.key_light_intensity_at_seat` (relative key-light fraction at the seat versus the room average), (d) `scene.surrounding_observer_count` (count of plausible observer positions or persons within sightline), and (e) `geometry.seat_back_to_wall_or_open` (whether the seat-back is against a wall, which materially reduces exposure). The Likert is composed via weighted sum, with centrality and isovist receiving the largest weights per Goffman (1963); strong key lighting amplifies a moderate centrality score. The V2.6 cue list 'Central seating; high visibility; strong key lighting' maps directly onto these features. Confidence is reduced when seat geometry or lighting cannot be cleanly disambiguated from image content alone.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR audience simulation' enrichment) the visibility isovist at the seat is computable directly from the 3D geometry, and key-light intensity is a metric quantity from the lighting model. Surrounding-observer counts can be sampled across plausible occupancy distributions. Seat centrality is a metric distance, not an estimate. As with the privacy_attention latents, 3D substrate provides materially higher confidence for L15 than 2D, particularly for distinguishing scenes that look central but have visual occluders, and scenes that look peripheral but have unobstructed long sightlines.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CROWD-2 (Social Exposure): rating items 'feels like social exposure', 'influence on choice', 'confidence from image'; behavioural proxies '2AFC choice frequency' and 'response-time consistency'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *crowding/density* superfactor alongside L14 (Crowding Pressure) and L16 (Resource Scarcity).
scope_excludes: Crowding-induced discomfort - that is L14 (Crowding Pressure); L15 is specifically the *visibility* dimension and applies even when overall density is low.
Acoustic exposure (being overheard) - that is the inverse of L11 (Acoustic Privacy); L15 concerns *visual* exposure only.

Spaces where the occupant is an intentional performer (stage actor, lecturer) - L15 measures the *space's* exposure affordance, not the occupant's role; exposure may be high but the construct of 'feeling self-conscious' is moderated by role.
scope_includes: Seats whose position and lighting cue front-region 'on display' visibility - central position, high isovist, focal lighting, and / or a plausible audience of surrounding observers.
Spaces that include a clear locus-of-attention (stage, dais, focal counter) such that occupying the seat puts the body in the social spotlight.
Layouts where multiple distinct observer groups share sightlines to the seat (e.g., a window seat visible from inside and outside).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.soft_fascination.definition: Soft Fascination: the appraised affordance for effortless, non-demanding attention capture per Kaplan & Kaplan's ART.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + VLM prompt encoding soft-fascination ART component + water-feature detection + motion-cue detection + fractal-pattern texture analysis

key_refs: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
Kaplan, S. (1995). The restorative benefits of nature: Toward an integrative framework. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 15(3), 169-182. https://doi.org/10.1016/0272-4944(95)90001-2
Berman, M. G., Jonides, J., & Kaplan, S. (2008). The cognitive benefits of interacting with nature. Psychological Science, 19(12), 1207-1212. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02225.x

Hagerhall, C. M., Purcell, T., & Taylor, R. (2004). Fractal dimension of landscape silhouette outlines as a predictor of landscape preference. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 24(2), 247-255. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvp.2003.12.004

notes.bn_notes: Per the panel-mandated Spohn 50%-overlap audit, L19's parent-tag set is intentionally distinct from L17's: L19 emphasises *attentional-capture* cues (water-feature presence, gentle motion, fractal-pattern strength, natural-pattern density) while L17 emphasises *whole-scene* cues (greenery presence, daylight, openness, calm visual field). The two latents are within-cluster siblings under the restoration subdomain but their upstream observable sets share less than 50% overlap, satisfying Spohn's <50% rule for distinguishability. This is the core BN-design content for L19. | Component of L17 (cognitive.restorativeness). Per Sprint 3 contest #1 structured-factor reorganisation; the parent-child relationship is a BN-structural commitment, not an extraction-time merger — independent extractor stubs retained.
notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 2 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 4). L19 is the *soft fascination* sub-component of ART per Kaplan and Kaplan (1989), distinct from L17 (integrated Restorativeness) and from siblings L18 (Being-Away) and L20 (Compatibility). The Berman, Jonides, and Kaplan (2008) empirical literature on nature-based attention restoration provides the operational ground for the construct: fractal-pattern density, gentle motion (water, leaves), and natural complexity at sub-attention-demanding levels are the canonical cues. Temporal window is *sustained* because soft fascination is constitutively a sustained involuntary-attention process, not a snapshot stimulus.

semantics.definition_long: The appraised affordance for effortless, non-demanding attention capture per Kaplan and Kaplan's (1989) Attention Restoration Theory. Soft fascination is involuntary attention drawn to gentle, sub-demanding stimuli - moving water, swaying leaves, fractal natural patterns, drifting clouds - that holds the mind without requiring directed effort, allowing the directed-attention system to recover. Operationalised through cues that predict effortless attentional capture: water-feature presence (fountains, streams, ponds); gentle motion (leaves in wind, slow water flow); fractal-pattern texture density (Hagerhall, Purcell, & Taylor, 2004, on fractal dimension as a preference predictor); and gentle complexity at the sub-demanding-attention threshold. The construct is operationalised in the Berman, Jonides, and Kaplan (2008) empirical attention-restoration literature, which demonstrates that brief exposure to nature stimuli rich in soft-fascination cues produces measurable directed-attention recovery on cognitive tasks.
semantics.definition_short: Effortless attention capture (non-demanding).
examples_negative: A blank white wall with no visual variation - no fascination cue at all.
A high-contrast urban scene with billboards, signage, and traffic - the visual environment captures attention but in an effortful, demanding way; this is *hard* fascination, not soft.
A cluttered storage room with random object piles - complexity is present but not patterned and not gentle; cognitively demanding rather than restorative.
An interior with rapidly-flashing screens - motion is present but it is demanding rather than gentle motion.
examples_positive: A stream-side bench beside a slow-flowing brook with sun-dappled water - the moving-water and dappled-light cues are canonical soft-fascination stimuli (Berman et al., 2008).
A garden view with leaves moving gently in wind, mid-distance scale - the slow motion and the fractal foliage texture together cue effortless attention capture.
An interior with a wall-mounted aquarium filled with slowly-swimming fish; the gentle-motion cue is an explicit institutional staging of soft fascination.
A large window onto a snowfall, slow drifting flakes against a green-conifer backdrop - the gentle motion and natural pattern density are both high.
A courtyard fountain with falling water, set in a planted enclosure; the water-feature plus fractal-vegetation cues converge.

A reading-room view of a mature tree with leaves in afternoon sun, the leaf-pattern's fractal dimension is high (Hagerhall, Purcell, & Taylor, 2004) and the slow leaf motion adds the involuntary-attention cue.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor uses a VLM prompted with the soft-fascination ART component, plus structured upstream observables: (a) `scene.water_feature_present` (fountain, stream, pond, aquarium detection), (b) `scene.gentle_motion_indicator` (motion-cue inference from blur, fluid surfaces, vegetation in wind - challenging from a static image but possible from motion-blur and deformation cues), (c) `texture.fractal_pattern_strength` (computed from local fractal-dimension estimates over textured regions, calibrated to Hagerhall, Purcell, and Taylor's 2004 D = 1.3-1.5 optimum), (d) `scene.natural_pattern_density`, and (e) `scene.gentle_complexity_index`. The Likert is composed by the VLM against the Berman, Jonides, and Kaplan (2008) attention-restoration empirical operationalisation. Per the Spohn 50%-overlap audit mandate, L19's parent-tag set is intentionally distinct from L17's; L19 emphasises attentional-capture cues (motion, water, fractal patterns) while L17 emphasises whole-scene cues. Confidence is moderate (Image_Judgability = 4) because gentle-motion inference from a static image is fragile; multi-frame video would substantially improve confidence.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR multimodal add-ons' enrichment) the extractor can render the scene with motion (water flow, leaf animation, drifting cloud) and directly observe the gentle-motion cue rather than inferring it from static image features; multimodal add-ons (audio rendering of water sound, haptic breeze) can further amplify the soft-fascination affordance. Fractal-dimension estimates over rendered textures are direct metric quantities. The Berman, Jonides, and Kaplan (2008) attention-restoration empirical literature provides validation for the transfer of 2D-calibrated soft-fascination scores to 3D-rendered scenes; subsequent VR-restoration work has extended the result. Confidence is materially higher than 2D for L19 specifically, because the motion-cue is the construct's defining feature and is directly observable in 3D / video substrates.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-REST-3 (Soft Fascination): rating items 'feels like soft fascination', 'influence on choice', 'confidence from image'; behavioural proxies '2AFC choice frequency' and 'response-time consistency'.
Loads as a sub-factor of the broader V2.6 *restoration* superfactor; one of four ART components per Kaplan and Kaplan (1989) and validated empirically by Berman, Jonides, and Kaplan (2008).
scope_excludes: Integrated restorativeness (the joint outcome of all four ART components) - that is L17 (Restorativeness); L19 is one sub-component intentionally narrower than the integrated outcome.
Hard fascination (effortful attention capture by demanding stimuli - billboards, action, alarms) - L19 is specifically *soft* fascination at the sub-demand threshold.
Visually arousing content that drives directed attention rather than restoring it - L19 affords directed-attention *recovery*, not deployment.
scope_includes: Spaces showing gentle moving stimuli (water, leaves, smoke, drifting cloud) at slow rates that capture involuntary attention without demanding it.
Spaces showing natural fractal patterns at moderate fractal dimension (Hagerhall et al., 2004 report optimal preference around D = 1.3-1.5) - foliage textures, branching patterns, ripple textures.
Spaces showing 'gentle complexity' - varied but coherent visual content that holds attention effortlessly per Kaplan (1995).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.surveillance_pressure.definition: Feeling watched or judged in the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + camera-instance detection + open-plan exposure index + lighting pattern analysis + gaze-line geometry

key_refs: Foucault, M. (1977). Discipline and punish: The birth of the prison (A. Sheridan, Trans.). Pantheon Books.
Zajonc, R. B. (1965). Social facilitation. Science, 149(3681), 269–274. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.149.3681.269
Ulrich, R. S. (1991). Effects of interior design on wellness: Theory and recent scientific research. Journal of Health Care Interior Design, 3, 97–109.
Buchanan, T. W., Bagley, S. L., Stansfield, R. B., & Preston, S. D. (2012). The empathic, physiological resonance of stress. Social Neuroscience, 7(2), 191–201.

notes.bn_notes: Distinct from cognitive.visibility_control (L03) along the prospect-refuge axis: surveillance pressure is the affective response to being watched (refuge failure); visibility control is the prospect-positive complement. A high-surveillance space is the dual of a low-visibility-control space, but the latents are kept separate because surveillance pressure carries social-evaluative content (self-consciousness, judgement) absent from L03's neutral monitoring asymmetry.
notes.extraction_notes: VLM prompted with the V2.7 indicator items ("To what extent would you feel watched or evaluated\u2026", "How self-conscious would you feel\u2026", "How comfortable would you be working here without being judged\u2026") returns a Likert 0–4.

semantics.definition_long: The viewer's anticipated experience of being watched, evaluated, or judged in the depicted space, drawing theoretically on Foucault's (1977) panopticon analysis of architectural surveillance and on Zajonc's (1965) social-facilitation work showing that the mere presence (or implication) of an audience changes performance and arousal. Surveillance pressure is the affective complement of low visibility control (L03): when the depicted space exposes the viewer to many incoming sightlines without offering refuge, the viewer anticipates social-evaluative scrutiny and reports self-consciousness and discomfort. Ulrich (1991) noted that healthcare environments with conspicuous monitoring infrastructure systematically elevate patient stress beyond what the medical procedures themselves require.
semantics.definition_short: Surveillance Pressure — feeling watched/judged.
examples_negative: An empty room with no cameras and no windows — zero surveillance cues; pressure is at floor.
A small alcove off a busier room — visible from the main room but tucked away enough that one is not the focus; some pressure but moderated by partial refuge.
An open plaza in daylight — high exposure but not directional; without specific gaze cues this is exposure rather than surveillance.
A space that is dimly lit and concealed — the viewer may feel threatened (L01) but not specifically watched.
examples_positive: To what extent would you feel watched or evaluated here?
How self-conscious would you feel in this space?
How comfortable would you be working here without being judged? (reverse-coded)
Seat-choice: peripheral/refuge vs exposed central seating (forced choice).
Attention to surveillance cues: dwell on cameras/signage (click/gaze optional).
An open-plan office central desk with multiple visible CCTV cameras on the ceiling and clear sightlines from surrounding workstations.
A spotlit raised stage at the front of an empty auditorium with rows of seats facing it.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `object.camera_presence` (count and salience of CCTV / monitoring devices), `scene.openplan_exposure` (degree to which the viewpoint is centrally exposed to peripheral sightlines), `lighting.spotlight_pattern` (spotlight or stage-lighting cues that focus attention on a position), `scene.gaze_lines` (oriented faces or workstations facing the viewpoint), and `scene.elevated_platform_focus` (whether the viewpoint sits on a stage / podium). The V2.7 cue list "cameras; open-plan exposure; spotlight-like lighting" maps directly onto camera_presence, openplan_exposure, and spotlight_pattern. The 2D extractor returns `partial` because gaze-line and exposure assessments require a 3D understanding of which surrounding regions can in fact see the viewpoint; a VLM prompted with the three V2.7 rating items aggregates the cues into the appraisal latent with confidence.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can compute explicit incoming-isovist coverage of the implied viewpoint — the union of regions from which the viewpoint is directly visible — normalised by total occupiable area. Per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR with other agents; gaze cues" — dynamic-agent presence and their head/gaze orientations toward the viewpoint provide direct measurement of social attention. Camera detections in 3D can additionally be represented as oriented sensor cones, allowing the extractor to weight cameras by their actual coverage of the viewpoint rather than by mere presence. Spotlight-pattern cues become explicit lumen-distribution maps in 3D, allowing the extractor to identify whether the viewpoint sits inside a high-illuminance focal pool while the surroundings are darker.
scope_excludes: Personal-attack threat — that's L01 (cognitive.perceived_threat), a danger appraisal not a social-evaluative one.
Visual privacy / refuge — that's L10 (cognitive.visual_privacy), the inverse of this construct's exposure component.
General openness or open-plan layout — a layout descriptor, not the felt-surveillance appraisal.
scope_includes: Anticipated experience of being watched, observed, or scrutinised in the depicted space.
Self-consciousness or evaluation apprehension triggered by the depicted scene.
Sensitivity to monitoring infrastructure (cameras, observation windows, supervision desks).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.territorial_support.definition: Territorial Support: the appraised affordance for an individual to claim and personalise micro-territory within the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + furniture-instance segmentation + boundary-feature detection (armrests, partitions) + personalisation-artefact detection (photos, plants, mugs, posters) + storage-feature detection

key_refs: Altman, I. (1975). The environment and social behavior: Privacy, personal space, territory, crowding. Brooks/Cole.
Brown, G., Lawrence, T. B., & Robinson, S. L. (2005). Territoriality in organizations. Academy of Management Review, 30(3), 577-594. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2005.17293710
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.

Sundstrom, E., Burt, R. E., & Kamp, D. (1980). Privacy at work: Architectural correlates of job satisfaction and job performance. Academy of Management Journal, 23(1), 101-117. https://doi.org/10.2307/255498

notes.extraction_notes: V2.6 Tier 2 (Image_Judgability_1to5 = 4). Cross-cultural variance is high per Hall (1966, ch. 10): the meaning of 'individual claimable territory' is sharply divergent across cultures (e.g., Japanese open-plan tradition versus North American assigned-desk norm) and the extractor must surface a cultural-prior caveat in its confidence interval. The construct is the *individual* analog of the Sprint-1 group-territoriality latent (L55).

semantics.definition_long: The appraised affordance for an individual to claim and personalise a defined micro-territory within the depicted space - a desk, chair, alcove, or other unit clearly bounded enough that an occupant can mark it as 'mine' for the duration of an activity. Operationalised through cues that predict claimability: defined desk units (assigned, not hot-desk), boundaries that visually delimit one person's zone from another's (armrests, partitions, side panels), evidence of personalisation (photos, plants, mugs, posters, customised arrangements), and individual storage (drawers, lockers, shelves). The construct is grounded in Altman's (1975) tripartite distinction between primary, secondary, and public territories - L13 measures the primary-territory affordance specifically - and in Brown, Lawrence, and Robinson's (2005) account of organisational territoriality. Cross-cultural variance is high: Hall (1966, ch. 10) documents that the strength and form of territorial marking varies substantially across cultures, and L13 must be interpreted with explicit cultural prior. L13 is the *individual* analog of the Sprint-1 group-territoriality latent (L55, group_territoriality).
semantics.definition_short: Ability to claim micro-territory.
examples_negative: A hot-desking bench with no partitions, no assigned-seat markers, and no personalisation artefacts - any worker may sit anywhere; territorial support is structurally absent.
A row of identical airport waiting chairs with no armrests between them and no surface for personal artefacts - the seating is public-territory only.
A cafe community table where six strangers sit shoulder-to-shoulder with no per-seat boundaries and no per-person surface; the layout cues anti-territorial sharing.
An open-floor agile-team space with movable furniture and no fixed associations between people and seats; the space is structurally anti-territorial in keeping with the workflow philosophy.
examples_positive: An assigned office workstation with a name plate, family photos pinned to the partition, a personal mug on the desk, and a closed drawer pedestal - all four cue families converge on strong individual territorial support.
An armchair in a residential reading nook beside a side-table with personal items (eyeglasses, half-read book, lamp); the bounded chair and adjacent surface together cue a claimed zone.

A library carrel with side panels reaching to head height when seated, a power outlet, a desk lamp, and a personal coat draped over the back of the chair; the carrel's defined boundaries plus visible personalisation cue strong claimability.

A coffee-shop banquette corner with a clearly delimited table, the occupant's laptop and bag deployed across the surface; the bounded corner and the deployed personal artefacts cue temporary primary-territory claim.
A studio cubicle with full-height side partitions, individually-controlled desk lamp, and a pinboard of personal images; high partition height plus personalisation cues maximum.
A residential desk with a chair tucked into a defined alcove, family photos, and a stack of personal books and mail; the alcove geometry plus personalisation cue clear primary territory.
A reserved-seat tag on a large reading-room chair with a hold-card showing the user's name and time-block; the explicit reservation cue is strong evidence of claimable territory.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From a 2D image the extractor consumes upstream observables: (a) `furniture.assigned_desk_present` (does the desk show signs of fixed assignment versus hot-desking arrangement), (b) `furniture.armrest_or_boundary_present` plus per-individual partition heights, (c) `surface.personalisation_artefact_present` (photo frames, plants, mugs, customisation - segmented and counted), and (d) `furniture.individual_storage_present` (drawer pedestals, lockers, shelves). The Likert is composed by weighted sum across these cue families; per Brown, Lawrence, and Robinson (2005), personalisation artefacts are the single strongest behavioural marker of territoriality and so receive the largest weight. The V2.6 cue list 'Defined desks; armrests; boundaries; personalizable surfaces' is operationalised conjunctively: each cue family contributes independently and a high score requires several cue families to be present. **Cross-cultural-variance warning (Hall, 1966, ch. 10)**: the extractor's calibration is largely Western-office-derived; it will systematically misread (a) Japanese open-plan offices where personalisation is culturally suppressed despite strong implicit territorial assignment, and (b) Mediterranean / Latin-American workplaces where shared territories are normative. The extractor MUST surface this caveat in its confidence interval.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate (V2.6 'VR claiming behavior' enrichment) territorial-claim behaviour is observable directly: place an avatar in the scene and observe whether the avatar deploys personal artefacts on the surface, whether the surface affords the deployment (desk depth, drawer presence), and whether the boundaries (partitions, armrests) actually shield the deployment from neighbours. Metric measurements - desk area, partition height, individual storage volume - are direct rather than monocular. Personalisation-artefact detection is more reliable in 3D because viewpoint can be varied to disambiguate occlusion. As with the other privacy_attention latents, 3D confidence is materially higher than 2D for this construct.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-PRIV-2 (Territorial Support): rating items 'feels like territorial support', 'influence on choice', 'confidence from image'; behavioural proxies '2AFC choice frequency' and 'response-time consistency'.
Loads on the broader V2.6 *privacy/attention* superfactor alongside L11 (Acoustic Privacy) and L12 (Interruption Likelihood).
scope_excludes: Group-claimable territory (sofas, group rooms) - this is L55 (Sprint-1 group_territoriality), the group analog of L13.
Public-territory spaces with no individual claimability (hot-desks, transit benches, cafe community tables) - these score 0 on L13.
Spaces whose 'personalisation' is institutional decoration rather than individual marking (e.g., a hotel lobby with art on the walls) - L13 specifically tracks *individual* claimability cues.
scope_includes: Spaces with defined per-person furniture units (assigned desks, individual carrels, personal armchairs) that visibly afford claimability over an activity duration.
Spaces showing personalisation artefacts (photos, plants, mugs, customisations) that signal a current occupant has claimed and marked the territory.
Spaces with per-individual boundaries (armrests, partitions, side panels) that delimit one person's zone from neighbours.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.valence_potential.definition: Pleasant/unpleasant tone the scene is likely to elicit.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for warm/cool palette, naturalness, cleanliness vs grime, daylight presence, organic vs harsh form, read by a VLM conditioned on Russell's circumplex and the SAM-pleasure item

key_refs: Russell, J. A. (1980). A circumplex model of affect. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 39(6), 1161-1178. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0077714
Russell, J. A., & Mehrabian, A. (1977). Evidence for a three-factor theory of emotions. Journal of Research in Personality, 11(3), 273-294. https://doi.org/10.1016/0092-6566(77)90037-X

Bradley, M. M., & Lang, P. J. (1994). Measuring emotion: The Self-Assessment Manikin and the Semantic Differential. Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry, 25(1), 49-59. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7916(94)90063-9

notes.extraction_notes: Bi-polar construct: the 0-4 Likert is anchored at 2 = neutral; values <2 indicate unpleasant, values >2 indicate pleasant. The `inverse_of` field is null because L24 is internally bi-polar — there is no separate 'unpleasantness' latent; the negative end of L24 *is* unpleasantness.

semantics.definition_long: Valence Potential indexes the predicted hedonic tone of a scene — the pleasant/unpleasant dimension of Russell's (1980) circumplex model of affect, orthogonal to arousal (L23). The circumplex anchors valence as one of two dimensions sufficient to span the structure of affective experience, with discrete emotions emerging as combinations of valence and arousal. The construct is bi-polar by definition (positive at one end, negative at the other, neutral at the midpoint), and the panel therefore assigns `valence_polarity: mixed` not because the construct could go either way in any application, but because it is internally bi-polar and a single positive/negative tag would mis-state the scale. Image-derived valence is predicted from upstream observables that empirically track preference: warm-colour dominance, presence of natural elements (vegetation, water, daylight), cleanliness vs grime, and organic vs harsh form. The valence response can in principle drive approach or avoidance; in the BN, valence is upstream of approach-bias measures.
semantics.definition_short: Pleasant/unpleasant tone the scene is likely to elicit.
examples_negative: A neglected stairwell with peeling paint, a dim flickering fluorescent fixture, scattered litter, and a pervasive grey-green palette — a clear negative-valence anchor.
An abandoned industrial corridor with rust-stained concrete, broken glass, and harsh sodium-vapour lighting.
A clinical procedure room with cold blue-white lighting, exposed equipment, and the institutional smell-implication of antiseptic.
A cluttered storeroom with brown cardboard boxes, exposed metal shelving, and no daylight.
examples_positive: A sunlit kitchen with light-wood cabinetry, fresh fruit on the counter, a vase of flowers, and a clean white ceramic sink.
A small park bench under a flowering tree with a tidy gravel path and the soft greens of mid-spring foliage.
A warmly lit Mediterranean courtyard with terracotta tile, climbing bougainvillea, and a quietly bubbling fountain.
A nursery with pastel walls, soft natural daylight from a side window, and a clean wooden cradle.
A bookshop reading nook with warm pendant lighting, an upholstered chair, and a side table with a steaming cup.
A traditional Japanese ryokan room with tatami flooring, a paper-screen window admitting diffuse daylight, and a single low table.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is canonical for L24. The extractor reads upstream observables of warm-colour dominance (mass of pixels in the red/orange/yellow hue ranges weighted against blue/grey), naturalness (presence and area of vegetation, water, sky, natural materials), cleanliness vs grime (luminance uniformity within materials, presence of stains/litter/decay signatures), daylight presence (high-CCT or diffuse-daylight signature in window/sky regions), and organic-vs-harsh form (curvature and roundedness of dominant lines vs sharp angularity). A VLM conditioned on Russell (1980) and the SAM-pleasure probe (Bradley & Lang, 1994) returns a 5-point Likert. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: warm-cool valence mappings vary modestly across cultures, but the construct itself is robustly cross-cultural in the affective-science literature.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan supports vantage-stable valence estimates by averaging across viewpoints, which corrects for the framing bias of any single photograph (a clever photograph can make a grim space look pleasant). Multi-vantage extraction also catches valence-degrading details that may be cropped out of a hero shot — back-of-house clutter, stained ceilings, broken fixtures. The construct itself remains snapshot-temporal: for sustained-exposure mood predictions the BN should chain L24 to a downstream temporal node, not modify L24 itself.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L24 indicator block — three rating items (pleasantness; mood improvement; attractiveness) anchored on SAM-pleasure (Bradley & Lang, 1994) and PANAS (Watson et al., 1988); two behavioural proxies (approach bias in 2AFC; willingness-to-pay/travel).
scope_excludes: Arousal (L23): valence and arousal are orthogonal axes of the Russell (1980) circumplex; a high-arousal nightclub and a high-arousal hospital ER differ on valence even if matched on arousal.
Specific discrete emotions (joy, fear, anger): the circumplex model treats discrete emotions as derived combinations, and L24 captures only the valence component.
Long-term place-attachment or aesthetic preference: those are downstream of momentary valence and are governed by separate constructs.
scope_includes: The full bi-polar range of pleasantness to unpleasantness; the Likert is anchored at neutral in the middle.
Both interior and exterior scenes.
Cases where valence is dissociated from arousal (a calm pleasant garden = high-valence, low-arousal; a calm grim corridor = low-valence, low-arousal).

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.visibility_control.definition: Ability to monitor environment without being monitored.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + sightline geometry + partition / vantage-point detection

key_refs: Appleton, J. (1975). The experience of landscape. Wiley.
Hildebrand, G. (1999). Origins of architectural pleasure. University of California Press.
Stamps, A. E. (2005). Visual permeability, locomotive permeability, safety, and enclosure. Environment and Behavior, 37(5), 587–619. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013916505276741

Plant, R. W., & Ryan, R. M. (1985). Intrinsic motivation and the effects of self-consciousness, self-awareness, and ego-involvement: An investigation of internally controlling styles. Journal of Personality, 53(3), 435–449.

notes.bn_notes: Distinct from cognitive.visual_privacy (L10): visibility control is a *prospect* construct (the viewer's ability to see out and monitor); visual privacy is a *refuge* construct (the viewer's protection from being seen). A single space can score high on both (a partition-bounded alcove with a window onto the room) or low on both (an open-plan workstation in the middle of a floor).
notes.extraction_notes: VLM prompted with the V2.7 IMI-derived rating items ("How much control would you have over\u2026", "How easy would it be to adjust\u2026", "How constrained would you feel\u2026") returns a Likert 0–4.

semantics.definition_long: The asymmetric-visibility construct from Appleton's (1975) prospect-refuge analysis: the viewer's perceived ability to monitor the surrounding environment from a vantage point without being readily monitored in turn. Hildebrand (1999) elaborated the construct in architectural settings, arguing that the most pleasurable spaces combine a prospect over a wider field with a refuge that limits the viewer's own exposure. Stamps's (2005) work on visual permeability provides the operational scaffolding: visibility control is high when sightlines extend outward from the implied viewpoint over a broad arc but inward sightlines onto the viewpoint are partially obstructed by partitions, alcoves, or backdrops.
semantics.definition_short: Visibility Control — ability to monitor environment without being monitored.
examples_negative: A small enclosed booth with no window — high refuge but zero prospect; this is L10 visual privacy, not L03 visibility control.
An open-plan central seat with full 360° sightline but also full exposure — high prospect but zero refuge; classical sociofugal arrangement, not visibility control.
A glass-walled meeting room — fully transparent in both directions; no asymmetry, so visibility control is moderate at best.
A space with a one-way mirror under controlled lighting — technically delivers asymmetric visibility but is so unusual that it should be treated as a special case rather than a typical exemplar.
examples_positive: How much control would you have over lighting/temperature/privacy here?
How easy would it be to adjust this space to suit you?
How constrained would you feel by the space? (reverse-coded)
Option use: number of controls/zones used when given choices (UI/VR).
Task persistence under distraction as a proxy for autonomy support.
A high-back lounge chair positioned in the corner of a café with a view across the entire room and a wall behind the seat.
A loft balcony overlooking a double-height living room, with a half-wall railing partially screening the balcony seat from the room below.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `affordance.vantage_point` (whether the implied viewpoint enjoys an elevated or backed position), `scene.sightline_extent` (the angular arc of unobstructed outward visibility from the viewpoint), `affordance.partition_height` (height of any partition behind or to the side of the viewpoint that screens it from inward sightlines), `affordance.mirror_presence` (mirrors that extend the prospect without exposing the viewpoint), and `scene.openness_index` (a Stamps-derived ratio of visible-volume to enclosed-volume). The V2.7 cue list "sightlines; vantage points; mirrors; open views" maps onto sightline_extent, vantage_point, mirror_presence, and openness_index. The 2D extractor returns `partial` confidence because monocular reconstruction of the asymmetry between outward and inward sightlines is ambiguous in the absence of depth cues; a VLM prompted with the three V2.7 rating items aggregates the cues into the appraisal latent.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can compute the explicit isovist from the implied viewpoint and, separately, the union of isovists from a sampled set of viewpoints in the surrounding space pointing back at the focal viewpoint. Per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR isovists from viewpoints" — visibility control is then quantified as the difference between outward-isovist area and inward-back-isovist area, normalised by total floor area. A high positive value indicates strong prospect with low exposure (high visibility control); a value near zero indicates symmetric visibility (no control); a strongly negative value indicates a high-exposure central position (low visibility control / high surveillance). This 3D operationalisation is the canonical Hildebrand-Stamps measure.
scope_excludes: Visual privacy / not being seen — that's L10 (cognitive.visual_privacy), the refuge complement to this prospect construct.
Surveillance pressure / feeling watched — that's L04 (cognitive.surveillance_pressure), the affective complement to low visibility control.
General autonomy / control over conditions — that's L06 (cognitive.perceived_control); L03 is specifically *visual* monitoring asymmetry.
scope_includes: Capacity to scan and monitor a wider environment from the implied viewpoint.
Asymmetric reduction of inward sightlines onto the viewpoint by partitions, alcoves, or backings.
Vantage advantages such as elevation, corner backing, or mirror-aided extension of the field.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.visual_comfort.definition: Low-glare, low-strain optical environment with comfortable contrast and brightness.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image features for high-luminance specular highlights, luminance-ratio extremes, glare-source presence, and contrast distribution, read by a VLM conditioned on Boyce (2014) and CIE 117

key_refs: Boyce, P. R. (2014). Human factors in lighting (3rd ed.). CRC Press. https://doi.org/10.1201/b16707
CIE. (1995). CIE 117-1995: Discomfort glare in interior lighting. International Commission on Illumination.

Wienold, J., & Christoffersen, J. (2006). Evaluation methods and development of a new glare prediction model for daylight environments with the use of CCD cameras. Energy and Buildings, 38(7), 743-757. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enbuild.2006.03.017

notes.extraction_notes: Image_Judgability is 4 in V2.6 because tone-mapped JPEGs compress dynamic range and may misrepresent glare. HDR capture pathway (Wienold & Christoffersen, 2006) recommended for high-confidence Sprint-3 extraction.

semantics.definition_long: Visual Comfort indexes the absence of glare, the moderation of luminance ratios, and the appropriateness of contrast and brightness for the prevailing task and exposure duration. Following Boyce's (2014) human-factors-of-lighting framework and the discomfort-glare formulation in CIE 117 (1995), visual comfort decomposes into disability glare (sources that compromise vision), discomfort glare (sources that produce unease without compromising vision), veiling reflections, and luminance-ratio moderation across the visual field. A scene is visually comfortable when glare sources are screened or absent, when the luminance ratio between task and surround is within recommended bounds, when colour-rendering is adequate, and when daylight is admitted without producing high-contrast bands across work surfaces. The construct is positively-valenced by definition (`valence_polarity: positive`); the inverse 'visual discomfort' is not separately tagged in Sprint 2 but exists as an implicit complement.
semantics.definition_short: Low-glare, low-strain optical environment with comfortable contrast and brightness.
examples_negative: A subway-station mezzanine with bare fluorescent fixtures producing direct discomfort glare, polished floor surfaces producing veiling reflections, and luminance ratios that exceed comfort bounds.
An open-plan office where afternoon sun strikes a glossy whiteboard at a glancing angle, producing a high-luminance specular hot-spot that drives observers to look away.
A retail showroom with intensely bright spotlights aimed at merchandise from low angles, producing both direct glare into the shopper's eye line and harsh reflections off polished display cases.
A vehicle interior at dusk with an unshielded interior dome light reflected in the windshield and a mismatched bright touchscreen creating dazzling secondary glare.
examples_positive: An office with daylight admitted through translucent diffusing blinds, supplemental indirect uplighting, and a uniform luminance distribution across the workstation field.
A museum gallery with carefully shielded track lighting, no specular highlights on the protective glazing of the artworks, and a restrained ambient luminance.
A reading room with shaded table-lamps providing localised task light over books, and a soft ambient ceiling wash that moderates the table-to-surround ratio.
A residential kitchen with under-cabinet task lighting, a daylight window screened by a sheer curtain, and matte counter surfaces that do not produce specular reflections.
A clinical examination room with high-CRI, glare-shielded fixtures and matte wall finishes that diffuse light without producing discomfort-glare hot spots.
A north-facing artist's studio with diffuse skylight, neutral-grey walls, and shaded supplemental fixtures positioned outside the line of sight from the easel.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image supports partial extraction: specular highlights are visible, luminance ratios can be estimated within the dynamic range of the camera, and glare sources can sometimes be identified. The from_2d flag is set to 'partial' because cameras compress dynamic range, and what reads as a moderate highlight in a JPEG may be a punishing glare source in person; conversely, an uncompressed scene may understate glare risk. The extractor reads upstream observables of specular-highlight extent (high-luminance pixel mass), luminance-ratio max/min (within the assessable dynamic range), explicit glare-source presence (bare bulbs, unshielded windows in line of sight), and contrast distribution. A VLM conditioned on Boyce (2014) and CIE 117 returns a 5-point Likert with confidence reflecting the dynamic-range uncertainty. Cross-cultural variance is `medium`: the underlying optical-comfort science is well-codified, but cultural lighting norms vary (Northern European preference for low-CCT, low-luminance vs East Asian preference for higher overall illuminance).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D/VR scan provides substantially better substrate than 2D because high-dynamic-range capture (HDR luminance maps) preserves the full luminance distribution and allows direct computation of CIE 117 discomfort-glare metrics (UGR — Unified Glare Rating). With per-vantage HDR data, the extractor can compute UGR for each viewpoint and bin into the Likert with high confidence. Without HDR data, the 3D path collapses to the 2D path's partial extraction. Sprint-3 BN integration should consider routing UGR-direct measurements as an upstream parent tag once HDR-capable corpora become available.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet: L26 indicator block — three rating items (felt visual comfort; influence on choice to be here; image-confidence) and two behavioural proxies (2AFC choice frequency against matched alternatives; RT consistency across repetitions).
scope_excludes: Coziness (L25): coziness depends on warm-CCT and partial enclosure, while visual comfort is independent of CCT — a cool-CCT space can be visually comfortable, and a warm-CCT space can be visually uncomfortable.
Aesthetic preference for the lighting (L24 territory): the construct is functional comfort, not aesthetic taste.
Long-term ocular health: the construct is the immediate optical-comfort read, not a prediction of pathological exposure consequences.
scope_includes: Interior scenes with assessable lighting conditions, including both task-lit and amenity-lit settings.
Exterior scenes where glare and luminance-ratio conditions can be assessed (a sun-bleached plaza with no shade vs a tree-shaded park bench).
Both daylit and electrically lit conditions; mixed-source conditions are within scope provided their interaction is assessable from the image.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.visual_privacy.definition: Not being seen by others while occupying the depicted space.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + partition / door / alcove segmentation + booth-seating detection + sightline-to-viewpoint geometry

key_refs: Altman, I. (1975). The environment and social behavior: Privacy, personal space, territory, and crowding. Brooks/Cole.
Westin, A. F. (1967). Privacy and freedom. Atheneum.
Pedersen, D. M. (1997). Psychological functions of privacy. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 17(2), 147–156. https://doi.org/10.1006/jevp.1997.0049
Appleton, J. (1975). The experience of landscape. Wiley.

notes.bn_notes: Refuge complement of cognitive.visibility_control (L03): visual privacy concerns *not being seen* (refuge); visibility control concerns *seeing* (prospect). Inverse of social.interactional_visibility (Sprint 1, L41-L58 layer): visual privacy is the refuge from view, while interactional_visibility is the propensity to be available for encounter.
notes.extraction_notes: Composite extractor combines partition / door / booth detection with a VLM scoring the V2.7 rating items.

semantics.definition_long: The viewer's anticipated capacity to remain unseen by others while occupying the depicted space. The construct draws centrally on Altman's (1975) privacy-regulation framework, which conceives privacy as a dialectical and dynamic boundary process — not a fixed quantity — by which the self regulates the openness of self to others. Westin's (1967) taxonomy of privacy types places visual privacy alongside the auditory, informational, and territorial sub-types, and Pedersen (1997) demonstrated that visual privacy supports distinct psychological functions including autonomy, emotional release, and self-evaluation. Appleton's (1975) refuge construct is the architectural-affordance complement: visual privacy is high when the depicted scene affords a refuge from incoming sightlines through partitions, doors, alcoves, or booth-style seating.
semantics.definition_short: Visual Privacy — not being seen by others.
examples_negative: A glass-walled meeting room — sound-isolated but visually exposed; visual privacy is low despite spatial enclosure.
A high-back lounge chair facing into an open room — the chair-back screens the seat from one direction only; partial privacy.
An open balcony overlooking a public square — fully visible from below; zero visual privacy.
A windowed nook with curtains drawn open — affords privacy if curtains close, but the depicted state shows full exposure.
examples_positive: How private would you feel from being seen here?
How easy would it be to avoid unwanted eye contact here?
How exposed would you feel while sitting/standing here? (reverse-coded)
Seat-choice: refuge/booth/partition vs open central seating (2AFC).
Attention to boundaries: dwell on doors/partitions (click/gaze optional).
A high-walled booth seat in a restaurant with side and back panels above head height and a small opening only at the entry.
An enclosed alcove off a corridor with a closed door, no interior windows, and an interior reading chair.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observables — `affordance.partition_height` (height of partitions screening the implied viewpoint from incoming sightlines), `affordance.door_present` (presence of a closable door bounding the privacy region), `affordance.alcove_depth` (depth and tightness of an alcove offering refuge), `furniture.booth_indicator` (presence of booth-style seating with high screening backs and sides), and `scene.sightline_to_viewpoint` (count and angular subtense of regions from which the viewpoint is visible). The V2.7 cue list "partitions; doors; alcoves; booth seating" maps directly onto partition_height, door_present, alcove_depth, and booth_indicator. A composite extractor combines the binary / scalar affordance cues with a VLM scoring the V2.7 rating items into a 0–4 Likert score.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction can compute the explicit incoming-isovist of the implied viewpoint per V2.7 `VR_or_Sensor_Enrichment`: "VR line-of-sight tests" — the visual privacy score is then 1 minus the normalised area of all surrounding regions from which the viewpoint is directly visible. The Altman (1975) privacy-regulation construct is dialectical, so the 3D substrate should additionally expose dynamic privacy: whether partitions, blinds, or doors are operable to transition between high- and low-privacy states. Booth depth, partition height relative to the viewpoint's seated/standing eye level, and alcove tightness become metric quantities in 3D rather than 2D inferences.
scope_excludes: Visual prospect / monitoring asymmetry — that's L03 (cognitive.visibility_control), the prospect not the refuge.
Felt surveillance / watchedness — that's L04 (cognitive.surveillance_pressure), the affective response to refuge failure.
Acoustic privacy — a different sensory modality covered by a separate construct.
scope_includes: Refuge from incoming sightlines: partitions, doors, alcoves, booth backs.
Capacity to occupy the space without being observed by passers-by or co-occupants.
Boundary-controlled visibility in Altman's privacy-regulation sense.

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.

change_log.change: Initial introduction (Sprint 2, cognitive/affect/perception latent layer L01-L40)

cognitive.welcome.definition: Binary sense that 'people like me' belong here and would be received hospitably.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + seating-variety counting + signage-tone classification + accessibility-cue detection + barrier detection

key_refs: Tuan, Y.-F. (1977). *Space and place: The perspective of experience*. University of Minnesota Press.
Smith, J. L. (1976). The early decoration of inclusive design. In *Designing for the disabled*. RIBA Publications.
Goffman, E. (1971). *Relations in public: Microstudies of the public order*. Basic Books.

notes.bn_notes: Welcome's parent set is dominated by signage-tone, accessibility, and barrier observables that are largely disjoint from the prestige and care parent sets, so the Spohn 50%-overlap audit should pass without restructuring. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: The binary (latent_score) value type follows the panel decision that welcome is read as 'for me / not for me'. Cross-cultural calibration is essential because welcome cues are culturally-coded (Hall on proxemics; Goffman on greeting rituals).

semantics.definition_long: Welcome/Inclusion is the perceiver's binary inference that the space is structured to receive a wide diversity of people hospitably — that 'people like me' belong here and would not be silently filtered out by environmental, behavioural, or normative cues. Following Tuan (1977, *Space and Place*), hospitable space is the architectural correlate of the social act of welcoming; it requires both the structural availability of space (seating, accessibility, undefended thresholds) and the legible social cue that occupation is invited rather than tolerated. Following Smith's (1976) inclusive-design tradition, the construct also takes in physical accessibility cues (ramps, lift availability, varied seating heights), demographic diversity in any visible occupants or signage, and the warmth of signage tone (welcoming versus prohibitive language). The binary encoding follows the Sprint 2 panel decision: welcome is more naturally read as 'this space is for me' versus 'this space is not for me' than as a smooth gradient. Cross-cultural variance is high: greeting norms, the role of thresholds, and the demographic markers of inclusion differ substantially across cultures, and extractors trained on one tradition will mis-classify spaces designed within another. Welcome is positive in valence, and its negative pole (exclusion) is recorded as `inverse_of: "exclusion"` even though no Sprint 2 latent encodes exclusion directly.
semantics.definition_short: Binary sense that 'people like me' belong here and would be received hospitably.
examples_negative: A high-end private club entrance with a uniformed doorman, a velvet rope, posted membership-only signage, and no visible seating in a reception area.
A corporate lobby with a floor-to-ceiling glass barrier behind which a turnstile and security desk control entry — physically open but normatively closed.
A retail space with prohibitive signage ('No backpacks', 'No food', 'Customers only', 'No loitering') stacked at the entrance, conveying suspicion rather than welcome.
A municipal office with a pull-a-ticket queue system and no seating for non-ticketed visitors — entrants who are not actively transacting are unwelcomed.
examples_positive: A community library with a varied seating menu (tall stools, low children's chairs, soft armchairs, table seating), no security desk between entrance and reading area, warm signage in multiple languages, and a visible accessibility ramp.
A neighbourhood café with a side-by-side mix of bench seats, bar stools, and small tables; a posted opening-hours notice; and no required-purchase signage.

A community centre lobby with seating provided for people who are not yet in any service queue, a visible information desk staffed by a person who looks up at entrants, and signage that names the centre's activities in welcoming language.
A church or temple with an undefended threshold, posted welcome to visitors, varied seating including accessible options, and a posted invitation to join services.
A public park entrance with no fences, visible benches near the entry, and signage that lists the activities permitted rather than prohibited.
A university student lounge with a mix of seating heights and styles, visible accommodations for accessibility, posted programmes naming many groups, and no posted membership requirements.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image supports welcome extraction when the entrance, signage, and seating menu are in frame. The extractor should consume upstream observables `furniture.seating_variety_score` (a count-and-diversity measure over visible seat types and heights), `signage.tone_sentiment` (a VLM-classified score over any visible signage as welcoming versus prohibitive), `accessibility.ramp_or_lift_visible` (binary), `accessibility.accessible_seating_present` (binary), `barrier.security_or_turnstile_present` (binary indicator of normative gating), and `objects.threshold_undefended` (binary on whether the principal threshold is open versus controlled). The binary positive judgement requires a positive seating-variety score, non-prohibitive signage tone, no security/turnstile barrier at the principal threshold, and at least one structural accessibility cue. Cross-cultural recalibration is required: extractors trained on Western inclusion cues will mis-read welcome-signalling traditions that operate through different cues (formal greetings rather than open thresholds, prescribed footwear changes rather than ramped access, etc.).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans, welcome extraction benefits from full inspection of the entry sequence (rather than a single still image), from canonical-distance evaluation of signage legibility and tone, and from full-mesh accessibility evaluation (does a wheelchair-traversable path actually exist from entry to seating?). The 3D extractor should compute (i) the seating-variety score over the full visible seating inventory, (ii) the entry-sequence barrier count and severity, (iii) the accessibility-path existence and width metrics, and (iv) the demographic-marker inventory of visible signage and imagery. Region support is false: welcome is an integrated entry-and-public-region binary. Monocular-3D approximation is partial because the entry-sequence evaluation is only as good as the depth estimate.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Welcome/Inclusion': rating items for (a) felt welcome, (b) extent to which the place is 'for people like you', (c) comfort of spending time alone there. Behavioural proxies (approach choice for social/solo scenarios, time allocation in browsing) belong in extractor docstrings.
scope_excludes: Comfort or coziness alone (L25 is the affective coziness construct; L34 is the inclusion inference). A cozy private members' lounge can be high on coziness and low on welcome.
Care/maintenance (L33) — a meticulously maintained but exclusionary space is L33-positive and L34-negative.
Prestige/luxury (L32) — high prestige often co-occurs with low welcome by design.
scope_includes: Variety of seating types and heights as a structural inclusion cue (Smith 1976).
Signage tone — welcoming or invitational language versus prohibitive language.
Visible accessibility cues — ramps, lift access, accessible seating, accessible signage.

semantics.definition: Use when the scene contains identifiable artworks that are intended to be noticed—large, central, repeated, or strongly lit/contrasted—rather than incidental decor. Works can be framed wall art, freestanding sculpture, murals, or curated installations.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene contains identifiable artworks that are intended to be noticed—large, central, repeated, or strongly lit/contrasted—rather than incidental decor. Works can be framed wall art, freestanding sculpture, murals, or curated installations.
semantics.definition_short: Artworks (paintings, sculpture, installations) are visually salient and a focal element of the space.
examples_negative: A small framed picture in a corner that is not visually salient.
Patterned wallpaper with no identifiable artwork (use pattern/ornament tags instead).
No clear evidence of Artworks Prominent is present.
examples_positive: A lobby with a large mural spanning a main wall and accent lighting.
A room where multiple framed paintings form a clear gallery wall.
A plaza with a large sculpture as the central visual anchor.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: In 2D: detect frames/canvases, sculptures, plinths; look for compositional centrality, scale, and lighting emphasis. CV hint: object detection for 'painting', 'sculpture', 'frame'; saliency maps to confirm prominence.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D: check line-of-sight frequency to art objects from typical paths; prominence increases if art lies on primary circulation routes and has high visual exposure. From 3D or scene data, use geometry/material cues for Artworks Prominent.
scope_excludes: generic posters/menus/signage (use branding/wayfinding)
purely ornamental textures without distinct art object
Ambiguous or incidental hints of Artworks Prominent.
scope_includes: murals, framed art, gallery walls
sculptures/installs placed centrally
spotlighting or display plinths
repeated art as a theme

semantics.definition: Use when there are visible constraints on movement or access: railings, fences, stanchions, turnstiles, bollards, gates, protective screens, or obvious security guards stationed for control. The key is **movement restriction or protection**, not just partitions for layout.
semantics.definition_long: Use when there are visible constraints on movement or access: railings, fences, stanchions, turnstiles, bollards, gates, protective screens, or obvious security guards stationed for control. The key is **movement restriction or protection**, not just partitions for layout.
semantics.definition_short: Physical barriers or guards are present to restrict access, separate zones, or provide security/safety.
examples_negative: Interior partitions forming rooms (use compartmentalized_plan).
Low decorative edging that does not restrict movement.
No clear evidence of Barriers / Guards Present is present.
