A nightclub dance floor — mobile, social, and standing, but oriented around music-and-dance rather than conversational clustering; the affordance is a different one (peripheral_engagement / shared-focus), and L56 should not absorb it.
examples_positive: A hotel lobby with a long bar-height ledge along one wall, two free-standing high tables in the centre, and a clear 1.5 m-wide loop around them — three or four standing pairs could form without blocking the door to the breakfast room.

A conference foyer between sessions: round high tables scattered three to five metres apart, no fixed seating in the central zone, coffee station offset to one side so queues do not cross the standing clusters.

A craft brewery taproom with a window-counter perch facing the street, a central pour-bar, and a generous residual floor that lets parties of two to four orbit the bar and re-cluster without colliding.

An art-gallery atrium during an opening: paintings on the perimeter walls, a central catering table, and a clear annular floor between them on which standing groups form, dissolve, and reform around the wine.

A university department's 'milk-and-biscuits' common room with a tall bench along one wall, a kitchenette counter at standing height, and a deliberately under-furnished centre — the room is empty most of the day and full at 11 a.m.
A street-front café whose front quarter has been left as standing-only counter space facing the window, with the seated tables relegated to the rear; commuters lean, talk, and rotate through.
A market hall whose stalls are arranged with wide cross-aisles and intermittent leaning rails, so visitors pause, talk, and continue without the spatial commitment of sitting down.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor combines three streams against image-only inputs. First, upstream tag `furniture.standing_surface_count` (expected name; Phase D may rename) enumerates bar-height tables, leaning rails, counters, and ledges; a count >= 2 with spatial dispersion >= a threshold contributes positively. Second, upstream tag `affordance.free_floor_area_fraction` (expected) estimates the proportion of the visible floor unoccupied by furniture or fixed obstacles; a mid-to-high range (roughly 0.35-0.65) contributes positively, while extremes contribute negatively (a packed room cannot host clusters; a vast empty floor offers no anchoring). Third, upstream tag `affordance.circulation_path_clearance` (expected) confirms that through-movement of approximately person-width remains possible around the standing zones. The composite is calibrated on Western interior corpora and should be flagged for cultural variance at the medium level — settings such as Japanese standing-bars (`tachinomi`) and Mediterranean standing-coffee bars use markedly different perch and spacing conventions and may be systematically under- or over-scored by a Western-trained extractor (Hall, 1966). Where floor-plan augmentation is available, integration value from the axial map enters as a multiplicative gate: a high local pocket score in a globally segregated room (low integration) is downweighted, since Hillier and Hanson (1984) show such rooms remain unfrequented regardless of local affordance. In purely image-only mode the integration term is omitted and the construct is scored as a local-affordance ceiling.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: A 3D / VR or monocular-depth pipeline strengthens every component. Free-floor-area becomes a metric quantity from the depth map (square metres of unobstructed standing surface within the bounded room), with a target band of roughly 4-15 m^2 of *residual* floor for a small mingling room and proportionally more for larger ones. Standing surfaces are confirmed at the canonical perch height range (1.0-1.2 m) rather than inferred from class label alone, distinguishing true bar-height tables from misclassified high stools or planter rims. Distributed-perch geometry can be tested directly: the centroids of standing surfaces should not all lie on a single axis (which would indicate a single bar, not distribution) but should populate at least two non-collinear cluster sites separated by >= 2 m. Circulation paths are validated by tracing a 0.7 m-clearance corridor between cluster sites and to room exits. With a floor plan, the room's integration value is computed against the wider axial map and applied as the configurational gate described above; without it, depth-derived `affordance.local_isovist_area` from the room's interior nodes provides a within-room proxy. The 3D version of the extractor should still emit a Hall-zone distance estimate for the *expected* inter-cluster spacing, since cluster geometry is itself culturally indexed.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators (L56): rated items hypothesised to load on a single factor — 'open floor pockets,' 'high tables,' 'clear circulation,' 'distributed perches.' Behavioral proxy item: observed frequency of standing clusters lasting > 30 s. Factor structure is unconfirmed in the V2.6 sheet (n < 20 raters); Sprint 2 may revisit with EFA once the corpus is tagged.
scope_excludes: The *configurational* question of whether the room is actually frequented (i.e., sits on an integrated axial line in the wider plan). This is held in `configurational_measure: integration` and flagged for a Sprint-2 split into L56a (local pocket geometry) and L56b (configurational frequentation). Hillier's argument that L56 fuses two genuinely distinct properties is acknowledged here; the Sprint 1 compromise is to keep L56 unitary but to mark the conflation explicitly so downstream consumers can model it.
Standing-and-moving settings whose primary social organisation is *focused* (dance floor, sports rink, performance pit) — these belong to other constructs.
Pure circulation infrastructure (corridors, foyers used only for transit) — the *opportunistic* mingling that occurs there does not establish the affordance; the geometry must invite the pause.
Seated sociability, however vibrant — the construct is constitutively about the *standing-and-circulating* mode, not sociability in general.
scope_includes: Settings whose seated furniture is *deliberately* sparse to leave room for standing clusters (the 'under-furnished centre' pattern — counts as positive evidence).
Settings where perches are distributed (>= 2 distinct standing-anchor surfaces visible in the image) rather than concentrated at a single bar — distribution is part of the construct.
Mixed-mode rooms in which a seated zone and a mingling zone coexist, scored on the mingling zone's affordance not on a room average.
Outdoor and semi-outdoor plaza-style settings (Whyte's original substrate) — the construct is not interior-only.

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Occupancy in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Occupancy in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Occupancy in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Occupancy.
Occupancy is not visibly present.
No clear evidence of Occupancy is present.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Occupancy.
Occupancy is a dominant visible attribute.
Clear evidence of Occupancy is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Occupancy; avoid inference. From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues for Occupancy.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Occupancy; avoid speculation. From 3D or scene data, use geometry/material cues for Occupancy.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Occupancy.
Inferred Occupancy without visual evidence.
Inferred Occupancy without evidence.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Occupancy.
Dominant visible instance of Occupancy.
Multiple consistent cues indicate Occupancy.

social.peripheral_participation.definition: Affordance for adjacent-but-not-incorporated presence: watching, listening, lingering on edges.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + furniture-class detection + edge/wall geometry + sightline approximation

key_refs: Whyte, W. H. (1980). The social life of small urban spaces. Conservation Foundation.
Lave, J., & Wenger, E. (1991). Situated learning: Legitimate peripheral participation. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511815355
Goffman, E. (1963). Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings. Free Press.

notes.bn_notes: Not in the L46/L52/L53 set requiring a Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic flag, so this field is optional. Note for Phase D Spohn-overlap audit: L45's parent set shares one observable (sightline-related) with L42 (`affordance.sightline_to_flow` may overlap with L42's visibility observables) and one (perch-typed furniture) with L56 (`affordance.perch_count` may overlap with L56's standing-affordance observables). Neither overlap exceeds 50% of L45's four-observable parent set (max overlap = 1/4 = 25%), so the Spohn rule is not violated. Phase D should confirm the upstream observable IDs once those tags are reconciled.
notes.extraction_notes:
No V2.6 Tier override for L45 (V2.6 Tier 1 retained as final Tier 1). The principal disambiguation made in this spec is the choice to scope L45 to its *spatial-physical* sense (perches, edges, rails facing a flow) rather than its *developmental* sense (apprentice trajectory toward full participation). The spatial sense matches the V2.6 `Observable 2D Cues` row ("Perches on edges; benches facing flow; café window seating; balcony rails") unambiguously. The developmental sense — Lave & Wenger's original meaning — is a learning trajectory that cannot be tagged from a single still image regardless of richness, and was almost certainly not intended by the V2.6 author for this row. The decision to cite both Lave & Wenger (for term provenance) and Whyte (for the spatial sense actually adopted) honours the special-notes instruction in the assignment brief.

L45 is explicitly distinguished from three sibling tags in the same neighbourhood: (i) L41 chance-encounter potential is configurational and not image-extractable; L45 is local-affordance and image-extractable. (ii) L56 mingling affordance requires standing-circulating space without a sustained focal flow; L45 requires a perch-or-edge fitting *and* a separable flow. (iii) L44/L48/L49 F-formation latents are inward-facing; L45 perches are outward-facing toward a non-incorporated flow.

semantics.definition_long: The degree to which a setting affords *peripheral participation* — the bodily posture of being adjacent to a focused activity or social flow without joining it. Operationally, the construct is the spatial-physical provision of perches, edges, benches facing flow, café-window stools, balcony rails, and similar fittings that allow a person to remain proximate to action while abstaining from full engagement (Whyte, 1980, ch. 2). The term was introduced by Lave and Wenger (1991) to describe a developmental trajectory in apprenticeship learning; this registry adopts only the *spatial-physical* sense — the affordance for a bystander posture — and treats the developmental sense as out of scope for image-extracted tagging. The construct is closely related to Goffman's (1963) notion of unfocused interaction, in which co-present persons monitor one another at the threshold of involvement without committing to a focused encounter.
semantics.definition_short: Affordance for adjacent-but-not-incorporated presence: watching, listening, lingering on edges.
examples_negative: A row of dining chairs around a single table — these face *each other*, supporting a focused F-formation, not adjacency to a separate flow.
A single armchair in the geometric centre of an empty room — there is no flow to be peripheral *to*, and no edge from which to be peripheral.
A queue of standing people facing one direction at a service counter — they are *participants* in a service script (L46), not bystanders to it.
A circle of benches around a fountain with backs to the surrounding plaza — this affords inward-facing group seating, not edge-perching toward an outer flow.
A standing-mingling room with no perches, edges, or sightlines onto a separate flow — this affords mingling (L56) but not peripheral participation, because there is nothing peripheral *to*.
A solo lounge chair in a cul-de-sac alcove with no view of any flow — this affords retreat or refuge, not peripheral participation.
examples_positive: A café with a continuous window-counter and bar stools facing the sidewalk, where solo patrons sit watching pedestrian flow without entering it.
A library reading-room with a low rail along a mezzanine, where a person leans on the rail looking down at the activity below.
A plaza ringed with benches whose backs are to a planter and whose seat-fronts face the central paved area where children play and adults walk.
A theatre lobby with a velvet-rope perch along one wall, allowing a guest to stand and watch arrivals without joining any of the conversational clusters.
A wide hallway with a recessed bay containing a built-in bench, where a student sits with a laptop while seminars let out into the corridor.
A train-station mezzanine with a knee-height rail overlooking the concourse, used by people who have arrived early and watch the boards and the crowd.
An open-plan office "touch-down" counter at the edge of the kitchen area, where a person stands with coffee monitoring the kitchen conversation without entering the F-formation.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Score by composing four upstream observables: (a) `affordance.perch_count` — count of stools, low rails, recessed bench-bays, and window-counter positions detected in the image; (b) `affordance.edge_seating_orientation` — ratio of seated-furniture detections whose front-vector points away from interior focal clusters and toward a circulation path or window; (c) `affordance.sightline_to_flow` — presence of an unobstructed view from each candidate perch to a separable activity flow (street, concourse, kitchen, plaza); (d) `affordance.rail_balcony_presence` — detection of horizontal rails, mezzanine edges, or balcony enclosures at standing-elbow height. The four are combined into a 0–4 ordinal: 0 = no perches and no edge-oriented seating; 1 = one or two incidental perches; 2 = a clear single-perch affordance with sightline; 3 = multiple perches with sightlines distributed along an edge; 4 = a continuous edge-affordance system (e.g., café window-counter the full length of a wall, or mezzanine rail with seating). Western-calibration warning is *not* required for L45 (cross-cultural variance flagged medium), but extractors should note that perch fittings (window-counter culture, balcony-rail culture) vary somewhat by tradition; the affordance itself is broadly recognised across cultures (Whyte's findings replicated in non-Western plazas).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D or VR scene, use furniture-class metadata to identify perch-typed objects (bar stools, window stools, low rails, recessed bench-bays) and use scene-graph geometry to compute, for each perch, the angular sweep of unobstructed sightline to nearby circulation paths or activity zones. The 0–4 ordinal is then computed from (perch count) × (mean sightline-to-flow angular sweep) × (boolean for continuous edge system present). Where the scene contains an explicit annotated `circulation_path` polyline or `activity_zone` polygon, those tags should be used directly; where they are not present, derive a proxy circulation path from the longest straight uninterrupted floor-segment and a proxy activity zone from the largest open floor area not enclosed by an F-formation furniture cluster. Edges where the perch faces *into* an F-formation (L44/L48/L49) rather than *toward* a separable flow should not count.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators row L45: rating items on edge-availability, sightline-to-flow, and perch-density; behavioral proxy item on observed lingering at edges. Specific factor IDs are pending availability of the V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet; placeholder `LI.L45.f1` (edge affordance), `LI.L45.f2` (sightline-to-flow), `LI.L45.f3` (perch-density) for Phase C reconciliation.
scope_excludes: Inward-facing seating arrangements that constitute their own focal group (L44, L48, L49).
Settings that afford only mingling without any perch, edge, or rail to support adjacent-but-not-incorporated lingering (L56).
Solo retreat or refuge alcoves with no view of any flow (would be a different construct, not in L41–L58).
The Lave-and-Wenger developmental sense of *legitimate peripheral participation* as a learning trajectory; this registry treats only the spatial-physical affordance.
Chance-encounter potential as a configurational property of the whole spatial system (L41).
scope_includes: Edges, perches, rails, and counters whose primary affordance is *facing* a separable flow or activity from the periphery.
Bench arrangements oriented toward a circulation path or open activity area rather than toward each other.
Window-seating in cafés, lounges, lobbies that places the seated person at the boundary of the building's activity and the street's activity.
Mezzanines, balconies, and stair-landings with rails or seating overlooking a lower-level activity.
Recessed wall-bays, niches, and "touch-down" surfaces along corridors or kitchens that allow lingering at the edge of moving traffic.

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.

social.presentation_one_to_many.definition: Presenter-to-audience configuration: clear front, oriented seating, focal lighting on speaker.
social.presentation_one_to_many.definition_short: Presenter-to-audience configuration: clear front, oriented seating, focal lighting on speaker.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + chair-orientation geometry + focal-object detection (podium/screen/board) + lighting-gradient estimate

key_refs: Goffman, E. (1959). *The presentation of self in everyday life*. Doubleday. (Front-region / back-region distinction, ch. 3)
Goffman, E. (1981). *Forms of talk*. University of Pennsylvania Press. (Footing and the platform format, pp. 124–159)
Kendon, A. (1990). *Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters*. Cambridge University Press. (F-formation system, including the asymmetric-semicircular case, ch. 7)
Atkinson, J. M. (1984). *Our masters' voices: The language and body language of politics*. Methuen. (Empirical analysis of platform-format configurations in political rhetoric)

notes.bn_notes: L52 is one of three binary-candidate latents (with L46 and L53) for which the panel's Spohn voice flagged a Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding recommendation. The binary `latent_score` value type is the right Sprint-1 commitment because (i) the configuration is genuinely categorical at first approximation — a room either commits to a one-to-many platform format or it does not — and (ii) the registry currently supports only probabilistic CPTs, and forcing a 5-state ordinal CPT for a near-binary construct would inflate the parameter count without explanatory gain. However, Spohn (2012, §5.3) argues that constructs of this binary "regime-switch" character are more naturally encoded as ranking functions over disbelief levels, which support default reasoning and graceful degradation under partial evidence. Sprint 2's BN-integration phase should evaluate whether L52 (and L46, L53) should be migrated from binary `latent_score` to a ranking-theoretic encoding once the registry schema admits ranking-function value types. Until then, the binary value type is honoured but should be regarded as provisional. The Spohn 50%-overlap rule applies to `bn.parent_tags`: L52's parent set is dominated by `affordance.*` and `furniture.*` observables specific to presentation geometry, with `lighting.focal_gradient_index` providing an additional disjoint signal. The most likely overlap conflict in audit will be with L53 (audience attentional focus), which will share the `furniture.chair_orientation_field` parent. The pair is intentionally distinguished by L52's reliance on front-region affordance markers (podium, screen, lectern) versus L53's reliance on audience-side gaze and posture observables. If audit shows >50% overlap, the resolution is to remove `furniture.chair_orientation_field` from L53's parent set and let L53 depend on a more specific gaze-direction observable (e.g., `pose.head_yaw_field`) instead. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied. V2.6 Tier 1 is preserved: the construct is genuinely image-extractable because the front-region markers (podium, screen, board, lectern) and audience-axis convergence are visible in a single still image without requiring floor-plan augmentation or whole-system configurational measures. The construct is binary at first approximation and is encoded as `latent_score` (binary) per the panel disposition (§3 (x)). Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding is flagged in `bn_notes`.
notes.validation_notes: Pending Phase F.5 panel vote. *Preserved disagreement (Kendon, recorded for student handoff):* Kendon argued during the panel deliberation that L52 is not ontologically distinct from the F-formation cluster (L44, L48, L49) but is instead the limiting case of a *highly asymmetric semicircular F-formation* in which one participant occupies the unique "transmitting" position while all others occupy receiving positions. On this reading, the architectural front-region marker (podium, screen) is a material elaboration of the o-space asymmetry rather than a separate ontological commitment, and L52 should be folded into the L48/L49/L44 cluster as a value of the F-formation arrangement enum (e.g., `"semicircular_asymmetric"` or `"platform_format"`) rather than carried as a separate latent. The Sprint 1 disposition (panel disposition §3 (xii)) rejected this folding for two reasons: (i) the V2.6 taxonomy has been shipped to students with L52 as a separate construct, and the registry's traceability to its source taxonomy outranks ontological purity within Sprint 1; (ii) folding L52 into the F-formation cluster mid-sprint would force a cascading re-key of all consumer contracts that already reference `social.presentation_one_to_many`. Kendon's argument is recorded here as one of the four explicit construct contests handed to the students for Week 5 deliberation (panel disposition §5, contest 3). Students should consider whether L52 should be folded into L48/L49/L44 as a categorical-value-of, retained as a separate latent with explicit cross-reference to the F-formation cluster, or split into a configuration-binary and an attentional-engagement pair (currently L52 + L53). The architectural panel as constituted on 28 April 2026 explicitly preserved this disagreement rather than resolving it.

semantics.definition_long: A *one-to-many presentation* configuration is a focused gathering in which the spatial arrangement establishes an asymmetric performance frame: a single, clearly-marked "front region" oriented toward an aggregated audience whose seating, body axis, and gaze are co-aligned upon that front. The construct picks out the architectural and furniture-level commitments that *afford* this performance frame — a discernible focal zone (stage, podium, screen, board, instructor's desk), audience seating whose long axis points toward that focal zone, and (typically) a lighting or scale gradient that distinguishes presenter from audience. Following Goffman (1959, ch. 3), the configuration creates a front/back-region division with the audience as ratified recipients; following Goffman (1981, pp. 124–159), it fixes the participation framework into the asymmetric "platform format" footing in which one party speaks and the rest are positioned to listen. Kendon (1990, ch. 7) treats the same arrangement as the limiting case of a *highly asymmetric semicircular F-formation*, in which one of the participants holds the only "transmitting" position while the rest occupy receiving positions around an o-space whose centroid lies on the front-region boundary. L52 is binary on the presence/absence of this configuration: either the room's geometry commits to a one-to-many frame, or it does not.
semantics.definition_short: Presenter-to-audience configuration: clear front, oriented seating, focal lighting on speaker.
examples_negative: A boardroom with twelve chairs around a single oblong table — every participant faces the table's centre; there is no front region and no asymmetric audience axis. (This is L48 vis-à-vis / L49 conference geometry, not L52.)
A cafe with small tables scattered across the floor, each table sociopetal in its own right, but with no shared focal front. (This is mingling/conversation geometry, not L52.)
A circular drum-circle arrangement of chairs with no privileged speaking position; participants face inward toward each other rather than outward toward a presenter. (Circular F-formation, not L52.)

A waiting room with chairs lining the perimeter walls, all oriented inward toward an empty central floor; there is no presenter region and no shared audience axis. (Sociofugal perimeter seating, not L52.)

An empty auditorium with seating fixed in rows but the front region completely undifferentiated — no podium, screen, lectern, or lighting marker — such that the geometry is ambiguous between a presentation hall and a holding area.
examples_positive: A university lecture hall with tiered seating, every row oriented toward a podium and projection screen at the front; a lectern with focal lighting marks the presenter's region.
A conference breakout room with chairs arrayed in arcing rows facing a flip-chart easel and a small raised dais; the presenter's table is placed at the convergent focus of the seating.
A house of worship with pews oriented toward a pulpit and altar; congregational seating shares a single forward axis.
A school classroom with student desks in rows facing a whiteboard and the teacher's desk; the chalk tray and a wall-mounted projector mark the front region.
A small startup all-hands space: thirty stackable chairs arranged in a fan facing a single TV monitor and a standing presenter spot marked by a microphone stand.
A press-briefing room with a fixed podium under directional lighting, rows of chairs facing it, and a backdrop wall behind the podium that visually frames the speaker.
A wedding ceremony arrangement with chairs in two columns separated by an aisle, all oriented toward an officiant's position at one end framed by an arch.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is sufficient when upstream observables jointly identify (a) a dedicated front region and (b) audience seating whose orientation axes converge on that front region. The extractor should consume upstream tags `affordance.podium_present`, `affordance.screen_or_board_present`, `affordance.lectern_present`, `furniture.chair_orientation_field` (a per-chair yaw vector), `furniture.seating_row_axis` (the principal axis of detected rows), and `lighting.focal_gradient_index` (whether one region of the scene shows a directional or higher-intensity lighting commitment than the rest). The binary positive judgement requires (i) at least one front-region marker AND (ii) ≥ 70% of detected audience-seating yaw vectors converging within ±30° of a single front direction. Symmetric F-formations (circular, vis-à-vis) are explicit negatives even if a screen is present, because the audience axis condition fails. The extractor should *also* emit the F-formation-geometry sub-fields required by Kendon-driven extractor contract: `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` where for L52 `predominant_arrangement` will typically resolve to `"semicircular_asymmetric"` and the o-space centroid lies on the boundary between front region and audience. Region support is true: positive instances should return a polygon for the front region and a polygon for the audience seating block. Cross-cultural variance is medium: the platform format is robust across cultures (the asymmetric speaker-audience configuration recurs widely in pedagogical, ceremonial, and political settings), but cultures vary in *how strongly* the front-region marker is materially elaborated — a Japanese lecture hall may have minimal stage furniture relative to a Western analogue.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans the same upstream observables are available with stronger geometric grounding: chair-orientation yaw vectors are recoverable from full mesh geometry rather than monocular pose estimation, and the focal-region commitment can be verified by computing whether the union of audience-chair viewing frusta intersects in a coherent region (the front region). The extractor should compute (i) per-chair viewing direction from chair-back normal vectors, (ii) the convergence point of those vectors (or the region of densest intersection if the vectors are near-parallel), (iii) whether a front-region marker (podium, screen, raised platform mesh) is present within that convergence region. A 3D positive instance requires the convergence region to coincide with at least one detected front-region marker. The F-formation o-space centroid is computed identically to the 2D case but with greater precision; the o-space radius can be computed from the mean distance from convergence point to first row of audience chairs. Monocular-3D-approximation extraction is also viable (Tier 1 in V2.6) using monocular depth estimation plus a planar-floor assumption; the binary judgement is robust to noise in depth estimation because it depends on the *direction* of seating axis convergence rather than absolute distances.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor "Presentation Geometry": rating items for (a) presence of clear front region, (b) presence of screen/board/podium, (c) audience-seating axis convergence, (d) presence of focal lighting on speaker zone. Behavioural proxies (presenter standing, audience seated and oriented) belong in extractor docstrings rather than the registry.
scope_excludes: Symmetric F-formations (vis-à-vis, L-arrangement, circular) where no party occupies a privileged transmitting position — these are L44, L48, L49.
Mingling configurations with no fixed seating and no front region — these are L56.
Multi-focus arrangements where several small audience clusters each face their own front (e.g., a poster session, a science fair) — these are not one-to-many but many-to-many.
Sociopetal perimeter seating around an empty centre, where the focal point is communal rather than performer-occupied (e.g., a campfire-style arrangement) — closer to L51 group assembly than to L52.
scope_includes: Educational settings (classrooms, seminar rooms, lecture halls) where the front-region commitment is institutionally stable even when no presenter is present.
Religious and civic-ceremony spaces (chapels, courtrooms, council chambers) whose seating asymmetry encodes a one-to-many participation frame.
Outdoor or temporary configurations (a folding-chair audience facing a temporary stage) whose geometry commits to the platform format even without permanent architecture.

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Privacy in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Privacy in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Privacy in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Privacy.
Privacy is not visibly present.
No clear evidence of Privacy is present.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Privacy.
Privacy is a dominant visible attribute.
Clear evidence of Privacy is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Privacy; avoid inference. From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues for Privacy.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Privacy; avoid speculation. From 3D or scene data, use geometry/material cues for Privacy.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Privacy.
Inferred Privacy without visual evidence.
Inferred Privacy without evidence.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Privacy.
Dominant visible instance of Privacy.
Multiple consistent cues indicate Privacy.

social.queue_support.definition: How well the space scaffolds orderly, single-file service interactions and turn order.
social.queue_support.definition_short: How well the space scaffolds orderly, single-file service interactions and turn order.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + object detection (stanchions, signage, floor markings) + service-point identification + spatial-relation inference between line endpoint and service point

key_refs: Mann, L. (1969). Queue culture: The waiting line as a social system. *American Journal of Sociology*, *75*(3), 340–354. https://doi.org/10.1086/224787
Larson, R. C. (1987). Perspectives on queues: Social justice and the psychology of queueing. *Operations Research*, *35*(6), 895–905. https://doi.org/10.1287/opre.35.6.895
Goffman, E. (1963). *Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings*. Free Press. (See pp. 23–24 on "withs" and "singles" in service queues.)
Whyte, W. H. (1980). *The social life of small urban spaces*. Conservation Foundation. (Observational documentation of impromptu vs. scaffolded queue formation in plaza service contexts.)

notes.bn_notes: Not in the L46/L52/L53 set, so the Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic flag does not apply. However, a related observation worth recording: the three sub-cues used in the extraction rubric (defined line, single service point, next-position cue) approximate a conjunctive scoring model. Sprint 2 may wish to model L47 as a noisy-AND of those three sub-cues rather than as a free-parameter ordinal CPT, on the grounds that (a) the sub-cues are designed as substitutes/complements rather than as independent evidence, and (b) the noisy-AND parameterisation will fit the small available corpus more reliably than a 5-state CPT with three parents.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override. V2.6 Tier 2 → registry mapping (from_2d: `partial`, from_3d_vr: `yes`, monocular_3d_approx: `partial`) is honoured. The Tier 2 placement reflects the briefing's special note that queue *infrastructure* is recoverable from a 2D image but queue *function* requires behavioural evidence the image cannot supply; the `partial` rating on `from_2d` encodes exactly this limitation. The VLM-based `method_family` is appropriate because the construct is best read by a model that can integrate signage text, floor markings, barrier topology, and service-point identification into a single ordinal judgment; no clean geometric primitive captures the conjunction.

semantics.definition_long: The degree to which the spatial setting provides legible, orderly scaffolding for sequential service encounters — that is, the extent to which a setting publishes "who is next" through stanchions, floor markings, a defined waiting line, and a single point of service such that users can reliably take turns without verbal negotiation. The construct is rooted in Mann's (1969) classic field study of queue behaviour, in which physical regimentation reduced ambiguity about precedence and lowered conflict at service points; in Larson's (1987) analysis showing that perceived waiting time is mediated as much by queue *legibility* as by queue *length*; and in Goffman's (1963, pp. 23–24) treatment of "withs" and "singles" who must reconcile their bodily orientation to the published order of service. L47 measures the *spatial* contribution to that scaffolding — the extent to which the room itself answers the question "where do I stand and who is next?" — independently of whether a queue is presently in use.
semantics.definition_short: How well the space scaffolds orderly, single-file service interactions and turn order.
examples_negative: A crowded farmers'-market stall where customers cluster in a semicircle around the produce, calling out orders as the vendor catches their eye — high service activity, zero queue scaffolding (this is opportunistic mingling, not queue support).

A hotel concierge desk with two staff but no rope or floor marking, where guests form ad-hoc clusters and rely entirely on verbal "are you in line?" negotiation — service exists, queue *infrastructure* does not.
A restaurant host stand with a clipboard and a verbal waitlist but no spatial queue and no posted next-position cue — turn-taking is sociotemporal but not spatially scaffolded.

An empty corridor with stanchions stored against the wall and a "queue here when busy" sign — the materials exist but the *configuration* does not currently scaffold queueing (queue infrastructure is latent rather than deployed).

A self-checkout area with six kiosks and a single roving attendant, where customers approach whichever machine frees up first without any single-point-of-service line — this is a parallel-service topology, not a queue.
examples_positive: A bank lobby with retractable belt stanchions zig-zagging between roped-off lanes, terminating at a single "next teller" sign and a numerical now-serving display.
A coffee-shop counter with a printed floor decal reading "ORDER HERE" at one end and "PICK UP HERE" at the other, with a painted line on the floor connecting the entry point to the order point.
A pharmacy consultation window with a clearly marked privacy line two metres back from the counter, a "wait here" floor sticker, and a single service window with a "Next Customer" backlit sign.
A passport-control hall with permanent metal railings forming a serpentine queue, overhead "next available officer" lights, and a single embarkation point at each booth.
A deli counter with a paper take-a-number dispenser at the entry, a digital "now serving 47" sign above the cases, and a defined customer side bounded by a low rail.
An airline check-in area with floor-mounted stanchion bases and posted lane discipline (Premium / Economy), each lane single-file and terminating at one agent at a time.
A theme-park ride entrance with a long switchback queue defined by metal pipe railings, regular "wait time from this point" signage, and a single turnstile at the boarding platform.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Extraction proceeds via VLM prompted with the V2.6 indicator set. The VLM is given the image and asked to score, on the 0–4 Likert above, whether each of three sub-cues is present: (a) a defined waiting line, evidenced by upstream tag `affordance.stanchion_count` ≥ 1 OR `affordance.floor_marking_present` = true OR a continuous railing flagged by upstream segmentation under class `barrier_railing`; (b) a single point of service, evidenced by upstream tag `furniture.counter_count` = 1 within the local region OR a single staffed `service_window` detection; (c) a clear next-position cue, evidenced by upstream `signage.numeric_display_present`, `signage.next_customer_marker`, or a "wait here" floor decal under `floor_marking.text_present`. The VLM aggregates the three sub-cues into the 0–4 score using the rubric in `value_type assignment`. **Critical 2D limitation:** static images cannot resolve whether the stanchions are *deployed* in service of an active queue topology or merely stored along a wall awaiting deployment; the prompt must instruct the VLM to penalise apparent storage configurations. **Cultural note:** queue-support cues are calibrated against North American and North-European hosting norms (Mann, 1969; Larson, 1987); settings in cultures with different queueing conventions (e.g., contexts where simultaneous-approach service is normative) may score artificially low — flag this in extractor output but do not adjust the score.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR substrates, extraction is straightforward and approaches `yes` confidence: stanchion meshes, floor decals, and service-window geometry are all directly recoverable as scene-graph entities, and the spatial relation between the line endpoint and the service point can be computed as a path through the navigable region. The VLM is replaced or augmented by a geometric pass that (i) identifies all `barrier_stanchion` and `barrier_railing` instances in the scene, (ii) traces their topology to determine whether they form a continuous lane terminating near (iii) a single `service_counter` or `service_window` instance, and (iv) checks for `signage.next_customer_marker` instances within sight of the lane endpoint. The 0–4 score is computed deterministically from the geometric primitives. Floor-plan augmentation is not required because the construct is local to a single room or service area. Monocular-3D-approx is `partial` because the depth and topology of stanchion lanes are recoverable in principle but stanchion-to-stanchion connectivity is fragile under occlusion.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet for L47, primary indicators: "defined waiting line", "stanchions / belt barriers / fixed railings", "single-point service termination", "clear next-position cue (sign, decal, light)". Behavioural proxy (not registry-level): observed adherence to lane discipline by present users.

Secondary loading is expected on hosting-script clarity (L46) — a well-scaffolded queue typically co-occurs with a legible service script — but the two should be separable when probed (e.g., a well-marked queue at an unmanned counter, or a clearly-scripted desk with no queue infrastructure).
scope_excludes: *Service script clarity* (L46): whether one understands what to *do* at the counter once one's turn comes is a different construct from whether the *path to the counter* is legibly ordered. L47 ends at the service point; L46 begins there.
Crowd-density management without any turn-order claim (e.g., a museum with directional flow markings to manage congestion but no service encounter at all).
Queue *behaviour* (whether people are actually in line, in order, and not cutting). L47 is the *spatial affordance* for orderly turn-taking; the behaviour is downstream and culturally moderated.
Self-service or parallel-service topologies (food-court ordering kiosks, supermarket self-checkout banks) where there is no single-server termination.
Egress queueing (theatre exits, bus boarding lines) — those are throughput-management settings; L47 specifically targets *service* encounters.
scope_includes: Settings where a queue scaffold is *deployed* even if no one is currently waiting (the configuration is what is being measured, not the live behaviour).
"Virtual" queue scaffolding via take-a-number systems plus ample seating, when paired with a clear single point of call (as in DMV waiting rooms).
Multi-lane queues where each lane is itself single-file and terminates at one server — the construct measures *per-lane* turn-taking scaffolding.
Hybrid hosted-and-queued settings (e.g., a restaurant podium with both a posted waitlist and a defined waiting bench).

ranking_function.note: Two-state ranking function κ: {0,1} → ℕ where κ(¬state) records disbelief in the alternative state. Initialized to (0, 0) — Sprint 4 will populate from estimated CPTs.

social.shared_attention_anchor.definition: Triadic anchor: salient focal object plus seating oriented toward it, fixing shared focus.

extraction.compute_from: 2D + segmentation + focal-object/feature detection + chair-orientation geometry + saliency-weighted anchor candidate ranking

key_refs: Tomasello, M. (1995). Joint attention as social cognition. In C. Moore & P. J. Dunham (Eds.), *Joint attention: Its origins and role in development* (pp. 103-130). Lawrence Erlbaum.
Tomasello, M. (2008). *Origins of human communication*. MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/7551.001.0001
Kendon, A. (1990). *Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters*. Cambridge University Press. (F-formation system, o-space and shared focus, ch. 7)
Goffman, E. (1963). *Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings*. Free Press. (Focused gatherings, ch. 6)

Carpenter, M., Nagell, K., & Tomasello, M. (1998). Social cognition, joint attention, and communicative competence from 9 to 15 months of age. *Monographs of the Society for Research in Child Development*, *63*(4), i-vi, 1-143. https://doi.org/10.2307/1166214

notes.bn_notes: L53 is one of three binary-candidate latents (with L46 and L52) for which the panel's Spohn voice flagged a Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding recommendation. The binary `latent_score` value type is the right Sprint-1 commitment because (i) the construct is genuinely categorical at first approximation — the room either commits to a triadic anchor or it does not — and (ii) the registry currently supports only probabilistic CPTs, and forcing a 5-state ordinal CPT for a near-binary construct would inflate the parameter count without explanatory gain. Spohn (2012, sec.5.3) argues that constructs of this binary 'regime-switch' character are more naturally encoded as ranking functions over disbelief levels, which support default reasoning and graceful degradation under partial evidence. In particular, L53's triadic-structure requirement makes it well-suited to a ranking-theoretic encoding because the construct admits a principled default — *no anchor* is the default disbelief-rank-0 case, and *anchor present* requires positive evidence on both poles of the triad. Sprint 2's BN-integration phase should evaluate whether L53 (and L46, L52) should be migrated from binary `latent_score` to a ranking-theoretic encoding once the registry schema admits ranking-function value types. Until then, the binary value type is honoured but should be regarded as provisional. The Spohn 50%-overlap rule applies to `bn.parent_tags`. L53's parent set is dominated by `affordance.focal_object_*` observables (the object pole of the triad) and by orientation-related observables (the participant pole). The most likely overlap conflict in audit is with L52 (one-to-many presentation), which shares the orientation-related observables `furniture.chair_orientation_field` and `furniture.seating_row_axis`. The pair is intentionally distinguished by L53's reliance on `affordance.focal_object_*` (a generic salient feature, not necessarily a presentation marker) versus L52's reliance on `affordance.podium_present` / `affordance.screen_or_board_present` / `affordance.lectern_present` (presentation-specific markers). To stay under the 50% overlap bound, L53's parent set is specified to use `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` and `pose.head_yaw_field` (gaze observables) rather than the generic `furniture.chair_orientation_field` parent that L52 uses, even though the underlying upstream signal is related. Phase D `audit-extraction-plan` will verify the disjointness of these parent sets; if the 50%-overlap test fails, the resolution is to further decompose `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` into anchor-direction-specific observables that L52 does not consume. A second potential overlap is with the F-formation cluster (L48 / L49), which shares orientation observables. The disambiguating parent is `affordance.focal_object_present`: F-formations specify the o-space's geometric arrangement without requiring a materially-marked anchor within it, while L53 requires both. Audit should treat the F-formation cluster's lack of an `affordance.focal_object_*` parent as the structural distinguisher. | Sprint 3 ranking-theoretic flag: this latent is a binary candidate for ranking-function encoding (Spohn, 2012, §5.3). The downstream BN engine may consult bn.ranking_function as an alternative to the binary CPT. Fallback: Likert encoding via the existing latent_score value_type.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied. V2.6 Tier 1 is preserved: the construct is genuinely image-extractable because both poles of the triad — the focal anchor and the seating-orientation convergence — are visible in a single still image without floor-plan augmentation or whole-system configurational measures. The construct is binary at first approximation and is encoded as `latent_score` (binary) per the panel disposition (sec.3 (x)). Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding is flagged in `bn_notes`. The panel-mandated triadic-structure exclusion (Kendon's argument that V2.6's 'dominant focal object/wall' cue identifies a *candidate* anchor but not the triadic structure that joint attention requires) is operationalised in the extractor via the orientation-convergence criterion (>= 60% of seating yaw vectors within +/-35 degrees of the anchor-direction vector). This is the most consequential extraction decision for L53: it commits the registry to coding *anchor-without-orientation* configurations (a striking wall in a lobby whose seating ignores it) as L53-negative even though the V2.6 cue language treats them as positive instances.

semantics.definition_long: A *shared attention anchor* is the spatial substrate of the joint-attention triad — the configuration in which a salient environmental feature (a focal object, wall, display, screen, hearth, central table feature) is paired with seating, postural, or circulation orientations that converge on that feature, jointly fixing a stable shared focus for the gathering. The construct is binary at first approximation: either the room commits to a single, materially-marked anchor that the seating arrangement actively orients toward, or it does not. The triadic specification is constitutive, not optional. Following Tomasello (1995, 2008), joint attention requires the simultaneous coordination of self, other, and a shared object of attention; the spatial-registry analog therefore requires both (i) a perceptually dominant anchor and (ii) a seating or body-axis arrangement that orients the participants' gaze and posture toward it. Following Kendon (1990, ch. 7), the anchor functions as the shared *focus* of the o-space — the inner transactional region of an F-formation — and a focal feature without an o-space oriented toward it is not a shared attention anchor but merely a salient object. Following Goffman (1963, ch. 6), a focused gathering is constituted by the participants' coordinated involvement in a single cognitive and visual concern; the anchor is the material correlate of that concern. L53 thus differs from L52 (one-to-many presentation) in that L52 specifies the *participation framework* (asymmetric speaker/audience) whereas L53 specifies whether a *triadic anchor* exists — a hearth-and-circle, a central conference table feature, or a museum display with a viewing bench all qualify for L53 without being one-to-many presentations.
semantics.definition_short: Triadic anchor: salient focal object plus seating oriented toward it, fixing shared focus.
examples_negative: A blank wall in a hotel lobby with a single piece of large abstract art mounted on it, but the lobby seating is arranged around small coffee tables with seats facing each other rather than the wall — the focal object is present but no triadic anchor exists. (Per the panel: focal object without seating oriented toward it is mere object presence, not a shared attention anchor.)

A fast-food restaurant with a wall-mounted television playing news, but tables and chairs are arranged for paired or quartet conversation with seats facing across the table; the television is salient but the seating refuses it as an anchor.

A hotel lobby with multiple competing focal candidates — a reception desk, a grand staircase, a video wall, a fountain — and dispersed seating clusters that orient toward different candidates or toward each other; no single shared anchor is established.
An airport gate area with rows of perimeter seating facing a central empty floor; there is no central feature and the seating axis converges on nothing.

A bare meeting room with chairs arranged in a circle around an empty conversation centre; the o-space exists (sociopetal seating) but there is no materially-marked anchor occupying it — this is L48/L49 territory, not L53.
examples_positive: A living room with a fireplace and a U-shaped sofa-and-armchair arrangement whose seating axes all converge on the hearth — the fireplace is the materially-marked anchor and the seating commits the room to it.
A museum gallery with a single sculpture on a central plinth and a curved bench arc placed at the canonical viewing distance facing the plinth.

A boardroom with an oblong conference table whose centre carries a fixed visual feature (a built-in display, an architectural model, a centrepiece) and chairs arrayed around the table with their long axes oriented toward that centre.
A campfire-style outdoor gathering: a fire ring at the centre, log benches arranged in a ring, every body axis oriented inward and downward toward the flames.

A waiting area in a children's hospital with a fish tank along one wall and a row of seats positioned and angled to face the tank, such that the tank functions as a shared point of regard for waiting families.
A classroom corner reading nook organised around a low display shelf of picture books, with floor cushions arrayed in a semicircle facing the shelf.

A traditional Japanese tea room with a *tokonoma* alcove containing a hanging scroll and arranged flowers; the seating positions for guests are fixed by convention to face the alcove, making it the canonical shared focus.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is sufficient when upstream observables jointly identify (a) a single dominant anchor candidate AND (b) seating whose orientation axes converge on that candidate. The extractor should consume upstream tags `affordance.focal_object_present` (binary on whether a perceptually dominant non-seating feature exists — fireplace, sculpture, screen, central table feature, display wall), `affordance.focal_object_saliency_score` (a continuous saliency or visual-prominence score for the top-ranked candidate, used to discriminate single-anchor from competing-anchor scenes), `affordance.focal_object_polygon` (region support for the anchor itself), `furniture.chair_orientation_field` (per-chair yaw vector), `furniture.seating_centroid` (the geometric centre of the seated region), and `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` (the angle between the seating-orientation principal direction and the anchor-to-centroid vector). The binary positive judgement requires (i) `affordance.focal_object_present == true`, (ii) `affordance.focal_object_saliency_score` above a single-anchor discrimination threshold (i.e. one anchor visually dominates rather than competing with peers), AND (iii) >= 60% of detected seating yaw vectors falling within +/-35 degrees of the anchor-direction vector from the seating centroid. The orientation-convergence criterion is the operationalisation of the panel-mandated triadic-structure requirement: a focal wall *with* seats facing it constitutes a triad; a focal wall *with seats facing each other* does not, and must be coded negative even when the anchor candidate is visually unambiguous. Because L53 co-occurs with L52 in most presentation contexts, the extractor should NOT short-circuit on L52-positive scenes; instead it should re-verify the anchor-orientation criterion independently, since some L52-positive scenes (a lectern in an empty room, a podium with no backdrop) lack the L53 anchor object even when the participation framework is committed. Cross-cultural variance is medium: the anchor-and-orientation pattern recurs widely (hearths, altars, *tokonoma* alcoves, display niches all exemplify it), but cultures vary in *what kinds of objects* qualify as canonical anchors and in *how strongly* the seating commitment is materially marked versus conventionally enforced. Extractors trained on Western interiors may underweight non-Western anchor types (alcoves, low altars, ground-level focal arrangements) and should be audited cross-culturally before deployment.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans the same upstream observables are available with stronger geometric grounding. Anchor-candidate detection benefits from full 3D saliency: a fireplace, sculpture, or display zone that is partially occluded in a 2D image is fully recoverable from mesh geometry. Per-chair yaw vectors are recoverable from chair-back normal vectors rather than monocular pose estimation, and the orientation-convergence criterion can be evaluated by testing whether the union of chair viewing frusta intersects coherently within the bounding region of the anchor polygon. The 3D extractor should compute (i) the saliency-ranked anchor candidate set within the visible mesh (using a combination of geometric prominence — surface-area exposure, central placement — and material-marker cues such as luminance or material discontinuity), (ii) per-chair viewing direction from chair-back normals, (iii) the intersection point or region of densest intersection of those view rays, and (iv) whether that intersection region overlaps the top-ranked anchor candidate's bounding region. A 3D positive instance requires the orientation-intersection region to fall within (or immediately adjacent to) the anchor-candidate region. Monocular-3D-approximation extraction is also viable (Tier 1 in V2.6) using monocular depth estimation plus a planar-floor assumption; the binary judgement is robust to depth noise because it depends on the *direction* of seating-axis convergence relative to the anchor rather than absolute distances. Region support is true: positive instances should return both the anchor polygon and the seated-region polygon, allowing downstream consumers to verify the triad geometry. The 3D extractor should also emit (alongside the binary value) the diagnostic triple `(anchor_polygon, seating_centroid, mean_axis_misalignment_degrees)` so that borderline cases can be inspected in Sprint 2 calibration.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Shared Focus Anchor': rating items for (a) presence of a perceptually dominant focal object/wall/display, (b) presence of a central table feature, (c) presence of a designated display zone, (d) seating orientation convergence on the candidate anchor. The fourth indicator — orientation convergence — is the operationalisation of the triadic-structure requirement. Behavioural proxies (people actually looking at the anchor) belong in extractor docstrings, not the registry.
scope_excludes: Focal object without seating oriented toward it. Mere object presence — a striking wall, a large artwork, a display screen — does not constitute a shared attention anchor unless the seating arrangement actively orients toward it. (Panel-mandated exclusion: per Kendon, the V2.6 cue 'dominant focal object/wall' identifies a candidate anchor but not the triadic structure that joint attention requires. A focal wall with seats facing it constitutes a triad; a focal wall with seats facing each other does not.)

Sociopetal seating without a materially-marked anchor. A circle of chairs around an empty centre is an F-formation o-space (L48 / L49 / L51) but lacks the object pole of the joint-attention triad and is therefore not L53.

Multi-anchor or competing-anchor configurations. Rooms with several plausible focal candidates and dispersed orientations do not commit to a single shared focus and should be coded negative on L53 even if individual sub-clusters within the room would qualify on their own.

Pure platform-format configurations where the anchor is the speaker rather than an environmental feature. L52 captures the speaker-and-audience commitment as a *participation framework*; L53 captures the *environmental object* pole of the triad. A bare lectern with no displayed content or backdrop, in a room committed to one-to-many seating, satisfies L52 without satisfying L53. (In practice, most lecture halls satisfy both — the screen, board, or framed front region acts as the L53 anchor while the speaker animates the L52 framework.)

Transient or contingent foci (a person being looked at, a momentary event) that depend on participant behaviour rather than the room's spatial commitments. The registry codes affordances for joint attention, not behavioural episodes of joint attention.
scope_includes: Domestic hearth-and-seating arrangements (fireplaces, central low tables, family altars) where the anchor is a permanent architectural or furniture feature and the surrounding seating commits the room to it.

Display-zone configurations (museum benches before sculpture, gallery viewing seats before paintings, observatory seating before a window or telescope) where the anchor is curatorially designated and seating is calibrated to it.

One-to-many presentation rooms (lecture halls, classrooms) where L52 *and* L53 both apply: the front region is also a shared attention anchor, and the audience seating orientation simultaneously satisfies both constructs. (L53 is not exclusive of L52; the constructs co-occur frequently. See `related_tags`.)

Central-table configurations whose table-top itself carries a focal feature (architectural model, game board, candelabrum, food platter at a banquet) that anchors the gaze of the seated participants inward and downward.

social.small_group_conversation.definition: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.
social.small_group_conversation.definition_short: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + chair-instance segmentation + per-chair orientation + chair-cluster grouping + round-table detection + free-floor pocket estimation + monocular distance estimate + ambient-acoustic-context proxy

key_refs: Kendon, A. (1976). The F-formation system: The spatial organization of social encounters. *Man-Environment Systems*, *6*(6), 291–296. (Foundational F-formation article; pre-DOI era.)

Kendon, A. (1990). *Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters*. Cambridge University Press. (Definitive treatment of p-space and o-space; ch. 7 on F-formation arrangements; pp. 209–214 on the analytic priority of the o-space.)

Hall, E. T. (1966). *The hidden dimension*. Doubleday. (Personal/social distance bands; ch. 10 on cross-cultural variance; the metric basis for distinguishing L49 conversational distance from L51 seminar distance.)

Steiner, I. D. (1972). *Group process and productivity*. Academic Press. (Small-group size effects; the 3–6 band as the size at which a single sustained conversation remains tractable without sub-grouping; ch. 4.)

Setti, F., Hung, H., & Cristani, M. (2013). Group detection in still images by F-formation modeling: A comparative study. In *Proceedings of the 14th International Workshop on Image Analysis for Multimedia Interactive Services (WIAMIS)* (pp. 1–4). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/WIAMIS.2013.6616147

notes.bn_notes: Not required for L49 (Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding flag applies only to L46, L52, L53). However: L49's secondary-Likert affordance-strength score depends on three multiplicatively-related sub-judgments (size-fit, free-floor adequacy, acoustic tractability), and the BN's CPT for L49 may be more parsimoniously expressed as a noisy-AND of three intermediate latent nodes than as a single 5-state CPT over the full parent set. This is a Sprint-2 BN-modelling refinement to consider when L49 is integrated into the network.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied — L49 remains Tier 1 as in V2.6. Three extractor-contract requirements deserve special note for the Phase E stub author: (a) the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` is mandatory output (Kendon panel mandate, panel disposition §3 ix); (b) the Hall distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is mandatory output (Hall panel mandate, panel disposition §3 viii) — and for L49 this is doubly load-bearing because without metric the construct collapses into L51 per the panel's special note for this latent; (c) the acoustic-tractability proxy (the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue) should be emitted as a separate boolean alongside the secondary Likert score so that downstream consumers can re-weight or veto on acoustic grounds. All three are runtime extractor-output specifications, not registry-level fields, and so are recorded here rather than in `extractability`.

semantics.definition_long: The property of a local spatial pocket whose furniture clustering, free-floor geometry, and ambient conditions afford the formation and maintenance of a focused conversational gathering of roughly three to six co-present persons — Steiner's (1972) small-group band, in which mutual perceptual access remains tractable without sub-grouping. The construct refers to a *cluster-scale* F-formation arrangement in Kendon's (1990, ch. 7) sense: a shared o-space large enough to seat or stand-circle three to six people at conversational distance, enclosed by clusterable furniture (movable chairs, round tables of suitable diameter, an open floor pocket), and acoustically tractable enough that ordinary speech across the o-space remains intelligible. L49 is distinct from L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), which is a 2-person F-formation construct, and from L51 (Group-Assembly Affordance), which addresses 7+ gatherings whose geometry must distribute people into sub-groups; the 3–6 band is the size at which one o-space still suffices and one conversation can still be sustained (Hall, 1966, ch. 10; Steiner, 1972, ch. 4).
semantics.definition_short: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.
examples_negative: A long rectangular boardroom table seating twelve, with conference chairs along both sides — the geometry is sized for a single 7+ gathering with formal facing-across, not for a 3–6 person F-formation; this is a presentation/large-group configuration (L51 / L52), not L49.
A pair of armchairs angled inward across a small coffee table in an alcove — the cluster is sized only for two; this is L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), not L49.

A row of single fixed seats along a corridor wall, all facing the same direction — there is no clusterable floor pocket and no convergent o-space; this is at most a peripheral-engagement / waiting-area arrangement, not L49.
A theatre-style seating block with five chairs in a single row, all facing forward — the seat count is right but the geometry is parallel-rank with no o-space; this is L52 (presentation), not L49.

A loud open-plan office break area where four chairs sit around a small table but the ambient noise from adjacent desks and equipment makes ordinary conversation across the table impossible — the geometry says L49 but the acoustic-tractability criterion fails; classify as `none` with a low secondary affordance score and an acoustic-context note.

A bar with seven movable stools spaced along a straight counter-front; the seats are clusterable in count, but the linear-rank geometry and the wide spacing do not enclose an o-space for 3–6 people, so the construct does not apply.
examples_positive: A round café table about 110 cm in diameter ringed by four movable chairs in a coffee-shop alcove; chair backs face outward, chair fronts converge on the table, and the surrounding open floor is wide enough for a fifth person to pull a chair up.

A clearing in a hotel-lobby furniture plan with a small low coffee table and five lounge chairs roughly evenly spaced around it at conversational distance, with no other furniture obstructing the cluster.

A standing huddle pocket in an exhibition hall where a high cocktail table is surrounded by an open floor disk roughly 2.5 m across, allowing four to six adults to stand-circle the table without bumping passers-by.

A meeting-room "informal corner" with three movable tub chairs and a sofa seat for one more, all converging on a small round table; the corner is set off from the main meeting table and supports a 3–4 person side-discussion.

A library group-study nook with a round table seating up to six, movable chairs, glass walls for acoustic separation, and an open pocket of floor that lets group members swap seats without disturbing the cluster.
A picnic ground arrangement of one round picnic table seating six, with the surrounding lawn open enough that group members can stand and lean during conversation while remaining in the same o-space.
A workplace "team huddle" zone with a round high-top table for four to six standing people, surrounded by clear floor and acoustically buffered from the open-plan desks beyond.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observable tags — most centrally `furniture.chair_cluster_count` (number of chairs spatially grouped within an inter-chair distance threshold), `furniture.round_table_present` (round-table detection within the cluster), `furniture.chair_orientation` (per-chair front-normal in image plane), `geometry.free_floor_pocket_radius` (distance from cluster centroid to the nearest occlusion or through-traffic boundary), `geometry.cluster_distance_estimate` (Hall-zone distance estimate between cluster members; mandated by Hall-panel disposition §3 viii because without metric, L49 collapses into L51 — a circle of chairs at social-close distance (4–7 ft / 1.2–2.1 m centres) supports L49 conversation, while the same chairs at social-far (7–12 ft / 2.1–3.7 m) supports a seminar (L51 / L52)), and `scene.ambient_noise_proxy` (a visual proxy for the acoustic context: presence of acoustic baffling, distance from open kitchen / equipment, glass-wall enclosure, etc.) for the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue. The extractor MUST emit, alongside the categorical and secondary-Likert values, the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` per Kendon-panel mandate (panel disposition §3 ix; Kendon, 1990, pp. 209–214). Cluster-size gating: the categorical value defaults to `none` when the chair-cluster-count is <3 (cede to L48) or >6 (cede to L51); within the 3–6 band, the `predominant_arrangement` is computed from the same angular-threshold logic as L44 (vis-à-vis at ~180° front-normal, L at ~90°, side-by-side at ~0° with shared focal artefact, semicircular at chair-back arc <180° around the centroid, circular at arc ≥270°). **Cross-cultural-variance warning (Hall, 1966, ch. 10)**: this extractor is calibrated against Western indoor-furniture corpora; it will systematically misread (a) Japanese tatami-room small-group arrangements where seating is around a low shared table at distances Western thresholds may flag as too close, (b) Mediterranean / Latin-American small-group conversational distances which the Hall-zone gate may push down a Likert step, and (c) Middle-Eastern majlis-style perimeter seating where the ring is technically circular but at distances exceeding the o-space radius constraint. Downstream model trainers MUST treat L49 outputs as culturally indexed and the secondary Likert as conditionally interpretable on the cultural prior of the scene.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction is materially easier: chair instances and orientations are metric, the floor plane is given, and the free-floor pocket can be computed as the largest empty disk centred on the cluster centroid that contains no other furniture or wall surface up to 0.9 m height. The extractor should take the 3D scene graph, identify chair clusters whose pairwise floor-plane distance falls within the Hall personal-far / social-close band (1.2–2.5 m centre-to-centre — the metric that distinguishes L49 from L51 per Hall, 1966, ch. 10, and per the panel's special note that without distance estimation L49 collapses into L51), gate on cluster size 3 ≤ n ≤ 6, project chair-front normals onto the floor plane, and compute the o-space centroid as the smallest enclosed region whose boundary contains all chair-front rays within their first 2.5 m. The arrangement-enum classification applies the same angular thresholds as in 2D but with metric distance gating: a circle of six chairs at 1.4 m centres is `circular` with secondary score 4; the same chairs at 3 m centres steps down to secondary 2 (and at 4 m centres steps to `none` because the cluster has crossed into seminar-scale L51 territory). Acoustic context is more directly estimable in 3D from room volume, surface materials, and distance to noise sources; the secondary score should integrate a simple acoustic-tractability boolean from these. As with L44 and L48, occupancy state is not part of L49 — the construct is an *affordance* of the geometry alone, with avatar occupancy contributing only to a separate confidence-of-realisation field. The `requires_floor_plan: false` setting holds even in 3D: L49 is a local cluster-pocket property, not a configurational property of the room as a whole.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CONV-3 (Small-Group Conversation Affordance): rating items "the space supports a small group talking", "movable chairs around a round table are present", "the floor pocket is open enough for 3–6 people to gather"; behavioral proxy "if 3–6 people stood/sat here, they could carry on one conversation".

Loads on the V2.6 *interactional-affordance* superfactor alongside L44 (Sociopetal Seating Topology), L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), and L51 (Group-Assembly Affordance), with L49 occupying the 3–6 person mid-band specifically.
scope_excludes: Dyadic clusters (two seats only, however well-arranged) — these belong to L48 (Dyadic Intimacy).
Large-group arrangements sized for 7+ persons whose geometry forces sub-grouping into multiple simultaneous conversations — these belong to L51 (Group Assembly Affordance).

Single-cluster geometry that meets the 3–6 size criterion but sits in an environment where the ambient noise floor exceeds normal-speech intelligibility across the o-space (the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue) — record as `none` with a noted acoustic-tractability failure rather than a positive `circular`/`semicircular` value.
One-to-many presentation arrangements where 3–6 chairs all face one focal speaker (lectern, screen, performer) rather than converging on a shared inner o-space — these are L52 (presentation).

Sociofugal back-to-back or radial-out clusters whose seats are within range numerically (3–6) but whose front-normals diverge — Osmond's (1957) sociofugal pole, the inverse of the construct, recorded as `none` with secondary score 0.
scope_includes: Multi-element clusters (chairs, round table, standing-table, soft seating mix) whose combined enclosed o-space is sized to seat or stand-circle 3 to 6 persons at conversational distance (Hall's (1966) personal-far to social-close band, roughly 1.2–2.5 m centre-to-centre).

Standing-only huddle arrangements (no seats, only a high table or nothing at all) provided the surrounding floor pocket is open, free of through-traffic, and acoustically buffered enough for a stand-circle to form.
Mixed-furniture clusters (e.g., one sofa segment plus three pull-up chairs) where the combined seat count falls in the 3–6 band and the chair fronts converge on a shared table or floor centroid.

Round-table arrangements at the upper boundary (six seats) where a single shared conversation can plausibly be sustained without splitting into sub-groups; the upper edge is fuzzy and depends on table diameter and local acoustics.

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Sociopetal.
Sociopetal is not visibly present.
No clear evidence of Sociopetal is present.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Sociopetal.
Sociopetal is a dominant visible attribute.
Clear evidence of Sociopetal is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Sociopetal; avoid inference. From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues for Sociopetal.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Sociopetal; avoid speculation. From 3D or scene data, use geometry/material cues for Sociopetal.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Sociopetal.
Inferred Sociopetal without visual evidence.
Inferred Sociopetal without evidence.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Sociopetal.
Dominant visible instance of Sociopetal.
Multiple consistent cues indicate Sociopetal.

social.sociopetal_seating.definition: Seating geometry whose angular convergence affords sustained face-to-face talk (F-formations).

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + chair-instance segmentation + per-chair orientation estimate + pairwise chair-front-normal angular geometry + optional intervening-artefact (table/hearth) detection

key_refs: Osmond, H. (1957). Function as the basis of psychiatric ward design. Mental Hospitals, 8(4), 23–29.
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.
Kendon, A. (1976). The F-formation system: The spatial organization of social encounters. Man-Environment Systems, 6(6), 291–296.
Kendon, A. (1990). Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters. Cambridge University Press.

Setti, F., Hung, H., & Cristani, M. (2013). Group detection in still images by F-formation modeling: A comparative study. In Proceedings of the 14th International Workshop on Image Analysis for Multimedia Interactive Services (WIAMIS) (pp. 1–4). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/WIAMIS.2013.6616147

notes.bn_notes: Not required for L44 (Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding flag applies only to L46, L52, L53). However: the Kendon-panel ontological argument that L52 is itself an F-formation variant (panel disposition §3 xii, deferred) suggests that if Sprint 2 re-opens that question, the L44 categorical enum may need to grow a `parallel_rank` value to absorb L52, or alternatively L52 may be re-keyed as a special case of L44 with the `none` enum value flagged with a presentation-arrangement subtype. This is logged as a Sprint-2 construct-contest item for the student handoff.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied — L44 remains Tier 1 as in V2.6. Two extractor-contract requirements deserve special note for the Phase E stub author: (a) the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` is mandatory output (Kendon panel mandate, panel disposition §3 ix); (b) the Hall distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is mandatory output (Hall panel mandate, panel disposition §3 viii).
