Both are runtime extractor-output specifications, not registry-level fields, and so are recorded here rather than in `extractability`.

semantics.definition_long: The spatial-organisational property of a multi-seat arrangement whose chair positions and orientations enclose a shared inner void — Kendon's *o-space* — such that two or more co-present persons, were they to occupy the seats, would form a transactional segment supporting sustained mutual orientation, gaze, and talk (Kendon, 1990, ch. 7). The construct is Osmond's (1957) "sociopetal" pole — arrangements that *bring people together* for conversation — operationalised through the F-formation tradition's distinction between p-space (the slot the bodies and chairs occupy) and o-space (the shared inner region around which the bodies are oriented). It is a property of *arrangements of multiple seats*, not of single chairs; a single chair, however oriented, cannot constitute a sociopetal topology because no o-space is enclosed.
semantics.definition_short: Seating geometry whose angular convergence affords sustained face-to-face talk (F-formations).
examples_negative: A row of theatre seats all facing forward toward a stage — each seat is occupied for focused attention, but the seats face *parallel*, not convergent, so no inter-seat o-space is enclosed; this is a one-to-many presentation arrangement (L52), not sociopetal.

A single armchair angled toward an open doorway in a hallway — looks "inviting" because of orientation, but no second seat is present to enclose an o-space; this is at most an approach-invitation cue (L43), not a sociopetal topology.
Four airport-terminal chairs welded back-to-back in a cross pattern with all chair-fronts pointing radially *outward* — Osmond's classic sociofugal configuration; the inverse construct.
A long boardroom table with chairs only along one side, all facing a wall-mounted screen — chairs face a common focus but do not face *each other*, so no F-formation o-space is enclosed.

A pair of chairs side-by-side on a porch, both facing the same garden view — Hall (1966) notes that side-by-side parallel seating affords companionship but not the mutual gaze of vis-à-vis sociopetal seating; classification depends on whether a shared o-space (e.g., a small table between them) is enclosed (see scope_includes 3).
A single sofa with three cushions, all facing the same TV — the cushions are clustered but their fronts are coplanar; no convergence, no o-space.
examples_positive: A pair of armchairs angled inward at roughly 90° across a small coffee table in a hotel lobby; the chair fronts converge on a shared point above the table surface, enclosing a clear o-space.
Four café chairs around a small round table, each chair-front normal pointing approximately at the table centre; the inward-facing seat ring defines a circular F-formation.
A semicircular cluster of low lounge chairs around a fireplace in a reading room, all chair backs on the convex side and chair fronts converging on the hearth-as-shared-focus.

A pair of bench seats in a railway compartment facing each other across a fold-down table, the backs flush with opposite walls and the o-space the table-top void between them — a vis-à-vis arrangement.
Three chairs in an L-arrangement at the corner of a sectional sofa, two side-by-side and one perpendicular, all three chair-fronts converging on a coffee-table o-space.
A circle of chairs in a therapy-group room with no central table, the o-space defined purely by the convergence of chair-front normals on the empty floor centre.
A pair of bar stools turned inward toward each other across the corner of an L-shaped bar counter, fronts converging at the corner.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observable tags — most centrally `furniture.chair_instances` (per-chair bounding box and instance mask), `furniture.chair_orientation` (per-chair front-normal vector in image plane, or its monocular 3D lift), `furniture.table_instances` and `furniture.hearth_instances` (intervening-artefact candidates), and `scene.floor_plane_normal` (for projecting chair fronts onto the floor plane to get planar angular geometry). The Setti et al. (2013) detection pipeline is the closest published precedent: detect upper-body or chair-front orientations, then cluster orientation lines by their intersection points to identify candidate F-formation o-spaces. For L44 specifically, the extractor MUST emit, alongside the categorical and secondary-Likert values, the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` per Kendon-panel mandate (Kendon, 1990, pp. 209–214); the V2.6 cue list "seats facing each other; circular/semicircular arrangements; clustered chairs" describes only the p-space furniture and elides the o-space, which is the analytically-load-bearing region. Predominant-arrangement classification proceeds: 2 chairs at front-normal angle ~180° → `vis_a_vis`; 2 chairs at ~90° → `l_arrangement`; 2+ chairs at ~0° (parallel) with shared focal artefact → `side_by_side`; 3+ chairs whose front-normals converge on a common centroid with chair-back arc spanning <180° of the centroid → `semicircular`; same with arc spanning ≥270° → `circular`; otherwise → `none`. Distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is required as a separate extractor-output field (panel disposition §3 viii). **Cross-cultural-variance warning (Hall, 1966, ch. 10)**: this extractor is calibrated against Western indoor-furniture corpora; it will systematically misread (a) Japanese tea-room and tatami arrangements where side-by-side around a shared focus substitutes for face-to-face, (b) Mediterranean/Latin-American close-angle conversational seating which the angular threshold may classify as `none` because the chairs are too close to resolve a stable o-space, and (c) Middle-Eastern majlis perimeter seating which is technically circular but at distances that exceed the o-space radius constraint. Downstream model trainers MUST treat L44 outputs as culturally indexed and the secondary Likert as conditionally interpretable on the cultural prior of the depicted scene.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction is materially easier: chair instances and orientations are already metric, the floor plane is given, and the F-formation o-space is computable as the smallest enclosed region whose boundary contains all chair-front rays within their first 2.5 m. The extractor should take the 3D scene graph, project chair-front normals onto the floor plane, compute pairwise ray intersections (or Voronoi-style closest-approach centroids for parallel-front cases), cluster the intersections by spatial proximity to identify candidate o-space centroids, and emit the largest cluster's centroid as `o_space_centroid_xy` and its enclosing-circle radius as `o_space_radius_m` (note: metric in 3D, pixels in 2D). The arrangement-enum classification applies the same angular thresholds as in 2D but with metric distance gating: vis-à-vis at >3 m intra-chair separation drops the secondary affordance score by 1 step per metre. In VR, occupancy state (whether the avatars are actually present) is observable, but L44 is an *affordance* construct and so should be computed from the seating geometry alone, with avatar occupancy contributing only to a separate confidence-of-realisation field. The `requires_floor_plan: false` setting holds even in 3D: L44 is a local seat-cluster property, not a configurational property of the room.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CONV-1 (Conversation Affordance): rating items "seats face each other", "chairs are clustered", "the arrangement invites conversation"; behavioral proxy "if people sat here, they would talk to each other".

Loads on the broader V2.6 *interactional-affordance* superfactor alongside L43 (Approach Invitation) and L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), with L44 providing the multi-chair angular-convergence component distinct from L43's single-chair approach cue and L48's distance/intimacy cue.
scope_excludes: Single-seat scenes regardless of orientation; sociopetal topology is constitutively a *multi-seat* property.

Parallel-rank seating arrangements (theatre, lecture-hall, transit-bench rows) where all chair-fronts are coplanar and converge only at infinity; these are L52 (presentation) configurations, not sociopetal.

Sociofugal arrangements (airport terminal back-to-back, library carrel rows facing walls) — these are the *inverse* construct and should receive the `none` enum value with secondary score 0, not a low sociopetal score.
Standing-only F-formations without seats; these are within the F-formation tradition but outside the *seating* scope of L44 (and largely covered by L56 mingling affordance).
Workstation/cubicle seating where chairs face screens or work surfaces independently; the convergence is on individual tasks, not on a shared o-space.
scope_includes: Multi-chair arrangements where chair-front normals converge on a shared point or compact region within roughly 3 metres of all participating seats (the F-formation o-space radius constraint).

Arrangements where the o-space is enclosed by the *combination* of seats and a shared focal artefact (table, hearth, screen) rather than by the seats alone, provided at least two seats face inward toward that artefact at angles less than 180° apart.

Side-by-side parallel seating with a small intervening table or shared focal object that re-establishes a partial o-space; classified as the `side_by_side` enum value with a moderate (not maximal) secondary affordance score, per Hall's (1966, ch. 10) observation that this arrangement is the dominant Japanese sociopetal form.

extraction.method_family: FD / DFA on amplitude envelope time series

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Amplitude Fractal Dimension in the scene or data. This tag should only be used when measurement, sensing, or metadata supports Amplitude Fractal Dimension, not from visual inference. For 2D/3D imagery alone, do not assign without corroborating data.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Amplitude Fractal Dimension in the scene or data. This tag should only be used when measurement, sensing, or metadata supports Amplitude Fractal Dimension, not from visual inference. For 2D/3D imagery alone, do not assign without corroborating data.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Amplitude Fractal Dimension in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
Amplitude Fractal Dimension is not visibly present.
No measurement indicates Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
examples_positive: Scene clearly shows Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
Amplitude Fractal Dimension is a dominant visible attribute.
Measured data indicates Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: From 2D imagery, rely on clear visual cues of Amplitude Fractal Dimension; avoid inference. Not inferable from 2D imagery.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: From 3D or scene data, use geometry, material, or lighting cues for Amplitude Fractal Dimension; avoid speculation. 3D geometry is insufficient without measurements.
scope_excludes: Ambiguous or incidental hints of Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
Inferred Amplitude Fractal Dimension without visual evidence.
Visual inference of Amplitude Fractal Dimension without sensor data.
scope_includes: Clear, primary presence of Amplitude Fractal Dimension.
Dominant visible instance of Amplitude Fractal Dimension.

semantics.definition: Measured tactile property: Haptic Fractal Dimension as reported by material or haptic data rather than visual appearance.
